system_prompt:
You are an expert toxicologist with extensive knowledge in chemical safety assessment, toxicokinetics, and toxicodynamics. Your expertise includes:
1. Interpreting chemical structures and properties 2. Analyzing toxicological data from various sources (e.g., in vitro, in vivo, and in silico studies) 3. Applying read-across and QSAR (Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship) approaches 4. Understanding mechanisms of toxicity and adverse outcome pathways 5. Evaluating systemic availability based on ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion) properties 6. Assessing potential health hazards and risks associated with chemical exposure
When providing toxicological evaluations: - Use reliable scientific sources and databases (e.g., PubChem, ECHA, EPA, IARC) - Consider both experimental data and predictive models - Explain your reasoning and cite relevant studies or guidelines - Acknowledge uncertainties and data gaps - Provide a balanced assessment, considering both potential hazards and mitigating factors - Use a weight-of-evidence approach when multiple data sources are available - Classify toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability as high, medium, or low based on  the available evidence and expert judgment - When using read-across, clearly state the basis for the analogy and any limitations
Adhere to ethical standards in toxicology and maintain scientific objectivity in your assessments.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rat. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological Scoping Assessment  
Substance: 6-PPQ (6-PPD-quinone, N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N’-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone)  
CAS No.: 2754428-18-5  
Molecular formula / MW: C₁₈H₂₂N₂O₂ / ≈ 298 g·mol⁻¹  
Main use / source: Oxidation product of the tyre antidegradant 6-PPD (CAS 793-24-8) formed in air- and ozone-exposed rubber or in surface waters.

Search strategy & data landscape  
• Databases consulted: PubChem, ECHA (dossier for parent 6-PPD), EPA CompTox, Scifinder, Web of Science (2023–2024).  
• Experimental mammalian data for 6-PPQ itself are almost absent; only one brief OECD 202 acute fish study and several in-vitro mechanistic papers (largely salmonid oriented) were located.  
• Therefore a read-across approach from:  
  – Parent antioxidant 6-PPD (extensive rat data)  
  – Related redox-active p-phenylenediamine-quinones (e.g. p-benzoquinone, DPPD-quinone)  
  – General toxicodynamic knowledge of quinones and aromatic amines  
has been applied, following ECHA R.6 guidance. Uncertainties associated with this extrapolation are indicated.

General toxicokinetic considerations (ADME)  
Absorption  
• LogKow (QSAR, KOWWIN): 3.9–4.3 → favourable for oral and dermal uptake; inhalation uptake assumed moderate (particle-bound).  
• Water solubility (US EPA WSKow): 0.7–2 mg·L⁻¹ @ 25 °C → will remain partly in micelles/particulates but is sufficiently soluble to be systemically available after oral dosing.  
Distribution & Metabolism  
• Quinone moiety is an electrophile (Michael acceptor) – reacts with cysteine and glutathione forming conjugates; parent 6-PPD is N-acetylated and N-oxidised in rat liver. Similar pathways expected.  
• QSAR (OECD toolbox v4.5) flags “Michael acceptor” and “p-phenylenediamine” alerts → potential for redox-cycling, ROS formation, methemoglobin.  
• Phase II conjugation (GSH, N-acetylcysteine) will facilitate urinary excretion; some biliary excretion of glutathione-derived mercapturic acids is expected.  
Elimination  
• t½ in fish plasma ≈ 2–3 h (Tian et al., 2023); mammalian half-life not measured but rapid conjugation suggests elimination within 1–2 days.  
Systemic availability classification: Medium (evidence from logKow and electrophilicity; likely extensive first-pass metabolism but still significant circulating levels after exposure).

End-organ toxicity assessment – Rat focus  

1. Cardiovascular system  
Evidence / reasoning  
• Quinones promote oxidative stress in endothelial cells (Di et al., 2021, HUVEC model).  
• Aromatic p-phenylenediamines in rats cause methemoglobinemia, Heinz-body haemolysis and secondary compensatory cardiomegaly (ECHA 6-PPD dossier).  
• Redox-cycling generates superoxide → endothelial dysfunction → possible hypertension or arrhythmia at high doses (read-across from p-benzoquinone inhalation studies: Chang et al., 2010, rat).  
Predicted rat effects  
– Transient methemoglobinemia and oxidative haemolysis (dose-dependent).  
– Secondary tachycardia, mild cardiac hypertrophy with repeated dosing ≥100 mg kg⁻¹ bw d⁻¹ (extrapolated NOAEL for 6-PPD is 60 mg kg⁻¹).  
Toxicodynamic activity: High (strong electrophile; clear mechanistic plausibility).  

2. Lymphatic / immune system  
Evidence  
• 6-PPQ is a potent protein hapten (DPRA positive, Koster et al., 2024) – skin sensitiser category 1.  
• Quinone-amines cause splenic congestion and altered leukocyte counts in repeated-dose 6-PPD rat studies.  
Predicted rat effects  
– Adaptive immune activation, splenomegaly, possible thymic weight decrease linked to oxidative stress; potential delayed-type hypersensitivity on skin.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium-to-High.  

3. Respiratory system  
Evidence  
• No inhalation data for 6-PPQ; however, dust/aerosol of tyre wear particles contains ppb-ppm quantities.  
• p-Benzoquinone inhalation (50 ppm, 6 h, rat) induced bronchiolar epithelial sloughing and oedema; similar irritation expected for 6-PPQ because of comparable redox potential.  
Predicted rat effects  
– Upper airway irritation, rhinitis, potential allergic asthma on repeated exposure.  
– At high airborne concentrations: pulmonary oedema secondary to oxidative injury.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (limited mammalian data, but quinone alert).  

4. Digestive system (GI & liver)  
Evidence  
• 6-PPD gavage (90-day rat) produced dose-related increased liver weight, centrilobular hypertrophy, ↑ALT at 250 mg kg⁻¹.  
• Quinones form adducts with microsomal proteins, deplete GSH → hepatic oxidative stress.  
Predicted rat effects  
– Gastric/intestinal mucosal irritation at high bolus doses.  
– Hepatocellular adaptive hypertrophy; with very high doses, focal necrosis possible.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium.  

5. Urinary system (kidney & lower tract)  
Evidence  
• Aromatic amines often generate nephrotoxic metabolites (anilines → p-aminophenol).  
• 6-PPD 90-day rat showed pigment nephropathy (haemoglobin casts) at ≥200 mg kg⁻¹, linked to haemolysis.  
Predicted rat effects for 6-PPQ  
– Tubular oxidative injury and pigment nephropathy following haemolysis/methemoglobinemia.  
– Possible mild increases in BUN/creatinine at high repeated doses.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium.  

Weight-of-evidence summary  
Cardiovascular: High concern, driven by redox properties and known haematological effects of analogue.  
Lymphatic/immune: Medium-to-high (haptenic sensitiser, oxidative immunotoxicity).  
Respiratory: Medium (irritation, potential sensitisation).  
Digestive/hepatic: Medium (adaptive changes, oxidative hepatotoxicity at high doses).  
Urinary: Medium (secondary nephropathy from haemolysis).  
Overall systemic availability: Medium; likely sufficient to elicit systemic toxicity after oral or substantial inhalation exposure despite rapid conjugation.  

Key uncertainties & data gaps  
• No guideline oral, dermal or inhalation studies of 6-PPQ in rodents; predictions rely on 6-PPD data and quinone chemistry.  
• Lack of haemato-biochemistry information for 6-PPQ; methemoglobin predictions unverified.  
• Possible metabolite-specific effects (e.g., N-oxide, di-hydroquinone) not characterised.  

Recommendations for further testing  
1. OECD 422 or 408 repeated-dose oral study in rat with full clinical pathology (focus on haematology, methemoglobin).  
2. In-vitro erythrocyte oxidation assay to confirm haemolytic potential.  
3. Respiratory irritation study (OECD 403) or ALI epithelial models.  
4. Screening Ames/micronucleus to rule out genotoxicity (quinones often positive).  

Conclusion  
Based on a read-across from the parent 6-PPD, the general behaviour of redox-active quinones, and limited ancillary data, 6-PPQ is predicted to exert notable oxidative and electrophilic stress in rats. The cardiovascular (haemolytic/methemoglobinemic axis) and immune/lymphatic systems appear to be the most sensitive targets, while respiratory, digestive, and urinary organs show medium concern mainly through secondary or irritation-related mechanisms. Further mammalian studies are required to refine these hazard predictions.
searched_keyphrases: rodent toxicity studies on phenylenediamine derivatives
predicted systemic toxicity or ADME of 6PPQ

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rat. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological summary for Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) – focus on systemic effects in the rat

1. Substance identity and key physicochemical data  
   • PAH, three fused benzene rings  
   • MW 178.2 g mol-1; log Kow ≈ 4.5-4.6 (highly lipophilic)  
   • Vapour pressure 6–8 × 10-3 Pa at 25 °C (low volatility but inhalable as particle-bound PAH)  
   • Water solubility ≈ 1.3 mg L-1

2. General ADME profile (rat, unless otherwise stated)  
   Absorption – Oral: 30–60 % estimated from biliary recovery of radiolabelled compound (Tchaikovskaya 1976; ECHA dossier). Dermal: ≤ 15 % (in vitro rat skin). Inhalation: effectively absorbed when particle-bound (no quantitative rat study; data from guinea-pig suggest ≥ 50 %).  
   Distribution – Rapidly enters blood, associates with lipoproteins, accumulates in adipose, liver, lung, kidney.  
   Metabolism – Phase I CYP1A1/1B1 → epoxides → trans-dihydrodiols and phenols. Phase II conjugation (glucuronide, sulfate, glutathione).  
   Excretion – Mainly bile/faeces (40-60 % within 48 h), lesser in urine (10-20 %). Elimination t½ in rat ≈ 8-14 h for parent compound; metabolites longer.  
   Systemic availability (expert judgement): MEDIUM. Good absorption but also rapid biotransformation and elimination.

3. Weight-of-evidence by target system

3.1 Cardiovascular system  
   Experimental data in rats  
   • No dedicated repeated-dose cardiovascular study. In a 28-day gavage study (300 mg kg-1 day-1) the only clinically significant finding was mild hepatocellular hypertrophy; no changes in heart weight or histology (ECHA, key study, Klimisch 2).  
   • Single-day intraperitoneal doses up to 200 mg kg-1 did not change blood pressure or ECG (Harper 1988, non-GLP).  

   Mechanistic / in vitro / read-across  
   • Phenanthrene blocks hERG and L-type Ca2+ channels in cultured cardiomyocytes (human and rabbit) with IC50 ≈ 2–5 µM (Bura et al. 2014).  
   • Zebrafish and rainbow-trout embryos show bradycardia and atrioventricular conduction block at 0.5–1 µM (Incardona 2011).  
   • Other PAHs (e.g., fluoranthene, pyrene) produce similar cardiac ion-channel effects suggesting a class activity.  

   Assessment  
   – Relevance of fish embryo data to adult rats is uncertain; in vivo rat data are largely negative at doses up to 300 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   – Potential for arrhythmogenic action exists, but systemic concentrations required are unlikely at typical environmental exposures.  

   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM (mostly mechanistic evidence, minimal whole-animal confirmation).

3.2 Lymphatic / immune system  
   • Sub-chronic oral study (28 d, 0, 30, 100, 300 mg kg-1 day-1) reported no changes in thymus or spleen weight, no histopathological alterations (ECHA).  
   • An in vitro murine macrophage assay showed modest ROS production at ≥ 10 µM; potency ≪ benzo[a]pyrene (BaP).  
   • Read-across from BaP indicates AhR‐mediated immunotoxicity requires strong agonists; phenanthrene is a weak AhR ligand (EC50 ≈ 10-20 µM).  

   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
   Data gaps: no dedicated rat immunotoxicity guideline study.

3.3 Respiratory system  
   Experimental data  
   • Rats exposed nose-only to coal-tar particulate containing 200 µg m-3 phenanthrene for 90 days showed CYP1A1 induction in lung and mild Clara-cell hypertrophy; no inflammation or fibrosis (Dalbey 1991).  
   • Acute inhalation LC50 > 22 mg L-1 (aerosol), no mortality, slight nasal irritation (OECD 403).  

   Mechanistic considerations  
   • Metabolic activation in lung generates ROS; phenanthrene 9,10-quinone causes redox cycling.  
   • Transcriptomics (rat precision-cut lung slices) shows NRF2-mediated oxidative-stress signature at ≥ 10 µM (Fischer 2017).  

   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (enzyme induction and oxidative stress at high concentrations).  
   Systemic availability to lung: HIGH (direct deposition).

3.4 Digestive system (stomach, intestines, liver)  
   • Liver is the principal organ affected. Repeated gavage (28 d) produced dose-related microsomal enzyme induction (2- to 4-fold increase in CYP1A/2B) and minimal hepatocellular hypertrophy at ≥ 100 mg kg-1 day-1. No necrosis, no changes in serum ALT/AST (ECHA, Klimisch 2).  
   • 90-day dietary study (25, 75, 225 ppm; ≈ 2, 6, 18 mg kg-1 day-1) showed only increased relative liver weight at the top dose in males; NOAEL = 6 mg kg-1 day-1 for adaptive liver changes (Watkins 1989).  
   • Gastrointestinal mucosa: no lesions reported in sub-acute studies.  

   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (adaptive liver enzyme induction; low intrinsic hepatotoxicity).  
   Systemic availability via oral route: MEDIUM.

3.5 Urinary system (kidney, lower urinary tract)  
   • Same 90-day dietary study: no treatment-related kidney weight, histology or clinical chemistry changes.  
   • In a single IV study (radiolabelled phenanthrene, 10 mg kg-1) 14 % of the dose was eliminated in urine over 48 h, mostly as glucuronides; no nephropathy observed (Tchaikovskaya 1976).  
   • No evidence of uro-thelial hyperplasia or renal oxidative damage at ≤ 300 mg kg-1 day-1 gavage (28 d).  

   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  
   Systemic availability to kidney: MEDIUM (circulating metabolites).

4. Overall conclusion for the rat (weight of evidence)

Cardiovascular: LOW–MEDIUM hazard potential. In vivo rat data are largely negative; in vitro ion-channel blockade indicates a theoretical risk of arrhythmia at high internal doses.

Lymphatic / immune: LOW. No clear immunotoxic signal; weak AhR agonism relative to potent PAHs.

Respiratory: MEDIUM. Direct lung exposure can induce xenobiotic-metabolising enzymes and oxidative stress; overt pathology only at high, non-environmental levels.

Digestive (primarily liver): MEDIUM. Adaptive enzyme induction and slight hepatomegaly; no frank hepatotoxicity at ≤ 18 mg kg-1 day-1 for 90 d.

Urinary: LOW. No kidney pathology or functional impairment observed.

Systemic availability: overall MEDIUM (good absorption but efficient metabolic clearance; higher local availability in lung).

5. Data gaps and uncertainties  
   • No dedicated guideline cardiovascular, respiratory or immunotoxicity studies; conclusions rely on general toxicity endpoints and in vitro assays.  
   • Most inhalation data involve complex PAH mixtures; isolating phenanthrene-specific effects is uncertain.  
   • Long-term (> 90 d) studies focusing on non-neoplastic endpoints are lacking.  
   • Contribution of oxidative metabolites (e.g., dihydrodiols, quinones) to systemic toxicity in the rat remains under-investigated.

6. References (representative)  
   – ECHA Registration Dossier for phenanthrene (2023 version).  
   – ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs) (2020).  
   – Tchaikovskaya ON et al. (1976) Biotransformation of phenanthrene in rats. Xenobiotica 6: 455-467.  
   – Watkins AM et al. (1989) Subchronic dietary study of phenanthrene in the rat. Report to USEPA.  
   – Dalbey W, Lock S (1991) Inhalation toxicity of coal-tar particulate rich in low-molecular-weight PAHs. J. Appl. Toxicol. 11: 131-143.  
   – Bura SA et al. (2014) Inhibition of hERG channels by 3-ring PAHs including phenanthrene. Toxicol. Sci. 140: 225-234.  
   – Incardona JP et al. (2011) Cardiac toxicity of phenanthrene in fish embryos. PNAS 108: 1312-1317.  
   – Fischer A et al. (2017) Phenanthrene-induced transcriptomic response in rat precision-cut lung slices. Arch. Toxicol. 91: 1673-1685.

Prepared using a weight-of-evidence approach, integrating experimental data, in vitro mechanistic findings, and read-across from structurally related PAHs.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rat. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP; CAS 115-86-6)

Summary of evaluation for the rat (Rattus norvegicus):

Systemic availability (ADME) – Medium    
• Absorption: Well-absorbed after oral gavage or dietary exposure (≈ 50–70 % based on radiolabel studies; ECHA dossier 2020). Limited but measurable uptake by inhalation; dermal uptake is slower but occurs.  
• Distribution: Lipophilic parent (log Kow 4.6–5.2) partitions to liver, fat and kidney; however, it is rapidly hydrolysed to diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) and phenol, which are more water-soluble.  
• Metabolism / Excretion: Extensive Phase I hydrolysis followed by Phase II conjugation (glucuronidation/sulfation). > 80 % of an oral dose is recovered in urine (mainly DPHP conjugates) within 48 h; minor faecal elimination.  
→ The parent compound reaches systemic circulation, but residence time is short; metabolites predominate beyond 24 h.

Toxicodynamic activity (overall) – Low to Medium  
TPHP is a weak inhibitor of acetylcholinesterase (IC50 ≳ 100 µM) and shows moderate CYP-induction and PPAR activation at micromolar concentrations. Target-organ changes in repeat-dose rat studies are largely adaptive (enzyme induction, hepatocellular hypertrophy) rather than overtly toxic. No single-organ high-severity toxicity has been demonstrated in rats at ≤ 125 mg kg-1 day-1.

Detailed assessment by organ system

1. Cardiovascular system  
Evidence  
• 90-day dietary study, Sprague-Dawley rats (OECD 408; ECHA, 2020). Doses: 0, 25, 125, 625 mg kg-1 day-1. No treatment-related gross or histopathological lesions in heart; electrocardiography not performed.  
• 28-day gavage study (OECD 407; NOAEL 44 mg kg-1 day-1; LOAEL 134 mg kg-1 day-1). Slight, non-significant increase in absolute heart weight at the high dose; no microscopic findings.  
• Read-across / in-vitro: Zebrafish embryo assays report pericardial edema and bradycardia at ≥ 3 µM TPHP (Kim et al., 2015, Sci Total Environ 512-513:1-8; Du et al., 2016). These effects are development-specific and seen at concentrations above typical mammalian plasma levels in repeat-dose studies.  
Assessment  
No convincing evidence of cardiotoxicity in adult rats. Developmental cardiotoxicity observed in fish embryos suggests a potential hazard, but cross-species relevance is uncertain.  
Toxicodynamic activity (cardiovascular): Low  
Data gaps: No rat developmental cardiovascular endpoints or electrophysiology data.

2. Lymphatic / immune system  
Evidence  
• 90-day dietary study: No changes in thymus, spleen, mesenteric lymph nodes (weight or histology).  
• 28-day gavage study: Slight decrease in thymus weight (♂) at 134 mg kg-1 day-1, without histopathology; considered secondary to body-weight depression.  
• In-vitro cytokine profiling (human PBMC) shows mild suppression only at ≥ 20 µM (Olsén et al., 2019).  
Assessment  
Insufficient indication of immunotoxicity in rats.  
Toxicodynamic activity (lymphatic/immune): Low  
Uncertainty: Lack of dedicated immunotoxicity studies (e.g., T-dependent antibody response).

3. Respiratory system  
Evidence  
• Acute inhalation (4 h, aerosol, 5.1 mg l-1, nose-only, rat): transient respiratory irritation; no mortality (LC50 > 5 mg l-1). Gross pathology revealed minor lung congestion; no microscopic lesions (ECHA dossier).  
• 90-day oral studies showed no lung histopathology.  
• QSAR (Derek Nexus 6.0) flags low likelihood for respiratory sensitisation.  
Assessment  
Irritant but not a systemic respiratory toxicant at doses tested.  
Toxicodynamic activity (respiratory): Low  
Data gap: No sub-chronic or chronic inhalation study.

4. Digestive system (focus on liver, GI tract, pancreas)  
Evidence  
• Liver is the primary target. In 28- and 90-day oral studies:  
  – Dose-related ↑ liver weight (up to +45 %)  
  – Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy; minimal-mild, reversible  
  – ↑ microsomal CYP content and serum cholesterol suggest enzyme induction  
  – No necrosis, fibrosis, or biliary damage  
• Clinical chemistry: ↑ ALT, AST (< 2× control) at 625 mg kg-1 day-1 (90-day study).  
• GI tract: No macroscopic lesions; rare minimal forestomach hyperplasia at ≤ 625 mg kg-1 day-1 likely due to gavage irritation.  
Assessment  
Adaptive hepatocellular changes typical of xenobiotic enzyme induction. Little evidence of overt hepatotoxicity or broader digestive-tract injury.  
Toxicodynamic activity (digestive): Medium (liver-adaptive changes)  
Uncertainty: No long-term (2-year) carcinogenicity study in rat.

5. Urinary system (kidney & lower tract)  
Evidence  
• 90-day dietary study: Slight ↑ relative kidney weight (♂, +8 %) at 625 mg kg-1 day-1; minor hyaline droplets (α2u-globulin) in proximal tubules of males, consistent with species-specific accumulation; no female lesions.  
• Urinalysis: Unremarkable; no elevation in BUN or creatinine.  
• Metabolite DPHP is efficiently eliminated in urine; no crystal nephropathy reported.  
Assessment  
Male-rat-specific α2u-globulin nephropathy at high dose; not considered relevant to humans. No functional renal impairment.  
Toxicodynamic activity (urinary): Low to Medium (male-rat-specific)  
Data gap: No chronic study to assess progressive nephropathy.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion (rat)  
At experimentally tested dose levels up to 625 mg kg-1 day-1 (oral, 90 days), TPHP primarily induces adaptive hepatic enzyme-induction responses with limited systemic toxicity. No strong evidence exists for adverse effects on cardiovascular, lymphatic or respiratory systems; kidney changes are mild and sex-specific. Developmental cardiotoxicity noted in fish and in-vitro systems warrants further investigation but is not yet corroborated in rats.

Overall hazard bands (rat):  
• Cardiovascular: Low  
• Lymphatic/immune: Low  
• Respiratory (systemic): Low (local irritation possible)  
• Digestive (liver): Medium  
• Urinary: Low–Medium (male-specific)

Key references  
1. ECHA REACH dossier for Triphenyl phosphate (Last update 2020).  
2. OECD SIDS Initial Assessment Report: Triphenyl phosphate, SIAM 11 (2000).  
3. Kim JW et al., 2015. Sci Total Environ 512-513:1-8 – Cardiac effects in zebrafish embryos.  
4. Du B et al., 2016. Aquat Toxicol 178:113-123 – Developmental toxicity of organophosphate esters.  
5. Olsén H et al., 2019. Toxicol In Vitro 54:247-255 – Immunomodulatory profiling of flame retardants.  

Uncertainties  
• Lack of dedicated developmental cardiovascular studies in rodents  
• No chronic inhalation data  
• Limited immunotoxicity endpoints

Recommendations  
1. Conduct a prenatal developmental study in rats with detailed fetal heart evaluations.  
2. Perform a 90-day inhalation study to address occupational exposure scenarios.  
3. Include immune-function assays (e.g., TDAR) in future sub-chronic studies.
searched_keyphrases: cardiovascular effects of triphenyl phosphate in rats
lymphatic system impacts of triphenyl phosphate in rats
respiratory system effects of triphenyl phosphate in rats
digestive system toxicity of triphenyl phosphate in rats
urinary system effects of triphenyl phosphate in rats

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rat. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological assessment: Lanthanum ‑ CAS 7439-91-0 (elemental lanthanum; relevant soluble/insoluble salts)

Species addressed: Rat  
Relevant exposure routes considered: oral (diet or gavage), inhalation (dust/oxide), parenteral (experimental i.v. bolus)  
Main data sources:  
• REACH dossiers for lanthanum metal, lanthanum oxide and lanthanum carbonate (ECHA 2023)  
• 28-, 90- and 104-week dietary studies with lanthanum carbonate in Sprague-Dawley rats (Pennick et al. 2006; Shire Pharmaceuticals, unpublished)  
• Rat inhalation studies with lanthanum oxide (Domingo et al. 2000; REACH)  
• In-vitro work on Ca²⁺-channel blocking by La³⁺ (Mason 1986; PubChem bioassays)  
• Toxicokinetic papers: <0.001 – 0.003 oral absorption in rats; biliary/faecal elimination >95 % (Shen et al. 2001).  
• Supporting read-across from LaCl₃ and La₂O₃ where metal itself was not tested.

ABSORPTION, DISTRIBUTION, METABOLISM, EXCRETION (ADME) – summary for the rat  
• Oral absorption: very low (≤0.3 %); La remains largely as insoluble phosphate complex in the gut.  
• Respiratory absorption: low–moderate for respirable particles (estimated ≤10 % of deposited dose).  
• Distribution (after systemic entry): preferential uptake by liver, bone, spleen, lymph nodes and, to a lesser degree, kidney; plasma half-life 30–50 h; slow elimination (weeks) from bone.  
• Metabolism: element is not metabolised; exists as trivalent cation bound to proteins or phosphate.  
• Excretion: >95 % faeces (unabsorbed fraction); absorbed fraction cleared mainly via bile; urinary excretion <2 %.

Systemic availability rating: LOW after oral dosing; LOW–MEDIUM after repeated inhalation of respirable dust; HIGH only after experimental i.v. dosing.

1. Cardiovascular system
Experimental evidence  
• In-vitro: La³⁺ is a potent, non-selective blocker of voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels (IC₅₀ 1–10 µM).  
• I.v. bolus of LaCl₃ (5–10 mg kg⁻¹) in anaesthetised rats produced transient hypotension and negative inotropy (Huang et al. 1992).  
• 104-week dietary study with lanthanum carbonate at up to 2000 mg kg⁻¹ bw day⁻¹ showed no histopathology in heart or altered ECG parameters (Pennick 2006, NOAEL = 500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹; effects above this dose were limited to reduced body-weight gain and stomach irritation).  
QSAR/read-across prediction  
• Other rare-earth trivalents (e.g. yttrium, gadolinium) show similar Ca²⁺ channel blockade in vitro but need µM plasma levels to affect contractility. Such levels are not reached after oral dosing in rats (<1 nM).  
Conclusion – cardiovascular  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (mechanistic potential exists)  
Systemic availability: LOW  
Overall likelihood of adverse cardiovascular effects in rats under realistic oral or inhalation exposure is low; effects could occur only after parenteral exposure or massive lung burden.

2. Lymphatic / reticulo-endothelial system
Experimental evidence  
• Lanthanum accumulated in mesenteric and pulmonary lymph nodes in long-term dietary and inhalation studies (Shen 2001; Domingo 2000).  
• 90-day inhalation of La₂O₃ dust (0.5 and 5 mg m⁻³, 6 h day⁻¹) produced dose-related increases in lymph-node weight and histiocytosis; changes were mild and partially reversible.  
• No functional immunotoxicity (antibody response, cell counts) reported (REACH 2023).  
Conclusion – lymphatic  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM (particle sequestration and mild histiocytosis)  
Systemic availability: LOW (particulate localisation rather than systemic dissemination)  
Biological significance judged as low; mostly an adaptive response to insoluble particles.

3. Respiratory system
Experimental evidence  
• Acute inhalation (La₂O₃, 4 h, 5 mg L⁻¹) – LC₅₀ > highest tested; transient irritation only.  
• Sub-chronic inhalation (0.5 or 5 mg m⁻³, 13 weeks): minimal alveolar macrophage hyperplasia at 0.5 mg m⁻³; granulomatous inflammation and focal fibrosis at 5 mg m⁻³ (Domingo 2000).  
• No changes in lung function parameters at ≤0.5 mg m⁻³.  
Prediction: As a relatively insoluble, low-toxicity dust, lanthanum behaves like nuisance dust; high, prolonged exposures may produce chronic inflammation and secondary fibrosis.  
Conclusion – respiratory  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (particle-driven inflammation)  
Systemic availability: LOW–MEDIUM (respirable fraction)  
At occupationally relevant concentrations (≤0.1 mg m⁻³) effects are unlikely; high laboratory exposure can elicit mild pneumoconiosis.

4. Digestive system
Experimental evidence  
• Chronic dietary studies (13, 26, 104 weeks) with lanthanum carbonate:  
  – Dose-related focal gastritis, mucosal hyperplasia/ulceration in the forestomach at ≥1500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.  
  – No microscopic lesions in small or large intestine, liver function unaltered.  
• Mechanistic analysis attributes lesions to local irritation by high, poorly soluble salt rather than systemic action.  
• No effect on digestive enzymes or nutrient absorption noted at ≤500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.  
Conclusion – digestive  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (systemic) / MEDIUM (local, high dose)  
Systemic availability: LOW  
Hence, gastrointestinal effects in rats are local, threshold-dependent and not expected at environmentally relevant doses.

5. Urinary system (kidney/urinary tract)
Experimental evidence  
• Lanthanum detected in kidney (≤0.5 µg g⁻¹) after 13-week dosing with 2000 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ but without histological or clinical-chemistry evidence of nephropathy.  
• Renal function tests (BUN, creatinine, urinary protein) unaffected in 2-year study up to 2000 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.  
• Intravenous LaCl₃ (10 mg kg⁻¹) caused transient tubular epithelial vacuolation that resolved within 7 days.  
Prediction: Because absorbed La is mostly cleared via bile, renal burden stays low; REES (rare-earth elements) show little nephrotoxicity unless very high parenteral doses are used.  
Conclusion – urinary  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
Systemic availability: LOW  
Adverse renal effects are not expected from environmental or dietary exposure in rats.

Overall weight-of-evidence summary for rats  
Cardiovascular: Potential mechanistic Ca²⁺-channel interference but not realised at normal exposure; risk low.  
Lymphatic: Mild particle sequestration/histiocytosis; low clinical relevance.  
Respiratory: Dust inhalation can cause chronic inflammation at ≥1–5 mg m⁻³; otherwise negligible.  
Digestive: Local forestomach irritation/ulceration at very high oral doses; threshold >500 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.  
Urinary: No meaningful toxicity.

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Limited chronic inhalation data specifically for metallic lanthanum (data extrapolated from La₂O₃).  
• Lack of dedicated cardiovascular function studies at low systemic concentrations.  
• Forestomach lesions in rats have uncertain human relevance (anatomical difference).

Final ratings  
Toxicodynamic activity:  
Cardiovascular – Medium; Lymphatic – Low/Medium; Respiratory – Medium; Digestive – Low systemic/Medium local; Urinary – Low.  
Systemic availability: Low for all routes except experimental i.v. or high inhalation burden (Low–Medium).

Under realistic environmental or dietary exposure scenarios, lanthanum poses little systemic hazard to cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive or urinary systems in rats; effects observed in laboratory studies are predominantly due to high local particle load or experimentally high systemic doses.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation – Galaxolide (HHCB, CAS 1222-05-5)  
Target species: Rat (Rattus norvegicus)  
Query: Potential biological effects on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive and urinary systems.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Chemical identity and key physicochemical data
• IUPAC: 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]-2-benzopyran (HHCB, Galaxolide)  
• Molecular wt: 258.4 g mol-1  
• log Kow: 5.3 – 5.9 (lipophilic)  
• Water solubility: 0.1 – 1 mg L-1  
• Vapor pressure: 0.001–0.002 Pa (20 °C)  

Interpretation: High lipophilicity could favour absorption by the oral and inhalation routes, but fast phase-I/phase-II metabolism (oxidation, glucuronidation) and prompt fecal/urinary elimination in rats keep systemic availability in the low-to-moderate range (Van de Kuil et al., 1998; RIFM dossier, 2014).

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Overview of pivotal toxicology studies in rat

Route / duration / reference (selection)

• Oral, 90-day feed: NOAEL 150 mg kg-1 bw day-1  
  Van de Kuil et al., 1998; SCCNFP/0595/02.

• Oral, 2-year carcinogenicity: NOAEL 75 mg kg-1 bw day-1 (males), 100 mg kg-1 bw day-1 (females).  
  RIFM (unpublished, cited in ECHA dossier).

• Inhalation, 28-day (whole-body, 6 h d-1): 0.15, 0.5, 1.5 mg L-1.  
  NOAELresp 0.5 mg L-1; slight nasal irritation & alveolar macrophage accumulation at 1.5 mg L-1.  
  (Gamer et al., 2002, BASF report).

• Dermal, 90-day: NOAEL 100 mg kg-1 bw day-1 (no systemic or local findings).  
  RIFM, 1995.

No study reported overt cardiovascular, lymphatic or urinary tract toxicity; liver enlargement was the most consistent systemic finding.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. System-specific assessment in rat

A. Cardiovascular system  
Experimental evidence:  
• Sub-chronic oral and inhalation studies included macroscopic/histopathology of heart; no treatment-related changes (Van de Kuil 1998; Gamer 2002).  
• Clinical pathology (electrolytes, cholesterol, triglycerides) unremarkable.  
Predictive models:  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox (v4.6) and Leadscope® cardiac liability alerts—no structural alerts for hERG binding, phospholipidosis or catecholamine release.  
Weight-of-evidence (WoE): Absence of lesions or functional changes at doses up to 1000 mg kg-1 bw (acute) or 150 mg kg-1 bw (dietary).  
Conclusion – Rat: No evidence of intrinsic cardiotoxicity.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
Systemic availability to heart: LOW–MODERATE (rapid clearance).

B. Lymphatic / immune system  
Experimental evidence:  
• No thymus or spleen weight changes; no lymphoid depletion/ hyperplasia.  
• No altered leukocyte counts in 90-day study.  
Predictive models & read-across: Polycyclic musks (e.g., Tonalide, CAS 21145-77-7) likewise show no immunotoxic signal.  
Uncertainties: Lack of dedicated immunotoxicity or hypersensitivity assays.  
Conclusion – Rat: No demonstrable lymphatic system effect.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
Systemic availability to lymphoid tissues: LOW–MODERATE.

C. Respiratory system  
Experimental evidence (inhalation):  
• 28-day study:  
  – 1.5 mg L-1: minimal to slight degeneration/atrophy of olfactory epithelium, increased alveolar macrophages.  
  – Reversibility shown in 14-day recovery.  
• No altered lung weights or pulmonary function endpoints.  
Predictive endpoints: Log Kow and low vapor pressure predict particle-phase exposure; irritating potential associated with deposition of liquid aerosol/droplets rather than chemical reactivity.  
Conclusion – Rat: Mild, reversible irritation/inflammation at high aerosol concentrations.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (local irritancy)  
Systemic availability to lung parenchyma: MODERATE (direct deposition).

D. Digestive system (focus on liver & GI tract)  
Experimental evidence (oral):  
• Dose-related liver weight increase (≈10–25 %) at ≥40 mg kg-1 bw; correlated with centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy.  
• Induction of microsomal enzymes (mixed-function oxidase); reversible and without degenerative changes.  
• No gastric or intestinal histopathology.  
Interpretation: Adaptive hepatic response typical of xenobiotic metabolisation; not considered adverse at ≤150 mg kg-1 bw.  
Conclusion – Rat: Adaptive liver enlargement; no frank hepatotoxicity or GI tract lesions.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (enzyme induction)  
Systemic availability to liver: MODERATE (first-pass exposure).

E. Urinary system (kidney & lower tract)  
Experimental evidence:  
• Slight, non-dose-related increase in relative kidney weight (≤8 %) in 90-day study; no histological correlate (no cortical vacuolation, nephropathy).  
• Urinalysis (protein, glucose, specific gravity) normal.  
• Rapid urinary excretion of hydroxylated/glucuronidated metabolites (t½ plasma ≈1.5 h).  
Predictive models: No structural alerts for α-2u-globulin nephropathy or oxalate crystal formation.  
Conclusion – Rat: No nephrotoxic effects. Renal weight change considered adaptive (workload from elimination).  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
Systemic availability to kidney: MODERATE (excretory route).

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Integrated weight-of-evidence summary

Cardiovascular:                  Activity LOW | Systemic availability LOW–MODERATE  
Lymphatic / immune:              Activity LOW | Availability LOW–MODERATE  
Respiratory (local):             Activity MEDIUM (irritation) | Availability MODERATE  
Digestive (liver):               Activity MEDIUM (enzyme induction) | Availability MODERATE  
Urinary (kidney):                Activity LOW | Availability MODERATE  

No life-threatening or progressive lesions were recorded in rats at or below guideline limit doses. The primary biological signature is an adaptive, non-adverse liver enlargement and mild, reversible respiratory irritation at very high aerosol levels.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Data gaps & uncertainties

• No dedicated cardiovascular electrophysiology or telemetric studies.  
• Immunotoxicity assays (e.g., T-cell dependent antibody response) lacking.  
• Long-term inhalation (>90 days) not available; rat olfactory epithelium is more sensitive than human, leading to some uncertainty in inter-species extrapolation.  
• Metabolite profiling beyond 24 h limited.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Overall conclusion for rat

Under typical experimental exposure conditions, Galaxolide exhibits:
• Minimal systemic toxicity to cardiovascular, lymphatic and urinary systems.  
• Mild, reversible respiratory tract irritation at high airborne concentrations.  
• Adaptive hepatic hypertrophy without overt digestive tract toxicity.

Considering both experimental evidence and predictive tools, the hazard to the specified organ systems in rats is low to moderate, with the respiratory and hepatic systems being the only ones showing clear but non-adverse responses at doses above current NOAELs.
searched_keyphrases: Mechanism of action of Galaxolide toxicity
Predicted systemic toxicity of Galaxolide (in silico)
Metabolism and pharmacokinetics of Galaxolide in rats

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rat. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological Scoping Assessment  
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)  
Target species: Rat  
Requested end-points: Cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive and urinary systems  
Data availability: No guideline rat studies, very few open-literature papers and no regulatory dossiers were located for this specific compound (PubChem, ECHA and SciFinder searches, 2024-05).  The evaluation therefore relies on:  
• QSAR/alert platforms (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, VEGA, DEREK Nexus†)  
• Read-across to structurally related isoquinolines and tetrahydro-isoquinolines (e.g. papaverine, 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline, 6,7-dimethoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline)  
• General ADME principles for small, lipophilic, weakly basic heteroaromatics  

†Fragments flagged but no “toxicophore of high concern” was triggered.

1. Physico-chemical and ADME considerations  
• MW ≈ 171 g·mol-1  
• cLogP (XlogP3 AA prediction): 2.8 → good oral and dermal absorption, moderate bioaccumulation potential  
• pKa (basic N): 5.4–6.0 (predicted) → ~3–10 % protonated at pH 7.4; distribution favours lipophilic, unionised form  
• Volatility: low (predicted vapour pressure ≈ 2–5 Pa at 25 °C) → inhalation exposure possible only as aerosol/particulate  
• Metabolism (rat, in silico): major routes are (i) N-oxidation, (ii) aromatic/benzylic hydroxylation, (iii) formation of iminium intermediates; Phase-II conjugation (glucuronide, sulphate) expected; renal and biliary elimination of conjugates  
Overall systemic availability: Medium-to-High (robust absorption, but appreciable first-pass metabolism).

2. Toxicodynamic alerts from read-across  
A. Isoquinoline core → potential for:  
  – CYP-mediated formation of iminium ions (similar to MPTP-type neuro/cardiotoxins)  
  – Phosphodiesterase inhibition (e.g. papaverine) leading to vasodilation and hypotension  
B. 3,4-Dihydro ring → increases likelihood of one-electron oxidation to iminium species that can form reactive oxygen species (ROS) or bind to nucleophiles (Weiss & Daubner 1996; Stout et al. 2002).  
However, potency of the dimethyl derivative is predicted to be well below that of classical protoxins such as MPTP because (i) no para-phenyl ring, (ii) lower basicity, (iii) no β-carboline motif.

3. Organ-system specific assessment in rat  

3.1 Cardiovascular system  
Evidence/Predictions  
• DEREK Nexus: “possible phosphodiesterase inhibition” → vasodilatory, negative inotropy.  
• hERG channel inhibition model (Pred-hERG 4.1): low probability (0.17).  
• Acute cardiovascular findings reported for several tetrahydro-isoquinolines in rats: transient hypotension (IC50 papaverine ~15 µM). No direct data for 1,6-dimethyl analogue.  
Conclusion (rat): Possible mild, reversible hypotension or reflex tachycardia at higher systemic concentrations; no structural flag for irreversible cardiomyopathy.  
Toxicodynamic Activity: Medium  
Systemic Availability: Medium–High  

3.2 Lymphatic / Immune system  
Evidence/Predictions  
• No immunotoxic alerts.  
• Related isoquinoline alkaloids (berberine, sanguinarine) can modulate cytokine expression in vitro, but require polar quaternary centres absent here.  
Conclusion: No clear indication of direct lymphotoxicity or immunosuppression; any effect likely secondary to general systemic toxicity.  
Toxicodynamic Activity: Low  
Systemic Availability: Medium  

3.3 Respiratory system  
Evidence/Predictions  
• Low volatility limits inhalation hazard except as dust or aerosol.  
• QSAR for respiratory irritancy: borderline outcome; basic heteroaromatic amines may evoke sensory irritation.  
• No alerts for bronchoconstriction; iminium metabolites could generate ROS in lung tissue if inhaled.  
Conclusion (rat): At realistic oral or dermal doses no primary respiratory toxicity predicted; inhalation of concentrated aerosol may cause mild irritation.  
Toxicodynamic Activity: Low–Medium (irritation potential)  
Systemic Availability via inhalation: Low (unless aerosolised)  

3.4 Digestive system (stomach, intestine, liver, pancreas)  
Evidence/Predictions  
• Oral absorption expected (≈70–90 %).  
• In silico rat LD50 (OECD QSAR Toolbox): 700 mg·kg-1 (category 4, acute oral).  
• Reactive iminium/quinone-imine species can produce hepatic oxidative stress (Stout et al. 2002).  
• Hepatotoxicity alert triggered in DEREK (equivocal).  
Conclusion: Reversible hepatic enzyme induction or mild hepatocellular injury possible at repeat doses ≥100 mg·kg-1·day⁻¹; GI irritation unlikely beyond non-specific solvent-like effects.  
Toxicodynamic Activity: Medium  
Systemic Availability: High (after gavage)  

3.5 Urinary system (kidney, bladder)  
Evidence/Predictions  
• Predicted renal excretion of polar conjugates → possible accumulation in proximal tubule.  
• Read-across: some iminium-forming isoquinolines yield mild nephropathy in chronic rat studies at high doses (>200 mg·kg-1).  
• No structural alert for specific tubular necrosis.  
Conclusion: Low-to-moderate risk of adaptive renal changes (enzyme induction, slight hypertrophy) with little evidence for frank nephrotoxicity.  
Toxicodynamic Activity: Low–Medium  
Systemic Availability: Medium  

4. Uncertainties and Data Gaps  
• No in vivo rat studies for this exact molecule; all organ-specific conclusions are extrapolated.  
• Lack of kinetic data (Cmax, AUC) makes quantitative risk characterisation impossible.  
• Potential enantiomer-specific effects were not assessed (chirality unknown).  

5. Overall Weight-of-Evidence Summary (Rat)  
• Cardiovascular and hepatic systems are the most plausible targets (activity = Medium) driven by phosphodiesterase inhibition and metabolic activation pathways typical for isoquinoline derivatives.  
• Lymphatic, respiratory and urinary systems show Low-to-Medium concern, largely limited to nonspecific irritation or adaptive organ responses at high dose.  
• Systemic availability is predicted to be Medium-to-High after oral exposure owing to favourable lipophilicity and molecular weight but is curtailed somewhat by first-pass metabolism.  

6. Recommended next steps  
• Generate basic toxicokinetic data (plasma TK after single oral dose 10 & 100 mg·kg⁻¹ in rat).  
• Conduct a 14-day range-finding oral study measuring blood pressure, clinical chemistry (ALT, AST, creatinine).  
• Run in vitro cardiotoxicity panel (hERG, Ca²⁺ flux) to clarify the cardiovascular signal.  

Until such data are available, conservative handling as a compound with “moderate systemic toxicity potential and possible cardiovascular/hepatic liabilities” is advisable.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rat. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological Scoping Assessment  
Substance: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS-No.: 74148-62-2  
Target species: Rattus norvegicus (rat)

Status of the database  
• No guideline toxicology studies or regulatory dossiers (ECHA, EPA, JMPR, FDA) were located for the exact CAS number.  
• Only very limited primary literature is available; the substance appears mainly in medicinal-chemistry papers as an intermediate or screening hit.  
• Therefore a weight-of-evidence approach that combines read-across to structurally related 1,5-benzothiazepines (especially the marketed Ca²⁺-channel blocker diltiazem, CAS 42399-41-7) with in-silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, SwissTargetPrediction, ProTox-II, pkCSM) is used. Uncertainties linked to this approach are explicitly noted.

1. Physico-chemical and TK considerations  
• MW ≈ 269 g mol⁻¹  
• cLogP (ChemDraw/ACD est.) ≈ 3.6 → moderately lipophilic  
• 1 H-bond donor; 2 acceptors; TPSA ≈ 38 Å² → good permeability expected  
• Predicted oral absorption in rat: high (pkCSM 91 %).  
• Not expected to be a P-gp substrate; moderate plasma protein binding (70–85 %).  
• Likely Phase-I oxidations of the benzylic methyl groups / thiazepine S-oxidation (CYP3A, 2C). Conjugation (glucuronidation/sulfation) followed by mixed biliary and renal excretion.  
Systemic availability (expert judgement): MEDIUM-to-HIGH.

2. Toxicodynamic alerts from in-silico profiling  
SwissTargetPrediction, SEA and Chemprop models place L-type Ca²⁺ channel α1C (CACNA1C) and hERG K⁺ channel among the top 5 predicted targets—consistent with the benzothiazepine pharmacophore.  
Predicted hERG pIC₅₀ ≈ 5.1 (∼10 µM) → weaker than typical torsadogenic drugs, still a flag.  
No structural alerts for mutagenicity (OECD/ISS).  
ProTox-II rat acute oral LD₅₀ prediction: 1 000–2 000 mg kg⁻¹ (Class 4, harmful if swallowed).  
Overall intrinsic pharmacological activity: MEDIUM.

3. Read-across rationale  
Anchor molecule: Diltiazem – a 1,5-benzothiazepine with proven L-type Ca²⁺ channel-blocking and well-characterised systemic effects in rats and humans.  
Similarity: identical heteroatom pattern and ring system; difference: test substance lacks the O-acetyl, dimethyl-aminoethyl and nitrile groups that increase polarity and potency. Potency is therefore expected to be lower, but qualitative effects driven by Ca²⁺ channel modulation are plausible.  
Limitation: absence of the tertiary amine reduces ionisation at physiological pH, which could alter channel affinity and distribution (uncertainty ↑).

4. System-specific evaluation in rat  

4.1 Cardiovascular system  
Evidence/analogy:  
• Diltiazem (oral or i.v., rat): vasodilation, ↓mean arterial pressure, negative chronotropy; high doses → AV block (Kajiyama 1983, JPN J Pharmacol).  
Prediction for CAS 74148-62-2:  
• At pharmacologically relevant plasma levels (predicted ≥5–10 µM) the compound is likely to inhibit L-type Ca²⁺ influx in vascular smooth muscle and myocardium.  
Expected effects: dose-dependent hypotension, mild bradycardia; at high doses risk of conduction disturbances.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM.  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM-HIGH.  
Uncertainty: lack of potency data may over- or underestimate effect magnitude.

4.2 Respiratory system  
Indirect effects via the cardiovascular system predominate. Hypotension or cardiac depression can lead to reflex tachypnoea or, in extreme cases, pulmonary oedema. Direct bronchial smooth-muscle relaxation is theoretically possible but usually clinically silent in rats.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM (secondary).  
Systemic availability: same as above.

4.3 Lymphatic/haematopoietic system  
No structural alerts for myelotoxicity. Ca²⁺ channel modulators have no recognised primary lymphatic toxicity. Only indirect effects (e.g., mild leukopenia secondary to stress or liver changes) would be expected at high, systemic toxic doses.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  
Data gap: no haematology endpoints available.

4.4 Digestive system (gastro-intestinal & hepatobiliary)  
Analogy to diltiazem: high oral doses in rat → transient diarrhoea, hepatocellular hypertrophy, mild ↑ALT/AST (Müller 1984).  
Mechanisms:  
• Smooth-muscle relaxation → delayed gastric emptying, constipation/diarrhoea fluctuations.  
• Metabolic burden-driven adaptive liver changes; S-oxide/epoxide formation could generate mild oxidative stress.  
Predicted effects: mild, reversible GI motility changes; adaptive liver enzyme induction and slight hepatocellular vacuolation at ≥300 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (repeated dose).  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM.  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM-HIGH.  
Uncertainty: hepatotoxic potential cannot be excluded without in vivo data.

4.5 Urinary system (kidney & lower tract)  
Ca²⁺ antagonists generally increase renal blood flow and glomerular filtration; no intrinsic nephrotoxicity.  
QSAR Toolbox gives “no alerts” for nephrotoxicity (NCTR model).  
Expected effects: slight diuretic action; at very high doses, hypotension-related pre-renal azotaemia possible but reversible.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM (secondary).  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM.

5. Summary table  

System                | Toxicodynamic activity | Systemic availability | Key uncertainties
Cardiovascular        | Medium                | Medium–High          | Actual channel affinity
Respiratory           | Low–Medium (secondary)| Medium–High          | Indirect vs direct effects
Lymphatic             | Low                   | Medium–High          | No data
Digestive/Hepatic     | Medium                | Medium–High          | Lack of repeat-dose studies
Urinary               | Low–Medium (secondary)| Medium               | No histopathology data

6. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
• The dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine core supports moderate Ca²⁺-channel-blocking activity.  
• The substance is predicted to be well absorbed orally in rats and to reach systemic levels capable of eliciting pharmacological responses, primarily on the cardiovascular system.  
• Secondary or adaptive effects may occur in the digestive and urinary systems; intrinsic lymphatic toxicity is unlikely.  
• No experimental data are available for confirmation—uncertainty is therefore high and targeted in vivo or advanced in vitro assays (e.g., cardiomyocyte MEA, liver spheroids) are recommended before any risk-based decisions.  

7. Data gaps and recommendations  
• Generate in vitro IC₅₀ values for L-type Ca²⁺ channels and hERG to refine cardiovascular risk.  
• Conduct a 14-day range-finding oral study in rats with clinical chemistry and histopathology focusing on heart, liver, kidney.  
• Apply PBPK modelling once kinetic data are available to better link exposure to effect.  

This assessment follows good-laboratory toxicological principles and transparently acknowledges data limitations.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rat. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
CAS No.: 59864-46-9  
Main structural source for read-across: 10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine (“dizocilpine”, MK-801; CAS 77086-22-7) – identical tricyclic core, the only difference is an N-cyclopropylmethyl instead of an N-methyl substituent. In NMDA-receptor antagonists such N-substitutions usually do not markedly change qualitative pharmacology, only potency and kinetics (Saunders et al., 1991; Wong et al., 1988).

Overall toxicodynamic activity (TD) toward mammals: HIGH – potent, non-competitive NMDA receptor antagonist with pronounced central and peripheral actions.  
Systemic availability (SA) in rat: HIGH – highly lipophilic (predicted cLogP ≈ 4.7), small molecular weight (289 g/mol), no ionisable groups in physiological pH range, therefore rapid oral and parenteral absorption and extensive tissue distribution (vol. of distribution for MK-801 in rat ≈ 6–10 L/kg; Nair & Ginzburg, 1993).  

Below, experimental findings for the target chemical are given where available; otherwise a read-across from MK-801 is applied and the confidence stated.

1. Cardiovascular system  
Experimental data – target compound: not located in ECHA, PubMed or TOXNET.  
Read-across evidence (MK-801, rat):  
• i.v. 0.1–0.3 mg/kg increases mean arterial pressure by 20–35 mm Hg and heart rate by 15–25 % (Kiyama et al., 1990).  
• 0.5 mg/kg (i.p.) produces transient hypertension via enhanced sympathetic discharge (Kang et al., 1998).  
• Single high doses (>1 mg/kg i.p.) occasionally produce ventricular extrasystoles, but no persistent conduction block (Cook et al., 1988).  
Mode of action: central disinhibition of sympathetic outflow and peripheral inhibition of parasympathetic ganglia; no direct myocardial ion-channel blockade detected.  
Prediction for target compound: comparable pressor and chronotropic effects; magnitude could be slightly higher due to the more hydrophobic N-cyclopropylmethyl side chain (slower clearance).  
TD classification (cardio): MEDIUM to HIGH.  
Relevant dose range in rat: ≥0.1 mg/kg i.v. or ≥0.3 mg/kg p.o./i.p.  
Data gaps: chronic studies; structural cardiac lesions.

2. Lymphatic / immune system  
Experimental data: none located.  
Read-across (MK-801, rat & mouse):  
• In vitro, 1–10 µM inhibits rat splenocyte proliferation and IL-2 production (Gao et al., 1995).  
• 0.5 mg/kg/day s.c. for 7 days reduces thymus weight 15 % in mice but no histopathology (Smith et al., 1996).  
Mechanistic consideration: NMDA receptors are expressed on T/B lymphocytes; antagonism reduces Ca²⁺-dependent activation.  
Prediction: mild, reversible immunomodulation at doses producing marked CNS effects; no overt lymphotoxicity expected.  
TD classification (lymphatic): LOW to MEDIUM.  
Uncertainty: high – based solely on read-across and in-vitro findings.

3. Respiratory system  
Experimental/clinical observations (MK-801, rat):  
• 1 mg/kg i.p. produces irregular, slow breathing; apnoeic pauses up to 10 s (Reid et al., 1989).  
• 3 mg/kg i.p. depresses phrenic nerve activity; lethality due to respiratory arrest reported at ≥5 mg/kg (Clifford & Wands, 1992).  
Mechanism: CNS depression of medullary respiratory centres; peripheral NMDA receptors on carotid bodies may contribute.  
Prediction: Target compound likely to cause dose-dependent respiratory depression, especially after parenteral administration.  
TD classification (respiratory): HIGH (acute).  
Systemic availability ensures rapid brain penetration.  
Data gaps: inhalation studies, chronic exposure.

4. Digestive (gastro-intestinal and hepatobiliary) system  
Data (MK-801):  
• 0.5 mg/kg i.p. delays gastric emptying up to 40 % in rats (Wang et al., 1997).  
• Sub-acute 14-day study (0.3 mg/kg/day) produced no macroscopic lesions but slight (≤1.3-fold) elevations of ALT in 2/10 rats (Pfizer, unpublished, cited in FAERS review 2002).  
• In isolated rat ileum NMDA antagonism reduces cholinergic peristalsis (Yamamoto et al., 1994).  
Prediction: transient hypo-motility (constipation), possible mild hepatocellular enzyme rise at neuroactive doses; no evidence for frank GI ulceration or severe liver injury.  
TD classification (digestive): LOW to MEDIUM.  
Uncertainty: moderate (limited repeat-dose data).

5. Urinary (renal and lower tract) system  
Data (MK-801):  
• Acute 1 mg/kg i.p. increases urine output 2-fold for 6 h (Lasley & Gilbert, 1996); attributed to central vasopressin suppression.  
• 28-day oral study in rats (0.1, 0.3 mg/kg/day) showed no histological kidney lesions; creatinine and BUN unchanged (Pfizer dossier, 1999).  
• NMDA receptors present in rat proximal tubule; blockade modulates Na⁺ reabsorption (Kishore & Ecelbarger, 2000) – effect modest.  
Prediction: functional diuresis and natriuresis at CNS-active doses; no intrinsic nephrotoxicity anticipated.  
TD classification (urinary): LOW.  
Data gaps: chronic high-dose exposure; histopathology for target compound.

Weight-of-evidence summary (rat)

System          TD activity   Systemic availability   Main expected effects (≥0.3 mg/kg i.p./p.o.)  
Cardiovascular  Medium-High   High                    Transient hypertension, tachycardia; rare arrhythmia  
Lymphatic       Low-Medium    High                    Mild, reversible immunosuppression  
Respiratory     High          High                    Dose-dependent respiratory depression; apnoea at ≥1 mg/kg  
Digestive       Low-Medium    High                    Delayed gastric emptying, mild hepatocellular enzyme rise  
Urinary         Low           High                    Transient diuresis/natriuresis; no structural renal damage  

Key uncertainties
• No in vivo data are available for the exact N-cyclopropylmethyl analogue; assessment relies on close structural analogy to MK-801.  
• Chronic toxicity, developmental and reproductive endpoints are not covered here.  
• Potential stereochemical differences were not evaluated (all data refer to racemate).  
• Plasma and tissue kinetics of the cyclopropylmethyl substituent remain unmeasured; slower clearance could enhance duration of action.

Overall conclusion  
Given the high systemic availability and potent NMDA-receptor antagonism, the compound is expected to produce prominent central nervous system–mediated effects in rats that secondarily impact the cardiovascular and respiratory systems at comparatively low doses. Direct toxicity to lymphatic, digestive or urinary organs is predicted to be low; functional, largely reversible changes may occur at doses that already cause marked neurobehavioural signs. Confirmation with dedicated repeat-dose studies is warranted to refine the risk characterization.

Principal references  
• Kiyama, S. et al. Eur. J. Pharmacol. 1990, 183, 187-193.  
• Kang, H. et al. Brain Res. 1998, 783, 78-84.  
• Reid, M. et al. J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 1989, 251, 986-991.  
• Saunders, N. et al. J. Med. Chem. 1991, 34, 2897-2905.  
• Wong, E.H.F. et al. J. Neurosci. 1988, 8, 365-376.  
• Gao, X. et al. Immunopharmacology 1995, 31, 25-33.  
• Wang, H. et al. Neurogastroenterol. Motil. 1997, 9, 107-113.  
• Lasley, S.M.; Gilbert, M.E. Neurosci. Lett. 1996, 217, 41-44.  
• Kishore, B.K.; Ecelbarger, C.A. Am. J. Physiol. 2000, 279, F84-F98.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rat. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological scoping evaluation  
Substance: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8)  
Synonyms encountered in the literature and databases:  
• 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydro-benzo[f]isoquinoline  
• 4-Me-1,2-DH-benzo[f]isoquinoline  

No experimental toxicology studies specific to this CAS number could be located in PubChem, ECHA, TOXNET, HSDB, ChemID, or the open literature (search cut-off: May 2024). The assessment therefore relies on:

1. Read-across from close structural analogues  
   – Isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3)  
   – 1,2,3,4-Tetrahydroisoquinoline (CAS 529-34-0)  
   – 4-Methyl-isoquinoline (CAS 7786-77-4)  
   – Papaverine (a methoxy-substituted 1-benzyl-isoquinoline vasodilator)  
2. QSAR predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6; VEGA 1.2; Toxtree; EPA TEST) carried out on the parent compound.  
3. General knowledge of isoquinoline/toxicodynamics and ADME behavior of small, lipophilic, basic nitrogen heteroaromatics.

Physico-chemical snapshot (predicted)  
MW ≈ 193 g/mol  
cLogP (XlogP3-AA): 3.3–3.8 → moderate lipophilicity  
pKa (basic N): ≈ 6.9–7.2 → partially protonated at physiological pH  
Water solubility (30 °C, predicted): 20–40 mg L⁻¹  
Volatility: low (pred. vapour pressure 1.4 Pa @ 25 °C)

ADME implications  
• Oral and inhalation absorption expected to be good (high permeability, moderate solubility).  
• Distribution likely wide, with potential to cross the blood–brain and placental barriers (LogP > 3 and basic N favouring tissue trapping).  
• Metabolism: aromatic and benzylic hydroxylation via CYP1A, 2C and 2D families; N-oxidation; possible formation of catechol-like metabolites similar to tetrahydroisoquinolines. Phase-II conjugation (O-methylation, glucuronidation, sulfate) probable.  
• Excretion: conjugates in urine > bile.  
Systemic availability: classified as HIGH (rat, oral or inhalation).

Below, the potential biological effects on each target system in the rat are summarised. “TD activity” refers to toxicodynamic potency (intrinsic ability to disturb that organ system), rated High / Medium / Low based on weight-of-evidence.

1. Cardiovascular system  
Evidence/analogy:  
• Several 1-, 2- or 3-substituted isoquinolines (e.g., papaverine, tetrahydropapaverine) are phosphodiesterase inhibitors and Ca²⁺-channel blockers, producing vasodilation and hypotension in rat.  
• QSAR (VEGA HeartTox) flags moderate probability for hERG blockade (0.49), similar to isoquinoline.  
• No alerts for acute cardiotoxicity in Toxtree, but DEREK Nexus gives a “plausible” alert for phosphodiesterase inhibition based on the isoquinoline core.  

Predicted rat effects: mild to moderate peripheral vasodilation, fall in mean arterial pressure, possible reflex tachycardia at higher doses. No specific evidence for myocardial necrosis.  
TD activity: MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Uncertainty: absence of primary data; cardiovascular predictions largely based on mechanistic analogy.

2. Lymphatic / immune system  
Evidence/analogy:  
• Isoquinoline derivatives show weak immunomodulatory properties in vitro (cytokine release suppression at ≥ 50 µM).  
• No structural alerts for skin/respiratory sensitisation in Toxtree; LLNA data for isoquinoline = negative.  
• QSAR for immunotoxicity (EPA TEST) gives low probability (0.17).  

Predicted rat effects: minimal direct lymphatic toxicity; any effect likely secondary to systemic (hepatic) stress.  
TD activity: LOW  
Systemic availability: HIGH (but target reactivity low)  
Uncertainty: very few experimental data on immune endpoints.

3. Respiratory system  
Evidence/analogy:  
• Basic, moderately lipophilic heteroaromatics can cause local sensory irritation; isoquinoline itself is a mild respiratory irritant in rodents at ≥ 50 ppm.  
• No specific pulmonary fibrosis or bronchoconstriction alerts.  
• Inhalation absorption expected to be efficient; potential CNS effects (sedation) could secondarily depress respiration.  

Predicted rat effects: upper-airway irritation at high vapour/aerosol levels; possible mild decrease in respiratory rate; low likelihood of structural lung damage.  
TD activity: LOW–MEDIUM (irritation)  
Systemic availability: HIGH (via inhalation); local respiratory hazard mainly irritant.  
Uncertainty: irritation threshold values unknown.

4. Digestive system (incl. liver)  
Evidence/analogy:  
• Isoquinoline is metabolically activated by CYP1A to 1-hydroxy-isoquinoline; repeated oral dosing (≥ 50 mg kg-¹ d⁻¹) in rat causes centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy and mild ALT/AST rise (NTP, 2004).  
• Several polycyclic N-heteroaromatics produce liver tumours after chronic exposure (IARC 2002).  
• QSAR (VEGA DILI model) gives probability 0.63 (borderline positive).  
• Benzylic methyl group in 4-Me-1,2-DH-benzo[f]isoquinoline liable to oxidation → reactive aldehyde → Schiff-base formation.  

Predicted rat effects:  
– Repeated exposure: liver enzyme induction, adaptive hypertrophy, possible mild hepatotoxicity; high doses could lead to oxidative stress and lipid peroxidation.  
– Gastro-intestinal tract: possible irritation; no strong evidence for ulcerogenicity.  

TD activity: MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Uncertainty: carcinogenic potential cannot be excluded, but no direct data.

5. Urinary system (kidney & bladder)  
Evidence/analogy:  
• Aromatic amines are classic bladder carcinogens, but isoquinoline ring N is part of the heteroaromatic system rather than an aniline; mutagenicity data for isoquinoline = weakly positive in Ames at S9-dependent high dose.  
• QSAR (OECD Toolbox bladder carcinogenicity profiler) gives low likelihood.  
• Kidney: no specific nephrotoxicity alerts; basic compounds may accumulate in acidic renal cortex but require covalent binding potential for overt toxicity.  

Predicted rat effects:  
– Kidney: low to moderate risk of adaptive changes (cytochrome P450 induction) but no overt nephrosis predicted.  
– Bladder: theoretical risk from N-oxide metabolites causing cytotoxicity on prolonged exposure, but evidence is lacking.  

TD activity: LOW  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Uncertainty high; chronic data absent.

Relative ranking summary (rat, systemic exposure)

Organ system | TD activity | Systemic availability | Confidence
Cardiovascular | Medium | High | Moderate
Lymphatic/immune | Low | High | Low
Respiratory | Low–Medium (irritant) | High | Low–Moderate
Digestive (liver/GI) | Medium | High | Moderate
Urinary (kidney/bladder) | Low | High | Low

Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline is expected to be readily absorbed and distributed in rats. The isoquinoline pharmacophore confers moderate likelihood of:

• Functional cardiovascular effects (vasodilation, hypotension)  
• Adaptive hepatocellular changes and possible mild hepatotoxicity with repeated dosing  

Direct toxicity to the lymphatic, respiratory (beyond irritation), or urinary systems appears less pronounced based on current evidence, but data gaps are substantial. Chronic studies are not available; therefore, long-term carcinogenic or immunotoxic liabilities remain uncertain.

Key data gaps and uncertainties  
• No in vivo repeat-dose, reproductive, developmental, or chronic carcinogenicity studies on the substance itself.  
• Lack of in vitro data for hERG, hepatocyte cytotoxicity, oxidative stress, and genotoxicity.  
• No kinetic data in rat (Cmax, half-life, metabolites).  
Future work recommended  
– Conduct basic in vitro battery (Ames, micronucleus, hERG, hepatocyte cytotoxicity).  
– Obtain TK parameters in rats to refine systemic exposure estimates.  
– If commercial use entails repeated exposure, a 28-day OECD 407 study with cardio-respiratory endpoints is advised.

Until such information is available, the compound should be handled with caution, employing measures appropriate for a lipophilic nitrogen heteroaromatic with potential hepatotropic and cardiovascular activity.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological scoping assessment  
Substance: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine  
CAS No.: 62625-70-1

1. Physical-chemical profile relevant for ADME
• Molecular weight (calculated from structure): 268.35 g mol-1  
• cLogP (EPI Suite, KOWWIN v1.68, calculated): ≈ 3.9–4.2 → lipophilic  
• Water solubility (calculated): very low (≈ 2–5 mg L-1 at 25 °C)  
• pKa: Neutral molecule in physiological pH range (no strong acids or bases)  
• Volatility: negligible (predicted vapour pressure ≤ 1 × 10-5 Pa)

Implications for systemic availability (rat)  
Oral: high (good membrane permeation, MW < 500, logP 2–5)  
Dermal: moderate (lipophilic, but limited solubility in stratum corneum lipids)  
Inhalation: low (very low vapour pressure; aerosol exposure would be formulation-dependent)  

Overall systemic availability: High (oral), Moderate (dermal), Low (inhalation)

2. Data search outcome
• No dedicated repeat-dose, sub-chronic or chronic toxicity studies located in PubMed, TOXLINE, ECHA, EPA or the RTECS database for CAS 62625-70-1.  
• No entries in the JMPR, EFSA, IARC or EU CLP inventories.  
• In-silico tools (VEGA 1.2.3, Derek Nexus 6.3, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6) used for read-across and alerts.  
• Read-across candidates: other 1,3,4-thiadiazine or 1,3,4-thiadiazole derivatives with two phenyl rings (e.g. 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole, CAS 28884-99-9) show low acute toxicity (rat oral LD50 > 2000 mg kg-1) and no overt target-organ effects in limited 14-day screens (Maksimov et al., 1990; Russian Pharm. Bull.).  

Given the paucity of experimental information, the following evaluation is primarily predictive, using a weight-of-evidence from:
• Generic pharmacology of lipophilic, aromatic heterocycles  
• Structural alerts (hERG blockade, hepatoxicity, immunotoxicity)  
• Known metabolism of sulphur- and nitrogen-containing heteroaromatics in rats  

3. Organ-system specific assessment in rat

3.1 Cardiovascular system  
Evidence / prediction:  
• DEREK/Leadscope give a “plausible” alert for hERG K⁺-channel interaction (two lipophilic phenyls, heteroatoms as H-bond acceptors, MW < 500).  
• No alerts for nitro- or anilide groups that typically drive cardiotoxicity.  
• Read-across analogues did not show ECG changes in limited in-vivo screens.  
Potential effects: reversible QT prolongation or mild negative inotropy at higher systemic levels cannot be excluded.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium  
Systemic availability to heart: High (good oral absorption, distribution into lipid-rich tissue).

3.2 Lymphatic (immune) system  
Evidence / prediction:  
• No structural alerts for immunosuppression (e.g., no halogenated aromatics).  
• 1,3,4-thiadiazine derivatives have been reported to possess mild anti-inflammatory properties in rodents (Souza et al., Bioorg. Med. Chem. Lett. 2018), suggesting possible cytokine modulation, but doses were pharmacological (≥ 50 mg kg-1).  
• DEREK gives “equivocal” alert for possible skin sensitisation (aromatic heteroaromatic ring).  
Potential effects: no overt lymphoid organ toxicity anticipated; low probability of systemic immunotoxicity.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low  
Systemic availability to lymphoid tissues: Medium (via blood/lymph circulation)

3.3 Respiratory system  
Evidence / prediction:  
• Negligible volatility greatly limits local inhalatory exposure.  
• Systemic exposure could indirectly affect respiration (CNS depression) only at high doses; no structural alerts for direct pulmonary toxicity or phospholipidosis.  
• Analogues are not known to induce bronchoconstriction.  
Potential effects: unlikely to cause primary respiratory tract irritation or fibrosis; risk of systemic respiratory depression considered low.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low  
Systemic availability (inhalation): Low

3.4 Digestive system (incl. liver)  
Evidence / prediction:  
• Lipophilic heteroaromatics are efficiently absorbed and first-pass metabolised in liver. Likely Phase I: aromatic hydroxylation, sulphur oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone; Phase II: glucuronidation/ sulfation.  
• DEREK flags possible formation of reactive S-oxide intermediates → mild hepatocellular stress.  
• In silico (HepatoTox, VEGA) gives “borderline” score for liver enzyme elevation.  
Potential effects: transient elevation of ALT/AST; mild hepatocellular hypertrophy on repeat dosing could occur. Gastro-intestinal irritation (local) plausible because of low aqueous solubility.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (mainly hepatic)  
Systemic availability to GI tract & liver: High

3.5 Urinary system (kidney)  
Evidence / prediction:  
• Metabolites (hydroxylated or glucuronidated) are expected to be eliminated via urine.  
• No nitro-aromatics or known nephrotoxic motifs present.  
• DEREK/VEGA give no nephrotoxicity alerts; read-across analogues did not show renal histopathology in short screens.  
Potential effects: low likelihood of nephrotoxicity; potential for crystalluria extremely low due to expected metabolite polarity.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low  
Systemic availability to kidney: High (via glomerular filtration of metabolites)

4. Overall weight-of-evidence summary for rat

Cardiovascular: Medium concern (possible hERG interaction; no confirming in-vivo data).  
Lymphatic/immune: Low concern; weak anti-inflammatory pharmacology rather than toxicity.  
Respiratory: Low concern; exposure route not favoured, no direct toxicity predicted.  
Digestive (GI & liver): Medium concern (hepatic metabolic stress possible; local GI irritation).  
Urinary: Low concern; no structural or read-across indication of renal injury.

Systemic availability (oral): High

5. Uncertainties and data gaps
• Absence of guideline-compatible repeat-dose studies (e.g., OECD TG 408) in any species.  
• No measured ADME data (Caco-2, in-vivo TK) to validate absorption prediction.  
• Cardiovascular in-vitro assays (hERG, Langendorff) have not been performed.  
• Metabolite identification in rat is lacking; reactive intermediates only hypothesised.  

6. Recommendations
• Conduct an in-vitro hERG channel assay and an in-vitro micronucleus test to clarify cardiotoxic / genotoxic potential.  
• Perform a 28-day oral toxicity study in rat with clinical chemistry (focus on liver enzymes) and full histopathology of heart, liver, kidney, spleen and thymus.  
• Generate basic TK data (plasma AUC, clearance) to refine systemic exposure estimates.

7. Ethical note
Assessment is based on currently available public data and recognised predictive tools; no proprietary or confidential data were used.

References (representative)  
1. PubChem CID 150942, retrieved 15-Jun-2024.  
2. Maksimov, V. I. et al. Acute toxicity of heteroaromatic compounds. Russian Pharmaceutical Bulletin, 1990.  
3. Souza, M. et al. Anti-inflammatory activity of 1,3,4-thiadiazine derivatives. Bioorg. Med. Chem. Lett. 28, 2018.  
4. OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 and VEGA 1.2.3 predictions run 15-Jun-2024.  
5. ICH S7B Guideline on Non-clinical Evaluation of the Potential for Delayed Ventricular Repolarization.
