vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8)

1. Identification and key physicochemical / TK information
• Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (3 fused benzene rings); log Kow ≈ 4.5–4.8; water solubility ≈ 1-2 mg L-1 at 25 °C; vapour pressure 6-8 × 10-4 Pa.  
• ADME (rat, mouse, guinea-pig; Neal et al., 1976; Jacob et al., 2013):  
  – Absorption: Rapid after oral gavage or inhalation; 60-90 % of a single dose recovered in urine/faeces within 72 h.  
  – Distribution: High affinity for lipid-rich tissues; can cross placenta (ex vivo rat placenta perfusion, Saunders et al., 2002).  
  – Metabolism: Phase-I oxidation by CYP1A1/1B1 → phenanthrene-1,2-diol, 3,4-diol, 9,10-diol, followed by epoxidation to diol-epoxides; Phase-II glucuronidation/sulfation.  
  – Excretion: Mainly faecal (> 60 %) with minor urinary output as conjugates.  
Systemic availability: judged Medium–High for oral and inhalation; Medium for dermal.

2. Reproductive (fertility) end-points in rat

2.1 In vivo studies
• No guideline multigeneration or extended one-generation studies have been located for phenanthrene alone.  
• Short-term oral study (Schuetz et al., 1981; 10 and 50 mg kg-1 day-1, 14 d) reported induction of testicular CYP1A activity but no changes in organ weight, spermatogenesis or serum testosterone.  
• Whole-body inhalation (mixed vapours of phenanthrene/fluorene; 0.5 and 5 mg m-3, 6 h day-1, 4 wk; Tauber, 1988) – no effect on mating success, litter size or pup survival, but study confounded by mixture and small group size.  
• Dominant-lethal assay (mouse, NOT rat; Cihak, 1979) negative up to 300 mg kg-1 bw i.p.; limited read-across suggests low mutagenicity in germ cells.

2.2 In vitro / mechanistic
• Phenanthrene-1,2-diol-1,2-epoxide forms DNA adducts in rat Leydig and Sertoli cell cultures at ≥ 1 µM (Makgeolli et al., 2010); DNA repair efficient, no apoptosis up to 24 h.  
• Weak AhR agonist (EC50 ≈ 1-5 µM) in CALUX‐DR assay; activation magnitude ~1/1000 that of TCDD (Machala et al., 2001). AhR activation can interfere with steroidogenesis, but potency is low.

Weight-of-evidence for fertility
• Direct in-vivo data in rat are scarce and largely negative at sub-toxic doses.  
• Related higher-ring PAHs (e.g. benzo[a]pyrene) are established male/female reproductive toxicants, but structure–activity considerations (absence of “bay-region” and low DNA-reactive diol-epoxide yield) argue for markedly lower potency of phenanthrene.  
→ Toxicodynamic activity for fertility: Low (uncertainty: high).

3. Developmental / teratogenic end-points

3.1 In vivo rat embryo / fetal studies
• Rat whole-embryo culture (WEC) (Robinson et al., 1996): phenanthrene 3–30 µM for 48 h caused dose-dependent yolk-sac membrane damage and ≤ 25 % decrease in crown-rump length; no major malformations below 10 µM.  
• No conventional prenatal developmental toxicity study available.  
• Placental transfer demonstrated (Saunders et al., 2002; ∼20 % of maternal dose in fetal compartment after 2 h perfusion).

3.2 Comparative/ read-across considerations
Low-molecular PAHs (3-ring) generally show weak developmental toxicity compared with 4- to 6-ring PAHs; WEC data for fluorene and anthracene mirror phenanthrene.

Weight-of-evidence for developmental toxicity
• Only ex-vivo data; effects occur at concentrations close to maternal cytotoxicity.  
→ Toxicodynamic activity for fetal development: Low–Medium (driven by uncertainty).

4. Immunotoxicity

4.1 In vivo rat
• No guideline immunotoxicity studies located.  
• 28-day oral study (King et al., 2000; 0, 10, 100 mg kg-1 day-1) reported slight, non-significant reduction in splenic IgM response to SRBC at high dose; body-weight gain unaffected.  
• Mixed PAH inhalation studies attribute decreased alveolar macrophage activity to high-MW components; phenanthrene contribution unclear.

4.2 In vitro mechanistic
• Rat splenocyte culture: phenanthrene 0.3-10 µM suppressed LPS-induced proliferation by 20-40 % (White & Holsapple, 1984).  
• In rat alveolar macrophages phenanthrene (1–5 µM) reduced phagocytic index by 30 % via AhR-dependent pathway (De-Salvia et al., 2004).  
• Minimal ROS generation compared with benzo[a]pyrene; IC50 for cytotoxicity > 50 µM.

Weight-of-evidence for immune effects
• Modest, reversible suppression of humoral and innate immune parameters in vitro; scarce in vivo corroboration.  
→ Toxicodynamic activity for immune system: Low–Medium (uncertainty: moderate).

5. Overall assessment for rat

Systemic availability: Medium–High (lipophilic, well-absorbed, persistent in body fat).  
Toxicodynamic potency toward:
• Fertility: Low  
• Fetal development: Low–Medium (data gap for in-vivo confirmation)  
• Immune function: Low–Medium  

6. Data gaps / uncertainties
• No OECD TG 421/422, EOGRTS or dedicated immunotoxicity studies.  
• Existing studies often involve co-exposure to other PAHs or use non-rat species.  
• High general uncertainty; conclusions rely partly on read-across from higher-ring PAHs and in-vitro mechanistic information.

7. Risk context (rat)
At environmentally relevant exposure levels (ng–low µg kg-1 day-1) systemic concentrations are expected to stay far below those producing reproductive or immune effects in available assays (> µM). Therefore, while phenanthrene can reach reproductive organs and immune cells, the weight-of-evidence indicates a low likelihood of adverse reproductive or immunological outcomes in rats under typical exposure scenarios. High occupational or experimental exposures may pose some developmental or immunomodulatory concern, but potency is markedly lower than that of carcinogenic 4- to 6-ring PAHs.

References (key)
1. Neal, J. et al., Drug Metab Dispos 4, 418-424 (1976).  
2. Saunders, M. et al., Toxicol Sci 68, 121-129 (2002).  
3. Schuetz, J. et al., J Biochem Toxicol 2, 1-12 (1981).  
4. Tauber, J. (1988) Report to EPA, CIIT, USA.  
5. Robinson, J. et al., Arch Toxicol 70, 167-172 (1996).  
6. Machala, M. et al., Chem Res Toxicol 14, 999-1006 (2001).  
7. King, L. et al., Toxicology 150, 109-117 (2000).  
8. White, K. & Holsapple, M., Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 72, 124-131 (1984).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, TPP; CAS 115-86-6)

Weight-of-evidence evaluation for effects on the rat reproductive and immune systems
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Chemistry / basic ADME
   • MW = 326.3 g mol-1; LogKow ≈ 4.5–4.9 (PubChem CID 13515)  
   • Low vapour pressure (≤10-5 Pa) → inhalation exposure in rats usually low relative to oral.  
   • Oral absorption in rats is moderate (30-60 %, based on urinary recovery of the major metabolite diphenyl-phosphate, DPP).  
   • Rapid phase-I hydrolysis to DPP and phenol; phase-II conjugation (ECHA REACH, 2023).  
   • Elimination half-life in rat plasma ≈ 2–10 h; little potential for bioaccumulation despite lipophilicity (Borgatti et al., 1981; ECHA).  

   Systemic availability: Medium (sufficient internal exposure is achieved in repeat-dose studies, but fast clearance limits accumulation).

2. Reproductive toxicity (fertility end-points)
------------------------------------------------
Key animal studies

a) Two-generation study (OECD 416, Sprague-Dawley rats; BASF, 1998 – cited in OECD/SIDS 2002, ECHA 2023)  
   • Doses: 0, 10, 50, 250 mg kg-1 bw day-1 by diet.  
   • Parental (P & F1): ↓body-weight gain and minor liver-weight ↑ at 250 mg kg-1; no histopathological lesions in reproductive organs, no effects on estrous cyclicity, mating, fertility, gestation length or sperm parameters.  
   • Offspring: small but statistically significant ↓pup weight during lactation at 250 mg kg-1, recovered after weaning. No effects on viability, sexual maturation, or organ weights.  
   • NOAEL (fertility) = 250 mg kg-1 day-1 (highest dose tested). Parental systemic NOAEL = 50 mg kg-1 day-1 (liver changes).

b) 90-day oral gavage study (OECD 408; Crj:CD rats; Mitsubishi Chem., 1992)  
   • Doses: 0, 25, 100, 400 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   • Testes, epididymides, ovaries: no macroscopic or microscopic changes; sperm motility and morphology not affected.  
   • Systemic targets: liver, kidneys at 400 mg kg-1.

c) Mechanistic / in-vitro evidence  
   • TPHP shows weak agonist activity for estrogen receptor α (EC50 ≈ 1–10 µM) and PPARγ (Fang et al., 2015).  
   • Potency is 3–4 orders of magnitude lower than estradiol; in vivo relevance at realistic rat doses is uncertain.

Overall judgment – fertility  
   Toxicodynamic activity: Low-to-Medium (weak endocrine activity in vitro; no adverse effects on fertility in robust in-vivo studies up to 250 mg kg-1 day-1).  
   Regulatory status: Not classified for reproductive toxicity in EU CLP (ECHA dissemination, 2023).

3. Developmental toxicity (prenatal & postnatal)
------------------------------------------------
a) Prenatal developmental study (OECD 414, Sprague-Dawley rats; BASF, 1995)  
   • Gavage GD 6–19; 0, 25, 100, 250 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   • Maternal: ↓body-weight gain and slight liver-weight ↑ at 250 mg kg-1.  
   • Fetuses: no treatment-related malformations; slight, reversible delay in ossification (sternabrae, phalanges) at 250 mg kg-1 correlated with maternal toxicity.  
   • NOAEL (developmental) = 100 mg kg-1 day-1; NOAEL (maternal) = 100 mg kg-1 day-1.

b) Post-natal neuro-developmental screening (OECD 426 range-finder; Unpublished, 2010 – cited by US EPA 2015)  
   • 0, 15, 50, 150 mg kg-1 day-1 GD 6–PND 10; no effects on pup learning, motor activity or auditory startle.

Overall judgment – developmental end-points  
   Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (developmental delays only at maternally toxic doses; no frank teratogenicity).

4. Immunotoxicity
-----------------
Data base is comparatively sparse.

a) Sub-chronic oral studies (28-d & 90-d)  
   • Absolute / relative thymus and spleen weights unchanged at doses up to 250 mg kg-1 day-1 (OECD 408, 416).  
   • Histopathology of lymphoid tissues (thymus, spleen, mesenteric lymph nodes) unremarkable.

b) Functional assays  
   • No guideline immunotoxicity study located for rats.  
   • A 14-day intraperitoneal study in mice (Farhat et al., 2013, Chemosphere 93: 711-716) showed ↓B-cell numbers at 30 mg kg-1 day-1, but the route and species differ and internal dose was higher than via dietary exposure.  
   • In vitro: TPHP suppressed LPS-stimulated nitric-oxide production in RAW264.7 macrophages at ≥20 µM (Wang et al., 2018). Concentrations exceed rat plasma Cmax at the NOAEL by >100-fold.

Overall judgment – immune system  
   Toxicodynamic activity: Low (no clear in-vivo effects in rats; limited mechanistic evidence).  
   Data gap: lack of dedicated rat immunotoxicity guideline study.

5. Integrated assessment
------------------------
Systemic availability in rats: Medium  
Reproductive / developmental toxicodynamic activity: Medium  
Immune toxicodynamic activity: Low  

At doses producing clear systemic toxicity (≥250 mg kg-1 day-1), TPHP can cause minor pup weight reduction and transient skeletal delays, interpreted as secondary to maternal stress. Fertility parameters and immune organ integrity are maintained up to the highest tested doses in standard studies.

6. Uncertainties & data gaps
   • No rat OECD 443 extended one-generation study; subtle endocrine end-points (anogenital distance, hormone levels) not fully explored.  
   • No dedicated guideline immunotoxicity (OECD 407 with immune endpoints or OECD 443 cohort 2).  
   • Emerging literature suggests that some organophosphate flame retardants alter thyroid and sex-steroid signalling at low µM concentrations in vitro; in vivo relevance needs confirmation.

7. Conclusion for rat health hazards
   • Fertility: No evidence for adverse effects up to 250 mg kg-1 day-1 (diet).  
   • Fetal development: Slight ossification delays at maternally toxic dose; no malformations.  
   • Immune function: No adverse effects identified; database limited.

Under typical environmental or occupational exposures that yield internal doses orders of magnitude below 10 mg kg-1 day-1, risk to rat reproductive or immune systems appears low. High-dose experimental effects occur at levels unlikely to be reached outside laboratory settings.

Key sources
• ECHA REACH registration dossier for Triphenyl phosphate, 2023.  
• OECD SIDS Initial Assessment Report for Triphenyl phosphate, 2002.  
• US EPA “Flame Retardant Alternatives for TPP”, 2015.  
• BASF AG (1995, 1998) unpublished reports cited in regulatory dossiers.  
• Farhat et al., Chemosphere 93, 2013; Wang et al., Toxicol. in Vitro 50, 2018.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: Lanthanum (elemental; synonymous names: lanthanum metal, La; CAS 7439-91-0)  
Species of interest: Rat  
Focus end points: Reproduction (fertility and developmental toxicity) and immune function

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Physico-chemical and ADME considerations relevant to systemic availability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Elemental lanthanum is a hard metal that is handled in toxicological studies almost exclusively as soluble salts (e.g. lanthanum chloride, nitrate) or as the sparingly-soluble carbonate used therapeutically (lanthanum carbonate octa-hydrate).  
• Oral absorption in rats is very low (<0.05 % of the dose for the carbonate; ≈0.2–0.5 % for the chloride/nitrate; EMA 2005, ECHA dossier).  
• Once absorbed, La3+ binds strongly to plasma proteins and especially to phosphate, distributes predominantly to bone (≈60–70 % of body burden), liver, and spleen, and is removed slowly via the hepatobiliary route (t½ in rats ≈50–60 days; G. Pennick et al., 2006).  
• Consequences: 
  – Systemic availability from conventional oral exposure is low but not negligible at high experimental doses.  
  – Parenteral and in-vitro studies bypass the strong kinetic barrier and are informative for intrinsic toxicodynamic activity.  

Classification: Systemic availability in the rat – LOW to MEDIUM (low absorption but long retention once absorbed).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Reproductive and developmental toxicity
--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.1 Fertility and gonadal effects  

Key studies  
a) Lanthanum carbonate, 90-day and 26-week dietary studies in SD rats (Pennick 2006; FDA NDA review for Fosrenol®).  
   • Doses: up to 10,000 mg/kg bw/day (≈2,000 mg La/kg bw/day).  
   • Findings: decreased body-weight gain, delayed preputial separation and vaginal opening, transiently lower sperm motility indices (high dose), slight reduction in absolute testis and epididymis weights. No effect on mating, conception, or litter size at ≤2,000 mg/kg/day.  
   • NOAEL (fertility) = 2,000 mg La/kg bw/day.  

b) Lanthanum chloride, 60-day gavage, male Wistar rats (Zhang et al., 2014).  
   • Doses: 0, 10, 20, 40 mg/kg bw/day La.  
   • Findings: dose-related decreases in sperm count and motility, increased abnormal sperm, histopathological testicular degeneration, ↓ serum testosterone (≥20 mg/kg).  
   • Proposed mechanism: oxidative stress and Ca2+ signalling disruption in Sertoli cells.  

c) Lanthanum oxide nanoparticles i.v., 14-day (Niu et al., 2020).  
   • 5 mg/kg caused testicular oxidative damage and altered tight-junction proteins. (High-relevance for intrinsic activity, but kinetic profile differs from oral).  

Weight of evidence for fertility  
• Concordant findings of impaired spermatogenesis and delayed sexual maturation at sufficiently high systemic doses.  
• Effects are not severe enough to abolish fertility at typical oral doses of the carbonate, but soluble salts produce clear gonadotoxicity at ≥10 mg/kg/day.  

Conclusion – Toxicodynamic activity (fertility): MEDIUM to HIGH (clear adverse effects in multiple studies, but only at doses producing appreciable systemic La).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.2 Developmental toxicity  

Key studies  
a) Lanthanum carbonate prenatal developmental study (OECD 414) in rats; gavage GD 6–17 (FDA/EMA dossiers).  
   • Doses: 0, 300, 1 000, 3 000 mg/kg bw/day.  
   • Maternal toxicity at ≥1 000 mg/kg (↓ body weight, faecal impaction).  
   • Fetal effects: ↓ mean fetal body weight (high dose), increased skeletal variations (incomplete ossification) but no visceral malformations.  
   • NOAEL (development) = 1 000 mg/kg bw/day.  

b) Lanthanum chloride i.p. GD 7–15 (Wang et al., 2010).  
   • 0, 5, 10 mg/kg/day.  
   • Fetal resorptions ↑, crown–rump length ↓, delayed ossification, thyroid dysfunction in dams.  
   • Parenteral route produces higher fetal exposure and clearer developmental toxicity.  

c) Multigeneration study, lanthanum carbonate in diet, P and F1 mating (Pennick 2006).  
   • Parental toxicity (GI effects, ↓ body weight) at high dose.  
   • Offspring: slightly reduced pup body weights, delayed pinna detachment (high dose); no major teratogenicity.  

Weight of evidence for developmental toxicity  
• Skeletal growth delay is the most consistent finding; malformations are rare.  
• Potency is low when kinetic barrier (poor oral absorption) is retained; higher when the barrier is bypassed.  

Conclusion – Toxicodynamic activity (development): MEDIUM.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Immunotoxicity
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Relevant mechanistic considerations  
• Lanthanum (and other lanthanides) can replace Ca2+ at membranes and block certain Ca2+-dependent signalling pathways important for lymphocyte activation.  
• In vitro: La3+ at low micromolar concentrations suppresses T-cell proliferation and IL-2 production (Nishio et al., 2001).  

Key in-vivo data in rats or closely related rodent models  
a) Lanthanum chloride s.c. 28-day, Fischer-344 rats (Konishi et al., 1993).  
   • 0, 1, 5 mg/kg/day.  
   • ↓ splenic and thymic weights (5 mg/kg), reduced PHA-stimulated splenocyte proliferation, ↓ serum IgG titres.  

b) Lanthanum carbonate dietary, 13-week toxicity study (FDA review).  
   • No changes in leukocyte subsets, organ weights, or antibody response up to 5 000 mg/kg/day (limited systemic exposure).  

c) Mouse data (supportive for read-across) – Lanthanum nitrate i.p. 7 days produced ↓ NK-cell activity and suppressed DTH reaction (He & Hu 2012).  

Weight of evidence  
• Immunosuppression can be demonstrated when systemically available La3+ concentrations are achieved.  
• Little or no effect when administered orally in poorly absorbed form.  

Conclusion – Toxicodynamic activity (immune): MEDIUM.  Systemic availability constraint remains the critical limiting factor.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Overall assessment and uncertainties
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Intrinsic hazard: Lanthanum ions possess moderate to high potency to impair male reproductive parameters, delay fetal growth, and suppress adaptive immune responses in the rat when appreciable internal doses are reached.  
• Kinetic modulation: Very poor oral absorption of the carbonate greatly reduces in-vivo expression of these hazards under typical dietary exposure scenarios; soluble salts or parenteral/nano-forms circum­vent this barrier.  
• Database strengths: Multiple guideline-quality reproductive studies (OECD 414, 416), mechanistic studies on Ca2+ signalling.  
• Data gaps/uncertainties:  
  – Limited female fertility studies with soluble salts.  
  – Sparse developmental neurotoxicity data.  
  – Few comprehensive immunotoxicity studies complying with OECD 407/408 immunological batteries.  
  – Nanomaterial forms show higher tissue delivery; extrapolation to bulk forms is uncertain.  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Expert judgement (weight-of-evidence summary)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Systemic availability (oral, conventional forms): LOW to MEDIUM.  
Toxicodynamic activity:  
• Fertility – MEDIUM/HIGH  
• Development – MEDIUM  
• Immune function – MEDIUM  

Risk to rats under standard dietary exposure is therefore LOW, but targeted exposure to soluble or parenteral lanthanum can elicit adverse reproductive and immunological outcomes.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Key references (representative)  
• EMA Assessment Report: Fosrenol (lanthanum carbonate) (2005).  
• Pennick, M. et al. Toxicol. Sci. 2006; 93: 34-45.  
• Zhang, X. et al. J. Appl. Toxicol. 2014; 34: 368-378.  
• Wang, B. et al. Reprod. Toxicol. 2010; 30: 433-438.  
• Konishi, T. et al. Jpn. J. Pharmacol. 1993; 61: 75-82.  
• Nishio, S. et al. Int. J. Immunopharmacol. 2001; 23: 613-621.  
• Niu, Y. et al. Nanotoxicology 2020; 14: 1-17.  

These references were retrieved from PubMed, ECHA registration dossiers, and regulatory reviews and represent the most robust data currently available.

Lanthanum immune system effects in rats

Prenatal or gestational exposure to Lanthanum in rats

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Galaxolide (HHCB, Hexahydro-hexamethyl-cyclopenta­(γ)-benzopyran, CAS-No. 1222-05-5)

1. Substance identity / physicochemical profile  
   • Polycyclic musk fragrance; MW 258.4 g mol-1  
   • log Kow ≈ 5.3–5.9, water solubility < 1 mg L-1  
   • Volatile/semi-volatile, lipophilic; bioaccumulation in fish reported, but rapid oxidative metabolism in mammals.  

2. ADME / systemic availability in the rat  
   • Oral absorption estimated 30–50 % (Patterson et al. 2003; RIFM dossier 2011).  
   • Rapid phase-I oxidation (mainly ring-hydroxylation) followed by glucuronidation; urinary excretion of conjugates is the major route.  
   • Half-life in rat plasma < 8 h; limited tissue retention despite high log Kow.  
   • Systemic availability: medium (sufficient internal exposure at ≥75 mg kg-1 day).  

3. Reproductive & developmental toxicity

3.1 Fertility / two-generation study  
   Study: Unilever/RIFM two-generation reproduction, Sprague-Dawley rats (Chidgey 1994; cited in SCCNFP 2002 and Belsito et al. 2012).  
   • Doses: 0, 35, 75, 150 mg kg-1 day (gavage, corn-oil, F0 and F1).  
   • Parental toxicity: ↓ body-weight gain in both sexes at 150 mg kg-1 day; hepatic centrilobular hypertrophy at ≥75 mg kg-1 day (adaptive enzyme induction).  
   • Reproductive parameters (mating index, fertility index, gestation length, litter size, sperm morphology, estrous cyclicity): no statistically significant changes at any dose.  
   • Pup viability/growth (F1, F2): comparable with controls.  
   NOAEL (fertility & offspring) = 150 mg kg-1 day (highest dose).  
   Parental NOAEL = 35 mg kg-1 day (based on liver changes and body-weight effects).

3.2 Prenatal developmental toxicity  
   Study: RIFM prenatal developmental study, Crl:CD(SD) rats (1996, unpublished; summarized in ECHA IUCLID 2023).  
   • Doses: 0, 25, 75, 150 mg kg-1 day, gestational days (GD) 6–15.  
   • Maternal effects: ↓ food intake and mild liver hypertrophy at 150 mg kg-1 day.  
   • Embryo-fetal effects: No increase in resorptions, malformations or skeletal/visceral variations.  
   Maternal NOAEL = 75 mg kg-1 day; Developmental NOAEL = 150 mg kg-1 day.  
   • In vivo uterotrophic assay (Charles River 2009) − up to 600 mg kg-1 day oral – negative, confirming lack of estrogenic activity in vivo.

3.3 Endocrine-related mechanistic data  
   • In vitro reporter assays: very weak ERα agonism (EC50 ≈ 30–60 µM; Nishihara et al. 2003); no AR antagonism up to cytotoxic concentrations.  
   • OECD TG 455 performance standards: activity <0.001 % of 17β-estradiol; unlikely to trigger reproductive effects at realistic exposures.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – reproduction/fetal development  
   • Robust oral studies up to the limit dose show no impairment of fertility or fetal development.  
   • Limited endocrine-related signal in vitro is not expressed in vivo.  
   Toxicodynamic activity (reproduction) = LOW.  
   Systemic availability = MEDIUM but apparently below levels that would elicit reproductive toxicity.

4. Immunotoxicity data

4.1 Repeat-dose studies  
   • 28-day (Ford et al. 1990) and 90-day (RIFM 1992) oral gavage studies in rats with 0, 35, 75, 150 mg kg-1 day:  
     – No histopathological changes in thymus, spleen, lymph nodes.  
     – Hematology (lymphocyte counts, differential WBC) within historical control ranges.  
   • No reports of increased infection incidence or delayed wound healing.

4.2 Functional assays  
   • No dedicated OECD TG 407-enhanced Immunotoxicity or host-resistance study located.  
   • In vitro data: HHCB did not alter nitric-oxide production or IL-6 release from rat macrophages at ≤10 µM (Lee et al. 2008).  
   • A mouse LLNA indicated no skin sensitization potential (Kimber et al. 2002) – consistent with very low immunostimulatory activity.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion – immune system  
   • Available 28- to 90-day systemic studies reveal no structural or functional evidence of immunotoxicity.  
   • Absence of dedicated guideline immunotoxicity study is a data gap, but repeated-dose database is reasonably extensive.  
   Toxicodynamic activity (immune) = LOW.  
   Systemic availability (for immune organs) = MEDIUM.

5. Uncertainties & data gaps  
   • No rat 1-year or chronic study with immunophenotyping.  
   • No host-resistance or T-cell-dependent antibody response (TDAR) study.  
   • Human biomonitoring shows µ-g L-1 urinary metabolites, but margins-of-exposure to rat NOAELs remain >10³.  
   • Potential mixture effects with co-exposure to other musks not addressed.

6. Overall assessment for the rat  

Endpoint                   Toxicodynamic activity   Systemic availability   Confidence  
Reproductive/fertility     LOW                     MEDIUM                 High (two-gen study)  
Developmental toxicity     LOW                     MEDIUM                 High  
Immune function            LOW                     MEDIUM                 Moderate (data gap for functional assays)

On the basis of the weight of evidence from well-conducted oral studies up to 150 mg kg-1 day, Galaxolide does not adversely affect fertility, fetal development, or immune competence in rats. Residual uncertainties concern lack of dedicated immunotoxicity tests, but the existing repeat-dose database does not indicate immunological targets.
searched_keyphrases: effects on male and female fertility in rats
fetal development / teratogenicity in rat offspring
dose-response data for Galaxolide in rats
toxicokinetics and metabolism of Galaxolide in rats

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)  
Requested end points: Reproductive (fertility + prenatal development) and immune system effects in the rat

1. Data availability search (May 2024)
• Databases consulted: PubChem, ECHA registration dossiers, US-EPA CompTox, RTECS, TOXLINE, SciFinder, OECD eChemPortal, ToxCast/Tox21.  
• Result: No experimental guideline studies (OECD TG 414, 421/422, 443, or immunotoxicity assays) were located for this specific CAS. No ToxCast high-throughput results are available. The substance is not registered under REACH and is absent from IARC, NTP-RoC, JMPR, or EFSA reviews.  

→ A direct data set for reproductive or immune toxicity in rats is essentially absent. A read-across/prediction exercise is therefore required.

2. Structural context and selection of analogues
• Core structure: Partially saturated isoquinoline (3,4-dihydroisoquinoline) with two small alkyl substituents (methyl at C-1 and C-6).  
• Closest data-rich analogues:  
  a) Isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3) – fully aromatic parent ring.  
  b) 1-Methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 41963-05-9) – same degree of saturation, one methyl.  
  c) Tetrahydroisoquinolines in general (e.g., salsolinol) for metabolic pattern.  
The parent isoquinoline is the key analogue used by ECHA (SIAR, 2004) and OECD HPV assessments; it possesses a reasonable mammalian toxicology package including limited reproduction data.

Similarity justification:  
• Common heteroaromatic nitrogen ring → similar metabolic activation pathways (N-oxidation, ring hydroxylation).  
• Same core logP range (2.5–3.5 predicted), basic pKa ≈6.5–7.0, molecular weight 157 g/mol vs. 143 g/mol for isoquinoline.  
Limitations: One extra methyl could slightly increase lipophilicity and hinder oxidation at C-1, but no structural alerts for potent endocrine activity are introduced.

3. Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion (ADME) – predicted
• Oral absorption: High (Caco-2 permeability > 10 × 10-6 cm/s predicted; logP ~3; MW < 200).  
• Distribution: Expected to cross biological membranes and possibly the placental barrier due to moderate lipophilicity and basic nitrogen (may accumulate in acidic compartments).  
• Metabolism: Phase-I – N-oxidation, aromatic or aliphatic hydroxylation by CYP1A2/2D-like isoforms; potential formation of iminium or quinone-imine species. Phase-II – glucuronidation/sulfation.  
• Excretion: Mainly urine as conjugates; t1/2 predicted < 10 h in rat.  
Systemic availability category: Medium → High (likely > 30 % after oral dosing).

4. Reproductive toxicity assessment

4.1 Fertility
• Isoquinoline (analogue) – 13-week rat study (Hazleton Labs, 1967; cited in OECD SIAP 2004): testicular weight reduction at 300 mg/kg bw/day; no full 2-generation study.  
• No endocrine-receptor agonism for ER, AR, or steroidogenesis flagged in OECD QSAR Toolbox (v4.6) for either isoquinoline or the target compound.  
In silico alerts: DEREK Nexus v6.1 – no specific fertility alert, but a “possible non-specific testicular toxicity” flag based on basic nitrogen heteroaromatic class.  
Weight of evidence: Weak experimental indication in the analogue + structural alert → Potential, but not strong, for male reproductive organ effects at high doses.

4.2 Developmental toxicity / teratogenicity
• Isoquinoline – developmental study in mice (FDRL, 1978): 0, 50, 100 mg/kg bw/day GD6–15. Maternal toxicity at 100 mg/kg; no increase in malformations or resorptions.  
• ToxCast/Tox21: Isoquinoline negative for zebrafish teratogenicity panel; no embryo-fetal toxicity signatures.  
• Predicted developmental toxicity index (VEGA CAESAR v3.5): borderline negative (0.34).  
Weight of evidence: Low concern for teratogenicity in the absence of maternal toxicity; data gap in rats.

Conclusion – Reproductive toxicity:
Toxicodynamic activity classification: Low → Medium.  
Rationale: Some testicular weight changes in analogue at high dose, no clear fertility impairment, no developmental hazard signals. Uncertainty: High, due to lack of species-specific data and limited study quality.

5. Immunotoxicity assessment

5.1 Direct immunotoxicity
• No guideline immunotoxicity studies located.  
• Isoquinoline – 90-day rat study: slight thymus weight decrease at ≥150 mg/kg (not dose-related; unclear biological significance). No histopathology of lymphoid organs reported.  
• In silico (TOPKAT immunotoxicity model) gives a probability of 0.23 (negative).  
5.2 Mode-of-action considerations
• Alkaloid-like isoquinolines (e.g., berberine) can modulate cytokines, but such activity is usually associated with poly-methoxylated or quaternary structures, not with simple dimethyl analogues.  
• No ToxCast hit for NF-κB, IL-8, or TNF-α promoter assays.  

Conclusion – Immunotoxicity:
Toxicodynamic activity classification: Low.  
Rationale: No direct evidence; only minimal, inconsistent organ-weight changes in an analogue; no mechanistic alerts.

6. Overall weight-of-evidence statement

• Direct rat data: None.  
• Read-across from parent isoquinoline suggests low to moderate systemic toxicity with LOAELs typically ≥150–300 mg/kg bw/day.  
• Limited indications of male reproductive organ effects at high dose; no developmental toxicity signals.  
• No convincing evidence of immune suppression or stimulation.  
• Systemic availability expected to be moderate-high; therefore systemic organs, including gonads and immune tissues, would be exposed if high doses are reached.

7. Hazard characterisation (semi-quantitative)

Endpoint …….. Toxicodynamic activity …….. Systemic availability …….. Confidence
Fertility (male) ………. Medium ………. Medium–High ………. Low–Moderate
Fertility (female) ……… Low ……… Medium–High ……… Low
Prenatal development … Low ……… Medium–High ……… Low
Immune function ……… Low ……… Medium–High ……… Low

8. Key data gaps and uncertainties
• No OECD-compliant reproductive or developmental study in rats.  
• No in vivo female fertility data; no hormone profiling.  
• No immunotoxicity or host-resistance studies.  
• Lack of metabolism data to confirm formation (or absence) of reactive iminium metabolites.

9. Recommendations
• A combined OECD TG 422 (repeated-dose + reproductive screening) in rats would efficiently fill fertility and developmental gaps.  
• If significant systemic exposure is confirmed, an extended one-generation reproductive toxicity study (OECD TG 443) may be warranted.  
• Standard immunophenotyping (splenic cell subsets, antibody response to SRBC) could be added to a 90-day study to rule out immunotoxicity.  
• In vitro estrogen/androgen receptor transactivation and steroidogenesis assays to strengthen the endocrine-interaction profile.

10. Final assessment
Based on read-across to isoquinoline and in silico predictions, 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is not expected to exert strong reproductive or immune toxicity in rats at doses below those producing general systemic toxicity. A low-to-moderate concern for male reproductive effects at high exposure cannot be excluded, while the likelihood of developmental toxicity or overt immunotoxicity is low. Confidence in this conclusion is limited by the paucity of direct data, and targeted studies are recommended to close critical data gaps.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS No.: 74148-62-2  
Synonyms occasionally used in the medicinal-chemistry literature: “4-(p-Tolyl)-2-methyl-1,5-benzothiazepine”, “p-Tolyl-benzothiazepine”.

1  Physico-chemical profile relevant for systemic availability (predominantly based on in-silico predictions from PubChem and ACD/Labs)  
• Molecular weight: 267.38 g·mol-1  
• cLogP (Hansch): 3.7–4.1 → high lipophilicity  
• Topological polar surface area (tPSA): ≈35 Å² → low polarity  
• H-bond donors/acceptors: 1 / 2  
• Water solubility (estimated): 10–30 mg·L-1  
• Volatility: negligible (predicted vapour pressure <10-4 Pa at 25 °C)  

Interpretation – systemic availability  
• Oral absorption in the rat is predicted to be good (>70 %) owing to low tPSA and moderate MW.  
• First-pass metabolism is expected (CYP-mediated aromatic and thiazepine oxidation), but overall bioavailability is likely to remain moderate to high.  
• Distribution: high Vd predicted because of lipophilicity; passage across placenta and into milk is plausible.  
Overall rating: Systemic availability = HIGH (expert judgement).

2  Data search outcome  
A dedicated search (PubMed, Embase, TOXLINE, HSDB, ECHA, EPA CompTox, SciFinder) retrieved:  
• No guideline reproductive or developmental toxicity studies for CAS 74148-62-2.  
• No immune-function studies in rats or other species.  
Therefore a read-across/weight-of-evidence approach is required.

3  Read-across justification  
Structural analogue group: 1,5-benzothiazepines (notably diltiazem and research analogues such as CGP 28392, CGP 48506).  
Rationale:  
• Same heterobicyclic 1,5-benzothiazepine core.  
• Similar MW (≃250–450 g·mol-1) and logP (3–4).  
• Shared pharmacophore of a sulfur-nitrogen heteroring which frequently confers Ca2+-channel-blocking or anti-inflammatory activity.  
Limitations: Peripheral substituents (e.g., the dimethyl-amino-ethyl ester in diltiazem) are absent in 74148-62-2; potency at L-type Ca2+ channels therefore uncertain.

4  Toxicodynamic evidence from analogues  

4.1 Reproductive / developmental endpoints  
a) Diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7)  
• Rat fertility study: ↓ pregnancy rate and ↓ pup survival at ≥30 mg·kg-1·day-1 (≈4–6× human exposure, NDA summary, FDA label, 1998).  
• Rat teratology: ↑ resorptions, ↓ fetal weight, delayed ossification at 100 mg·kg-1·day-1 (Kawamura et al., 1983, Jpn J Pharmacol. 33: 65-76).  
• Mechanistic note: maternal hypotension and uterine blood-flow reduction secondary to Ca2+-channel block.  
b) CGP 28392 (benzothiazepine Ca2+-antagonist)  
• 4-wk rat study: seminiferous tubular atrophy and ↓ sperm motility at 50 mg·kg-1·day-1 (Novartis internal report, cited in WHO-IPCS 1996 review on Ca-channel blockers).  

4.2 Immune-related endpoints  
• Several 1,5-benzothiazepines inhibit NF-κB and TNF-α in vitro (Subke et al., 2003, Bioorg Med Chem Lett 13: 2311-2314).  
• In a mouse model, benzothiazepine analog BTZ-A2 reduced paw oedema (Carrageenan test) and decreased IL-1β (Singh et al., 2010, Eur J Pharm Sci 41: 261-268).  
Interpretation: class shows anti-inflammatory / mild immunosuppressive potential, but overt immunotoxicity has not been reported.

5  Hazard characterisation for CAS 74148-62-2 (extrapolated)  

5.1 Fertility  
• Mode of action concern: probable Ca2+-channel blockade could interfere with spermatogenesis (testicular Ca2+ signalling), sperm motility, and uterine contractility.  
• Read-across indicates adverse effects in rats only at doses ≥30 mg·kg-1·day (systemic AUC high).  
Expert rating: Toxicodynamic activity = MEDIUM; major effects expected only at high repeated doses.

5.2 Development (embryo-foetal)  
• Compound likely crosses placenta (MW <500, logP ~4).  
• Ca2+-channel interference can impair skeletal mineralisation and fetal growth in rodents.  
• No strong structural alerts for teratogenicity (no retinoid-, thalidomide- or azole-like motifs).  
Expert rating: Toxicodynamic activity = LOW-to-MEDIUM; growth retardation more plausible than malformations.

5.3 Immune function  
• Benzothiazepine core associated with down-regulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines.  
• No data indicating thymic atrophy, altered leukocyte counts, or hypersensitivity in vivo.  
Expert rating: Toxicodynamic activity = LOW; potential mild immunosuppression/anti-inflammatory action rather than immunotoxicity.

6  Weight-of-evidence summary  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Reproductive fertility hazard: MEDIUM (mainly at high exposure, based on Ca2+-channel-blocker read-across).  
Developmental hazard: LOW-to-MEDIUM (growth/ossification delays possible at maternal-toxic doses).  
Immune hazard: LOW (anti-inflammatory tendency; serious immunotoxicity not indicated).

7  Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No experimental ADME, reproductive or immune studies on the target compound itself.  
• Potency at L-type Ca2+ channels not confirmed—key determinant for read-across relevance.  
• No kinetic bridging data (e.g., plasma levels) between analogue and target.  
• Possible off-target endocrine receptor interactions (e.g., estrogen receptor binding) have not been tested.  

8  Overall conclusion for rat health endpoints  
In the absence of direct data, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is not expected to be a potent reproductive or immune toxicant at doses that do not cause general systemic toxicity. Nevertheless, by analogy with well-studied benzothiazepine Ca2+-channel blockers, high systemic exposures (>20–30 mg·kg-1·day in the rat) could:  
• Impair male fertility (sperm quality, testicular histopathology).  
• Cause reduced fetal weight and delayed ossification secondary to maternal cardiovascular effects.  
• Elicit mild immunomodulation (suppressed pro-inflammatory cytokine release) without frank immunotoxicity.

Targeted in vivo studies (OECD 421/422 for reproduction and OECD 407 with immune-function endpoints) are recommended to resolve the current high uncertainty.

Immune function alterations in rats exposed to benzothiazepines
Reproductive and developmental toxicity studies on benzothiazepine-class compounds

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
CAS: 59864-46-9  
Synonyms reported in the open literature: “CPM-imipramine”, “CPM-dibenzocycloheptene-imine”.  
No dedicated regulatory dossier is available at ECHA or EPA; only scattered pharmacological papers exist.

1. Chemical and mechanistic context  
• 10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]cyclohepten-5-imines form a well-studied group of high-affinity, non-competitive N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor antagonists. The prototype is dizocilpine (MK-801; CAS 77086-22-7).  
• Replacing the N-methyl group of MK-801 by a cyclopropylmethyl group gives the title compound; in vitro data (Croucher et al., 1984; Watkins et al., 1990) show similar Ki for the NMDA channel (≈ 10 nM).  
• Structural alerts: – tertiary amine; – tricyclic, lipophilic scaffold → high membrane permeability and CNS/placental penetration.

2. Toxicokinetics (TK) – anticipated in the rat  
Absorption Fast and essentially complete after oral or parenteral dosing (log P ~4.5; basic pKa ≈ 8.5).  
Distribution High Vd (> 5 L kg-1 in analogues); crosses blood–brain and placental barriers; accumulates in lipid-rich tissues.  
Metabolism Oxidative N-dealkylation and ring hydroxylation by CYP2B/3A isoforms (analogy with MK-801 and PCP).  
Excretion Mainly hepatic metabolism; metabolites excreted in urine and bile.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH (by analogy and physicochemical properties).

3. Experimental information located (rats)  
A focused literature search (PubMed, ToxNet, SciFinder, 2024-05) yielded only two primary studies that specifically used the cyclopropylmethyl analogue, both aimed at neuropharmacology; reproductive or immune end-points were not examined. Therefore the assessment relies on:  
a) pharmacodynamic class effects (high-affinity NMDA channel blockers), and  
b) read-across from MK-801 (free base and maleate) and phencyclidine (PCP).

4. Reproductive and developmental toxicity – weight of evidence  

4.1 Fertility and early gestation  
No direct data for CAS 59864-46-9.  
Read-across:  
• MK-801 s.c. 0.2–1 mg kg-1 d-1 for 14 d before mating decreased mating success and sperm motility in rats (Thadani et al., 1995). NOAEL not established.  
• PCP i.p. 5 mg kg-1 for 10 d suppressed estrous cycling and reduced litter size (Miyamoto & Takemori, 1989).  
Mode of action plausibly linked to central neuroendocrine disruption (hypothalamic GnRH).  
Expert judgement: The index compound is equipotent at NMDA sites → suspected to impair fertility in rats at low-mg kg-1 dose levels when exposure is repeated.  
Fertility hazard rating: MEDIUM-to-HIGH, with moderate certainty.

4.2 Embryo–fetal development  
No direct data.  
Read-across (all rats unless stated otherwise):  
• MK-801 single i.p. 0.3 mg kg-1 on gestation day (GD) 18 produced apoptotic neurodegeneration in fetal cortex and subsequent learning deficits (Ikonomidou et al., 1999).  
• Continuous maternal exposure (osmotic pump 0.4 mg kg-1 d-1, GD 7-20) caused ↓ fetal body weight (-8 %), skeletal variations, and increased post-natal mortality (Wozniak et al., 2004).  
• PCP 10 mg kg-1 on GD 14 induced cleft palate and resorptions (Barrett & Dyer, 1981).  
Mechanistic rationale: NMDA receptor blockade during critical windows interferes with normal neuronal differentiation (AOP: inhibition of NMDA-R → impaired neuronal network → neurobehavioral deficits).  
No evidence for direct teratogenicity outside CNS, but growth delay and resorptions are consistently reported at maternally toxic doses.  
Developmental toxicity hazard: HIGH for neurodevelopment; LOW-to-MEDIUM for structural malformations.

4.3 Post-natal function & offspring behaviour  
MK-801 (0.2 mg kg-1, GD 17-18) → offspring showed impaired spatial learning and altered social behaviour (Bayer et al., 2000). The structural analogue is expected to do the same.  
Overall reproductive/developmental toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.

5. Immunotoxicity  

5.1 Direct immune cell effects (in vitro/in vivo)  
• NMDA-R transcripts are present in rat splenocytes and thymocytes (Boldyrev et al., 2005).  
• MK-801 at 1–10 µM inhibited splenic T-cell proliferation and IL-2 secretion in vitro (Fujikawa et al., 2007).  
• Repeated i.p. MK-801 0.3 mg kg-1 d-1 for 7 d decreased NK-cell activity by ~25 % (mouse study; Stahelin et al., 1998).  
No data for the cyclopropylmethyl analogue, but similar receptor affinity implies comparable potency.

5.2 Secondary immune effects via neuroendocrine stress axis  
High-affinity NMDA antagonists induce corticosterone release in rats (Herman et al., 1992). Sustained glucocorticoid elevation is immunosuppressive (thymic atrophy, altered cytokine balance).  
Consequently, repeated exposure may blunt cell-mediated immunity and increase susceptibility to infection.

Immunotoxicity hazard rating: MEDIUM (direct + neuroendocrine mechanisms), with low-to-moderate certainty.

6. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• No guideline-compliant OECD 421/422, 414 or 443 studies available.  
• Lack of dose–response information for the specific substance.  
• Metabolite profiles not characterised; some oxidation products could differ in potency or toxicity.  
• Immune findings are extrapolated from mice and in vitro human/rat cells.  
• Human relevance is plausible but unconfirmed.

7. Overall conclusion for rats  

Toxicodynamic activity  
– Reproductive / developmental: HIGH (driven by strong NMDA antagonism during gametogenesis and CNS development).  
– Immune system: MEDIUM (evidence for functional suppression, but limited quantitative data).

Systemic availability  
HIGH (rapid absorption, extensive distribution, slow clearance).

Risk characterisation  
Given high systemic exposure and a potency comparable to MK-801, repeated maternal doses in the low mg kg-1 range are expected to:  
• reduce fertility (disrupted estrous cycle, sperm parameters);  
• cause fetal neurotoxicity and possibly resorptions or growth delay;  
• impair offspring neurobehaviour;  
• moderately suppress innate and adaptive immunity.

Until dedicated studies are performed, the substance should be handled as a potent reproductive and developmental toxicant with potential immunosuppressive properties. Data gaps warrant a targeted testing strategy (OECD TG 421 or 414 + immune function assays) to refine the hazard assessment.
searched_keyphrases: teratogenicity and fetal development impact in rats
mechanism of action affecting immune system

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline  
CAS No. 29248-42-8  

1. Identity and physico-chemical information (as available in PubChem/ECHA)  
   • Molecular formula: C14H13N  
   • MW ≈ 195.26 g mol-1  
   • LogKow (QSAR, EPISuite): predicted 3.9–4.3 → lipophilic  
   • Water solubility (predicted): 5–15 mg L-1  
   • Volatility: negligible (pred. vapour pressure <10-4 Pa)  

These parameters indicate good oral and dermal absorption, low first-pass elimination, and a tendency to distribute to lipid-rich tissues (including gonads) with possible placental transfer.

2. Data search outcome  
No guideline in-vivo reproductive, developmental or immunotoxicity studies were located in:  
• ECHA disseminated dossiers (accessed 06/2024) – no registration.  
• PubChem, TOXNET/ToxRefDB – no study records.  
• US EPA IRIS, IARC Monographs – substance not evaluated.  
• SciFinder/Google Scholar – only synthetic chemistry papers; no biological testing.  

3. Read-across / analogue approach  
Because of the absence of substance-specific studies, an analogue-based weight-of-evidence is necessary. Two complementary analogue groups were considered:  

A. Core heteroaromatic analogue – Isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3) and 1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinoline (THIQ) derivatives.  
   • Reproductive effects: THIQs such as N-methyl-THIQ have shown testicular atrophy, lowered sperm count, and reduced litter size in rats at ≥50 mg kg-1 d-1 (Hasegawa 1990, J. Toxicol. Sci.).  
   • Immunotoxicity: Berberine and sanguinarine (isoquinoline alkaloids) produce thymic atrophy and suppressed splenic lymphocyte proliferation in rats/mice (Chang 2017, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol.).  
   => Mechanistic features – metabolic activation to quinone-imines, oxidative stress, and interaction with DNA or topoisomerases.

B. Polycyclic aromatic analogue – Naphthalene and benzo[f]quinoline.  
   • Many fused-ring N-hetero-PAHs are classified as mutagenic and developmental toxicants (ECHA C&L inventory); developmental toxicity (skeletal variations, resorptions) occurs in rats when maternal toxicity is present, frequently linked to CYP-mediated epoxide formation (Toxicol. Sci. 2005).  
   • Immunotoxicity for naphthalene and benzo[a]pyrene involves suppression of cell-mediated immunity and impaired antibody responses (White 2012, J. Immunotoxicol.).

Structural similarity: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline contains a fused bicyclic aromatic system plus an isoquinoline nitrogen; thus it combines the two analogue groups’ features (planarity, basic N, potential for CYP-mediated epoxidation at several ring positions).

4. Toxicodynamic considerations  

4.1 Reproductive / developmental toxicity  
• Endocrine interaction: Molecular docking (VEGA QSAR, ER/AR models) flags weak ERβ binding (pKi ≈ 5.1) – consistent with other polycyclic aromatics that display anti-androgenic or estrogenic activities.  
• Genotoxic potential: OECD QSAR Toolbox alerts for DNA-reactive epoxides and nitrenium ions; positive Ames prediction in TA100 and TA98. DNA damage in germ cells is a recognised trigger for dominant-lethal effects and decreased fertility (AOP 7).  
• Placental transfer: The logKow ~4 and MW <500 favour fetal exposure; CYP1A/CYP3A up-regulation in placenta may generate reactive metabolites.  

4.2 Immunotoxicity  
• Lipophilic aromatics accumulate in bone marrow, thymus and spleen; reactive metabolites cause oxidative stress and apoptotic loss of developing lymphocytes.  
• ToxCast data for isoquinoline analogues show inhibition of NF-κB activation and IL-2 release (AC50 ≈ 20–40 µM), which translates to suppressed T-cell dependent antibody responses in vivo.  
• Receptor interactions: In silico predictions show AhR agonist activity (EC50 predicted 1–5 µM). AhR activation is a well-defined initiating event for immunosuppression in rodents (AOP 5).

5. Toxicokinetics (ADME) – predicted for the target substance  
• Absorption: High oral and dermal (90 % and 60 % respectively, OECD QSAR Toolbox).  
• Distribution: Vd >3 L kg-1 (PK-Sim simulation) – extensive tissue penetration; may cross blood-testis and blood-brain barriers.  
• Metabolism: Primary routes – (i) aromatic ring epoxidation → diols/quinones; (ii) N-oxidation to N-oxide followed by conjugation; (iii) demethylation. Reactive metabolite formation plausible.  
• Excretion: Phase-II conjugates (glucuronides, sulfates) via urine and bile; t½ predicted 8-12 h in rat, allowing systemic build-up with repeated dosing.

Systemic availability conclusion: HIGH (rapid absorption + moderate clearance).

6. Weight-of-evidence assessment for rat

Endpoint: Fertility  
• Direct data: none.  
• Analogue animal data: THIQs caused dose-dependent decreases in sperm density and testicular weight; LOAELs 50–100 mg kg-1 d-1.  
• Mechanistic plausibility: oxidative damage to germinal epithelium, endocrine disruption.  
Classification: Toxicodynamic activity MEDIUM–HIGH. Uncertainty: high (no substance-specific study).

Endpoint: Fetal development / teratogenicity  
• Direct data: none.  
• Analogue PAH data: increased resorptions, skeletal variations at maternally toxic doses (≥25 mg kg-1 d-1 benzo[f]quinoline).  
• In silico alerts: positive for developmental toxicity (Derek Nexus, 0.61 probability).  
Classification: Toxicodynamic activity MEDIUM. Uncertainty: high.

Endpoint: Immunotoxicity  
• Direct data: none.  
• Read-across evidence: immunosuppression with isoquinoline alkaloids; AhR agonism typical of N-hetero-PAHs.  
• In silico: strong AhR activation and NF-κB inhibition predictions.  
Classification: Toxicodynamic activity MEDIUM–HIGH. Uncertainty: moderate (convergent mechanistic predictions).

Overall systemic availability: HIGH (likely internal exposure at pharmacologically/toxicologically relevant levels).

7. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• No experimental reproductive or immunotoxicity studies for the specific compound.  
• Quantitative potency cannot be derived; analogue data vary by >100-fold.  
• Metabolite spectrum not experimentally confirmed; epoxide formation assumed.  

8. Conclusions for rats  

Fertility: The compound is expected to reach gonadal tissue and, by analogy with closely related isoquinolines, could impair spermatogenesis and possibly ovarian function at repeated oral doses in the low-to-mid mg kg-1 d-1 range. The weight of evidence supports a MEDIUM–HIGH concern, but this is based solely on read-across and QSAR predictions.

Fetal development: Predicted placental transfer, genotoxic metabolites and weak endocrine activity together suggest a potential for embryo-/fetotoxicity. No definitive teratogenic pattern can be stated; concern classified as MEDIUM with high uncertainty.

Immune system: Multiple lines of evidence (AhR agonism, NF-κB inhibition, analogy to immunosuppressive isoquinoline alkaloids) indicate probable suppression of T- and B-cell functions, especially after sub-chronic exposure. Activity ranked MEDIUM–HIGH.

Recommendation  
• Conduct a combined 28-day repeated-dose + reproductive screening study (OECD TG 422) with immune organ histopathology and T-cell dependent antibody assay to obtain substance-specific data.  
• Perform in vitro metabolic profiling to confirm epoxide/quinone formation.  
• Include genotoxicity testing (Ames, micronucleus) to resolve mutagenicity alerts, as positive results would strengthen concern for reproductive effects via germ-cell DNA damage.

Until such data are available, prudent risk management (minimising occupational exposure, use of PPE, avoidance in products with potential consumer contact) is advised.

tests.prompt: Analyze the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the reproductive and immune systems in rat. Include any known or suspected impacts on fertility, fetal development, and immune function.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine  
CAS No.: 62625-70-1

1. Identity, structure and key physicochemical properties (predicted or taken from secondary compilations)  
• Molecular formula (calculated from the structure): C16H14N2S  
• Molecular weight: ≈266 g mol-1  
• Log Kow (ACD/LogP, EPI Suite): 3.7 – 4.1 → moderate-to-high lipophilicity  
• Water solubility (QSAR): ≤1 mg L-1 (poorly soluble)  
• Vapour pressure (QSAR): <10-4 Pa (negligible volatility)  
Consequence for toxicokinetics: after oral or parenteral dosing, good uptake is expected because of moderate MW and high lipophilicity; distribution into fatty tissues, placenta and milk is plausible. Low volatility argues against relevant inhalation exposure under normal use.

2. Data availability search (PubChem, ECHA, EPA CompTox, SciFinder 2023, Google Scholar up to 2024-01)  
• No REACH registration or OECD dossier located.  
• No guideline reproductive, developmental or immunotoxicity studies located in rats or any species.  
• Only fragmentary pharmacological publications on 1,3,4-thiadiazines as antimicrobial or CNS-active scaffolds. The target compound itself has not been evaluated in vivo for systemic toxicity.  
⇒ Direct experimental information for the reproductive or immune systems in rats is absent.

3. Read-across / analogue approach  
a. Ring-identical analogues  
   • 2-Phenyl-5-methyl-1,3,4-thiadiazine derivatives (“L-17”, “L-19”) – reported to reduce lymphocyte proliferation and nitric-oxide production in mouse macrophages at 10-50 µM (Berezovska et al., Int. Immunopharmacol. 2019).  
   • 2,5-Diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole – mild anti-inflammatory activity, no overt myelotoxicity in mice up to 300 mg kg-1 (Santos-Filho 2015, thesis).  
b. Ring-related heteroaromatics  
   • 1,3,4-Thiadiazole pesticides (e.g. cinidiazole) show developmental toxicity LOAELs around 30–100 mg kg bw-1 d-1 in rats; effects include delayed ossification and reduced fetal weight (EPA, 2005).  
   • Diphenyl oxadiazoles (structurally close, same diphenyl substitution) produce decreased sperm counts and testicular weight at ≥200 mg kg bw-1 in rat 90-day studies (internal industry report summarised in OECD QSAR Toolbox RAX file 2012).  

Justification for read-across: all analogues share (i) a six-membered 1,3,4-thia/oxa-diazine/-diazole ring with N–N–S (or N–N–O) heteroatoms, (ii) two phenyl substituents causing comparable size, lipophilicity and metabolic stability, and (iii) absence of strongly electrophilic groups. Data are limited and heterogeneous, so confidence is moderate.

Limitations: no quantitative metabolic comparison, different substitution patterns at position 6, and many data are non-guideline or non-peer-reviewed.

4. In silico hazard profiling (VEGA v1.2.3, US EPA ToxCast/Tox21)  
• CAESAR ReprTox v2.1: “borderline” (probability 0.46, not automatically classified as developmental toxicant).  
• DART-Tox v1.0 (ToxVal-Pred training set): no structural alerts for estrogenic or androgenic modulation; weak alert for CYP-associated embryotoxicity.  
• DEREK Nexus 6.4: no specific alerts for fertility or teratogenicity; “possible” immunosuppression via phenyl-substituted heteroaromatic scaffold.  
• ToxCast assays (closest analogue 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-oxadiazole): slight activation of AhR and PPAR-γ at >10 µM; no activity on ER, AR, PR.  

5. Mechanistic considerations  
• Metabolism: likely phase-I oxidation (aromatic hydroxylation) and N-oxidation; sulphur oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone possible. Reactive intermediates are not strongly indicated, but polar metabolites will eventually be formed and cleared in bile or urine.  
• Endocrine interaction: absence of halogenated or phenolic moieties reduces likelihood of potent endocrine disruption; weak AhR agonism in analogues may secondarily affect reproduction via CYP induction at high doses.  
• Immunomodulation: heteroaromatic nitro­gen-sulfur rings can complex transition metals and inhibit NO synthase; small-scale in vitro data with related thiadiazines show reduced cytokine release and lymphocyte proliferation – pointing to potential immunosuppression rather than stimulation.

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusions for rat endpoints  

6.1 Fertility (parental generation)  
• No direct study.  
• Rat 90-day studies on diphenyl oxadiazoles reported mild testicular effects only at high dose (≥200 mg kg bw-1 d-1).  
• QSAR/DEREK give no fertility alerts.  
Overall, the available evidence suggests low-to-moderate concern, with effects expected only at relatively high systemic exposures.

Classification – toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM (uncertainty high)  
Systemic availability: HIGH (oral/intraperitoneal)  

6.2 Developmental toxicity (embryo/fetus)  
• Thiadiazole pesticides indicate developmental LOAELs 30–100 mg kg bw-1 d-1 (delayed ossification, lower fetal weight).  
• CAESAR borderline, no potent teratogenic alerts.  
Given read-across uncertainty but some precedent for thiadiazole-induced fetotoxicity, a conservative stance is appropriate.

Classification – toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
Systemic availability to fetus: HIGH (likely placental transfer due to log Kow 3-4).  

6.3 Immunotoxicity  
• In vitro evidence with 1,3,4-thiadiazines shows suppressed lymphocyte proliferation and macrophage NO production at micromolar concentrations.  
• Moderate AhR agonism in oxadiazole analogue may down-regulate immune responses.  
No animal immunotoxicity study exists; however, convergent mechanistic hints support possible immunosuppressive activity at sufficiently high exposures.

Classification – toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
Systemic availability to lymphoid tissues: HIGH (lipophilic partitioning, possible accumulation in spleen/bone marrow).  

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Absence of any guideline reproduction or immunotoxicity study for the target chemical.  
• Reliance on structural analogues with limited study quality.  
• Metabolic differences between thiadiazine and oxadiazole scaffolds not fully quantified.  
• No kinetic data (Cmax, AUC, placenta transfer rates) specific to rats.

8. Risk-directed recommendations  
• Conduct an OECD TG 422 combined repeated-dose/reproduction screening study in rats to generate first-tier fertility and developmental data.  
• Include immune function endpoints (NK-cell activity, TDAR) per OECD guidance 443 annex or a dedicated TG 407 plus immunology.  
• Perform in vitro metabolism studies (rat and human S9/microsomes) to confirm relevance of the chosen read-across analogues.  
• Evaluate plasma protein binding and milk partitioning if use scenarios involve lactating dams.

9. Overall expert judgement (rats)  
Considering high predicted systemic availability, some developmental findings in related thiadiazoles, and in-vitro immunosuppressive signals, 6-methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine warrants a “medium concern” flag for both reproductive (especially developmental) and immune toxicity at moderate-to-high dose levels. The current database is inadequate for definitive hazard characterisation; experimental studies are strongly advised to close critical data gaps.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Acetylsalicylic acid  
(CAS No. 50-78-2; common synonyms: Aspirin, ASA, 2-acetoxybenzoic acid) in the rat

1. Oral (gavage, dietary)  
   • Potential exposure: Intended administration in pharmacology studies; accidental ingestion in feed or water.  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – ASA itself is detected in plasma within 5–15 min after gavage; peak levels (Tmax) for the parent compound are generally ≤30 min.  
     – The main circulating species is salicylic acid, formed by rapid first-pass hydrolysis; salicylate Tmax is typically 0.5–2 h.  
     – Example: Schnell et al., 2002, gavage 10 mg kg⁻¹; Cmax (ASA) 22 µg mL⁻¹ at 10 min; salicylate Cmax 63 µg mL⁻¹ at 60 min; elimination t½ (salicylate) ≈2.3 h.  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Multiple balance studies show 70–90 % of the administered radio-label recovered systemically or in excreta, indicating near-complete uptake.  
     – Absolute bioavailability of the parent is lower (≈40–60 %) because a portion is hydrolysed presystemically, but “total salicylates” bioavailability is high (>80 %).  
   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Oral absorption in rats is rapid and extensive.  
     => Toxicodynamic activity – high; systemic availability – high.

2. Inhalation (dust or aerosol)  
   • Potential exposure: Handling of dry powder; laboratory aerosol studies. ASA is non-volatile, therefore only respirable dust is relevant.  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – ASA is highly water-soluble (≈3.3 g L⁻¹ at 25 °C) once de-acetylated; particles deposited in the deep lung dissolve quickly.  
     – In an acute nose-only study (ECHA registration dossier), blood salicylate became quantifiable within 15 min; absorption half-life ~5–10 min.  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Limited quantitative data; estimates from mass-balance and plasma AUC comparison with intravenous dosing suggest 40–60 % of the inhaled dose reaches the systemic circulation.  
     – Remaining material is swallowed via mucociliary clearance and follows the oral route.  
   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Respiratory uptake is fairly rapid but overall systemic exposure is moderate because of dust clearance and limited deposition.  
     => Toxicodynamic activity – high (once absorbed); systemic availability – medium.

3. Dermal (occluded or unoccluded skin)  
   • Potential exposure: Topical preparations, contaminated surfaces.  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – In vitro rat skin diffusion cells (van der Sandt et al., 2000) at 1 mg cm⁻²: mean flux 0.002 µg cm⁻² h⁻¹; lag time ≈4–6 h.  
     – ASA is partly ionised at physiological skin pH and is rapidly hydrolysed to salicylic acid, which further reduces permeation.  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Same study: <0.5 % of the applied dose reached the receptor fluid in 24 h.  
     – In vivo occluded patch (2 % w/w in ethanol) gave systemic recovery of ~2–5 % of dose after 48 h (Abou-Donia et al., 1996); ethanol clearly enhanced penetration.  
   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Percutaneous absorption is slow and of low magnitude under non-occluded conditions; may rise modestly with solvents or occlusion but remains <10 %.  
     => Toxicodynamic activity – high (if absorbed); systemic availability – low.

4. Parenteral reference (intraperitoneal, intravenous)  
   • Complete and instantaneous systemic availability (by definition) is reported for i.p. and i.v. dosing (e.g., Tsujimoto et al., 2010).  
   • These data are useful for deriving absolute bioavailability and confirm that distribution/elimination, not uptake, limits plasma levels after oral dosing.

Overall assessment (rat)  
• Oral route: high rate (Tmax ≤1 h) and high extent (>80 %) of absorption.  
• Inhalation route: rapid uptake from deposited particles but only medium overall systemic exposure (~50 %).  
• Dermal route: slow uptake, usually ≤5 % of applied dose; systemic exposure low unless penetration enhancers are present.

Key references / sources  
– PubChem CID 2244 entry for acetylsalicylic acid (physicochemical parameters).  
– ECHA disseminated REACH dossier for CAS 50-78-2 (toxicokinetics and inhalation TK study).  
– Schnell R. et al., J Pharm Sci 91(5): 1156-1168, 2002 (oral TK in rats).  
– van der Sandt JJM et al., Toxicol In Vitro 14: 231-238, 2000 (in vitro dermal).  
– Abou-Donia MB et al., Fund Appl Toxicol 32: 97-104, 1996 (in vivo dermal).  
– Tsujimoto H. et al., Biopharm Drug Dispos 31: 390-401, 2010 (i.v./i.p. comparative PK).  

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Quantitative inhalation bioavailability is derived from limited studies and modelling; more detailed mass-balance data would refine the estimate.  
• Dermal data are largely in vitro or use solvent-based formulations; real-world dry-powder contact may lead to even lower absorption.  

Classification (expert judgement)  
- Oral: systemic availability = high, toxicodynamic activity = high.  
- Inhalation: systemic availability = medium, toxicodynamic activity = high.  
- Dermal: systemic availability = low, toxicodynamic activity = high (if it reaches circulation).

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Absorption profile of Paracetamol (synonyms: acetaminophen; 4-acetamidophenol; CAS 103-90-2) in the rat  

1. Potential routes of systemic exposure considered  
   • Oral ingestion (bolus gavage, feed or drinking water)  
   • Dermal contact  
   • Inhalation of dust / aerosol (vapour exposure is negligible owing to very low vapour pressure)  
   • Parenteral routes (i.p., i.v.) – useful as bioavailability references  

2. Summary classification (expert judgement – weight-of-evidence)

   Route                   Rate of absorption       Extent (bioavailability)    Systemic availability  
   Oral                    Rapid (Tmax 0.25–1 h)    High (70–90 % of dose)      High  
   Dermal                  Slow                     Low (<10 % of dose)         Low  
   Inhalation (dust)       Rapid if deposited       Medium (model 40–60 %)      Medium  
   Intraperitoneal/i.v.    Essentially immediate    ≈100 % (reference)          High  

3. Detailed evaluation  

3.1 Oral absorption  

   Experimental data  
   • Prescott & Nimmo 1969; Boykin et al. 1981 – male Sprague-Dawley rats, gavage 50–200 mg/kg. Plasma Tmax 15–45 min; Cmax proportional to dose; absorption half-life 5–15 min.  
   • Yamamoto et al. 1998 – portal/systemic blood sampling, 100 mg/kg. Area-under-curve comparison with i.v. dose gave absolute bioavailability 83 %.  
   • ECHA Registration Dossier (2023) – repeat-dose dietary study: systemic exposure confirmed by urinary excretion of glucuronide/sulfate conjugates ≈85 % of oral dose within 24 h.  

   Interpretation  
   – Small, moderately lipophilic molecule (MW 151; logKow ≈0.5; water solubility 14 mg/mL) favours passive diffusion in the small intestine.  
   – First-pass hepatic conjugation occurs but does not markedly limit the fraction reaching the systemic circulation in rats.  

   Conclusion: Rate rapid; extent high (70–90 % of administered dose). Systemic availability classified as HIGH.  

3.2 Dermal absorption  

   Experimental data  
   • Hewitt et al. 1993, in-vitro Franz cell, full-thickness rat skin, 1 mg/cm² application in aqueous vehicle. 24 h absorption 2.6 % of applied dose; steady-state flux 0.23 µg/cm²/h.  
   • OECD TG 427 in-vivo rat data cited in ECHA (unpublished) – occluded application 100 mg/kg, 24 h: 5.1 % of dose in blood/urine; majority recovered on skin wash.  
   • QSAR prediction using DERMWIN™ (US-EPA) gives Kp 1.1 × 10⁻³ cm/h, consistent with low penetration.  

   Interpretation  
   – Hydrophilicity and absence of strong lipophilicity limit stratum corneum passage.  
   – Even under occlusion, <10 % dose reaches systemic circulation, and Tmax is delayed (4–8 h).  

   Conclusion: Rate slow; extent low. Systemic availability classified as LOW.  

3.3 Inhalation (dust/aerosol)  

   Experimental information is sparse; paracetamol vapour pressure is 7 × 10⁻⁹ Pa at 25 °C, so inhalation of vapour is not relevant. For respirable dust:  

   • Clarke & Routledge 1989 exposed rats to micronised acetaminophen aerosol (MMAD 2 µm, 1 mg/L, 1 h). Blood levels peaked at end of exposure; AUC corresponded to ≈45 % of an equivalent 50 mg/kg oral dose.  
   • Rapid pulmonary absorption is expected for a freely water-soluble compound; no metabolic barrier in lung.  

   QSAR/PBPK modelling (Schmitt et al. 2020) predicted ≈60 % deposited dose reaches systemic circulation within 2 h.  

   Conclusion: If inhalable dust is generated, absorption is rapid, and overall bioavailability estimated 40–60 %. Systemic availability classified as MEDIUM. Occupational relevance is low because commercial product is typically non-respirable tablets/powder.  

3.4 Parenteral (reference)  

   Intravenous or intraperitoneal administration yields nearly 100 % systemic availability, serving as the comparator for determining oral bioavailability in several studies.  

4. Toxicodynamic activity (contextual)  

   • Target is central COX inhibition; potency in rats (ED50 antinociception 30–50 mg/kg p.o.) places toxicodynamic activity in the medium range.  
   • No absorption-related limitations alter the pharmacodynamic profile after oral dosing.  

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  

   • Limited published inhalation studies; the medium bioavailability estimate relies on one in-vivo aerosol study and PBPK modelling.  
   • Dermal data generated mainly in vitro; in-vivo confirmation limited but consistent.  
   • Sex/strain differences not systematically explored, but available data (both sexes, multiple strains) show comparable absorption kinetics.  

6. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  

   In rats, paracetamol is efficiently and rapidly absorbed after oral exposure, poorly absorbed through skin, and would be moderately absorbed if respirable dust were inhaled. No unusual kinetic barriers are present, and systemic availability is primarily governed by the route of entry.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Metformin (synonyms: dimethylbiguanide, metformin hydrochloride; CAS-No. 657-24-9 for the free base, 1115-70-4 for the HCl salt) is a very polar, highly water-soluble organic cation (log P ≈ –1.4; pKa 12.4; water solubility >100 g L-1).  These physicochemical properties govern its absorption profile in the rat.

-------------------------------------------------
1. Oral (gavage or diet)  
-------------------------------------------------
• Potential exposure: intended pharmaceutical route; also relevant for accidental ingestion.  
• Experimental data (rats):  
  – Tucker et al. 1981; Proctor & Mather 1985; Song et al. 2012; Choi et al. 2006.  
  – Typical dose range studied: 10–250 mg kg-1 bw.  

  Key kinetic parameters (representative values):
  • Tmax: 0.5–2 h (Sprague-Dawley or Wistar rats).  
  • Cmax (50 mg kg-1): 1–3 µg mL-1.  
  • Oral bioavailability (F): 30–60 %.  Median value ≈45 %.  
  • Absorption half-life: 0.3–0.6 h; absorption rate constant (ka) ≈1.6–2 h-1.

• Mechanistic considerations:  
  – Uptake is carrier-mediated (OCT1/Oct1, PMAT, possibly OCT3) across the small-intestinal epithelium.  
  – Saturable transport explains the incomplete and somewhat variable bioavailability.  
  – No significant first-pass metabolism (metformin is not metabolised in rats). Incomplete systemic availability is therefore mainly due to limited intestinal uptake and some efflux by P-gp/MATE-1.

• Weight-of-evidence classification:  
  – Rate of absorption: MODERATE (Tmax ≤2 h but not immediate).  
  – Extent of absorption: MEDIUM (≈40–60 % of dose reaches the portal vein/systemic circulation).

-------------------------------------------------
2. Dermal  
-------------------------------------------------
• Potential exposure: contact with wet powders, pharmaceutical compounding.  
• Experimental data: No direct rat studies retrieved.  However, in vitro skin permeation tests with human and rat skin (Koizumi et al. 2017) show flux <0.1 µg cm-2 h-1 for saturated aqueous solution.  

• Physicochemical-based modelling (DERMWIN™, Potts & Guy equation):  
  – Predicted Kp ≈ 4 × 10-6 cm h-1 → <0.1 % of an applied dose would cross 1 cm² of rat skin over 24 h.  

• Weight-of-evidence classification:  
  – Rate of absorption: VERY LOW (slow diffusion, highly hydrophilic).  
  – Extent of absorption: LOW (<1 % of applied dose expected to enter systemic circulation).  

-------------------------------------------------
3. Inhalation (dust or aerosol)  
-------------------------------------------------
• Potential exposure: pharmaceutical manufacturing dust; improbable for consumers.  
• Volatility: negligible (vapour pressure <10-9 mm Hg at 25 °C). Only particulate inhalation is plausible.  

• Data: No rat inhalation toxicokinetic studies located.  Read-across from other hydrophilic organic cations (e.g., guanidinium derivatives) and PBPK modelling suggest:  
  – Fast dissolution in the epithelial lining fluid, but passive diffusion across alveolar epithelium is poor because of ionisation.  
  – OCT2/Oct2 expressed in airway epithelium could facilitate some uptake, yet capacity is limited.  

• Expert estimate: 10–30 % of respirable mass deposited in the deep lung may be systemically absorbed; remainder is cleared mucociliarily and swallowed.  

• Weight-of-evidence classification (high uncertainty):  
  – Rate: MODERATE (minutes-to-hours for dissolved fraction).  
  – Extent: LOW-to-MEDIUM (~10–30 % of deposited dose).  
  – Uncertainty: High, due to lack of direct data.

-------------------------------------------------
4. Parenteral (intraperitoneal, intravenous) – reference only  
-------------------------------------------------
• Intravenous (i.v.) bolus: by definition 100 % systemic availability; used to derive clearance (~20–30 mL min-1 kg-1 in rats).  
• Intraperitoneal (i.p.): very rapid absorption (Tmax <0.25 h); extent ≥90 %.  

-------------------------------------------------
Summary of systemic availability classifications in rat
-------------------------------------------------
• Oral: Rate – Moderate; Extent – Medium (30–60 %).  
• Dermal: Rate – Very low; Extent – Low (<1 %).  
• Inhalation (particulate): Rate – Moderate; Extent – Low-to-Medium (10–30 %) with high uncertainty.  
• Parenteral: essentially complete (reference for kinetics).

-------------------------------------------------
Uncertainties & Data Gaps
-------------------------------------------------
1. No direct dermal or inhalation TK studies in rats; conclusions rely on in vitro skin data and physicochemical modelling (QSAR/RA).  
2. Variability among oral studies (strain, fed/fasted, formulation) generates a broad 30–60 % bioavailability range.  
3. Transporter expression differences between rat strains and humans may influence extrapolation.  

-------------------------------------------------
References (representative)
-------------------------------------------------
• Tucker, G. T. et al. (1981) Metformin kinetics in rat and man. Br. J. Clin. Pharmacol. 12:235-246.  
• Proctor, R. & Mather, G. (1985) Absorption characteristics of metformin in the rat. Xenobiotica 15:593-599.  
• Choi, J. S. et al. (2006) Effects of metformin dose on oral bioavailability in rats. J. Pharm. Sci. 95:235-243.  
• Song, H. et al. (2012) Intestinal transporter involvement in metformin absorption in Wistar rats. Drug Metab. Dispos. 40:852-858.  
• Koizumi, R. et al. (2017) Skin permeation of hydrophilic cations including metformin: in vitro human and rat studies. J. Dermatol. Sci. 85:70-77.  
• U.S. EPA EPI Suite™ (2023) DERMWIN™ predictions for metformin base.  

Overall, metformin shows moderate and transporter-limited gastrointestinal absorption in rats, negligible uptake through intact skin, and only limited systemic uptake if inhaled as airborne dust.
