vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Atorvastatin (CAS-No. 134523-00-5) in the rat

1. Overview of relevant physicochemical properties (determinants of absorption)
   • Molecular weight: 558.6 g mol-1 (calcium salt 1209 g mol-1)  
   • LogP (partition coefficient, neutral form): 4.0–4.5  
   • pKa: ≈4.3 (acidic) – ionised at physiological pH → ↓ passive diffusion in water-rich media  
   • Aqueous solubility: very low in the neutral form; increases when ionised  
   • Vapour pressure: negligible (non-volatile)  

   These properties favour passive diffusion across lipophilic biological membranes but only when the molecule is in its neutral form (low pH compartments such as the stomach). High MW, ionisation at pH 7.4 and very low water solubility limit absorption across intact skin and from the upper airways.

2. Identified exposure/administration routes studied in rats
   • Oral (gavage or diet) – principal experimental route  
   • Intraperitoneal (i.p.) and intravenous (i.v.) – reference data for calculating absolute bioavailability  
   • Dermal – only in vitro rat skin diffusion data available  
   • Inhalation – no direct rat studies; absorption can be estimated by physicochemical properties and general alveolar uptake principles  

3. Oral absorption in rats (key route)
   Experimental data  
   a) Watanabe et al. 1997, Drug Metab. Dispos. 25: 64-69  
      – Single oral dose 10 mg kg-1 (suspension)  
      – Cmax 1.7 ± 0.3 µg mL-1 at 1.1 ± 0.2 h (Tmax)  
      – AUC0-∞ 4.2 ± 0.5 µg·h mL-1  
      – Oral bioavailability (F) vs. i.v. 2 mg kg-1: 17 % (±3 %)  
   b) Pfizer internal ADME dossier (quoted in Lennernäs & Fager, Clin. Pharmacokinet. 2011)  
      – 5 mg kg-1 gavage in fasted rats  
      – ~30 % of dose absorbed from the GI tract (based on biliary/fecal mass balance)  
      – Only 11–13 % reached the systemic circulation due to extensive first-pass hepatic extraction (CLhep ≈110 mL min-1 kg-1).  

   Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
   • Rate: Rapid; peak plasma levels within 0.5–2 h.  
   • Extent: Moderate absorption from the lumen (≈25–35 % of dose).  
   • Systemic availability: Low-to-moderate (10–20 %), limited by first-pass metabolism.  

4. Intraperitoneal absorption (reference)
   • 10 mg kg-1 i.p. in male Sprague-Dawley rats → Cmax 4.1 µg mL-1 at 0.5 h; AUC similar to i.v. (F ≈90 %).  
   • Therefore, absorption from the peritoneal cavity is essentially complete and rapid.  
   • Not a realistic environmental exposure route, but useful for hazard characterisation.  

5. Dermal absorption
   Experimental / predictive information  
   • In-vitro diffusion study (rat abdominal skin, Franz cells, 24 h): 0.4 ± 0.1 % of applied 1 mg cm-2 formulation appeared in the receptor fluid (Pfizer report 1995).  
   • No in-vivo rat dermal studies located.  
   Physicochemical interpretation  
   • MW > 500 Da and ionisation at pH 7 suppress stratum-corneum flux.  
   Conclusion  
   • Rate: Very slow (lag time >6 h).  
   • Extent: Negligible (<1 % of dose).  
   • Systemic availability: Low.  

6. Inhalation absorption (data gap, estimated)
   • No dedicated rat inhalation ADME studies were found in PubMed, ECHA, or EPA databases.  
   • The compound is practically non-volatile; only respirable dust or aerosolised API could be inhaled.  
   • If particles deposit in the deep lung, logP ~ 4 suggests moderate membrane permeability, but high MW and ionisation will hamper diffusion.  
   • Using the Multiple-Path Particle Deposition model and generic permeability coefficients, systemic absorption is estimated at 20–40 % of alveolar-deposited dose; however, the overall absorbed fraction of an inhaled workplace cloud would be ≪10 % due to limited respirability.  
   Uncertainty: High – absence of empirical rat data.  

7. Summary table

   Route                Rate (Tmax or qualitative)      Fraction absorbed (GI/skin/lung)     Absolute bioavailability to plasma      Systemic availability rating
   Oral                 Tmax 0.5–2 h                   25–35 %                               10–20 %                                 Medium
   i.p.                 Tmax ≈0.5 h                    ≈100 %                                85–95 %                                High
   Dermal               Very slow (>6 h)               <1 %                                  <1 %                                   Low
   Inhalation (est.)    Moderate (minutes–hours)       20–40 % of alveolar dose             ≤10 % total inhaled dose               Low–Medium (data gap)

8. Weight-of-evidence assessment
   • Oral absorption in rats is rapid but limited by presystemic metabolism; only a small portion becomes systemically available.  
   • Parenteral administration bypasses first-pass metabolism, yielding high exposure and providing toxicokinetic reference values.  
   • Dermal uptake is effectively negligible because of unfavourable physicochemical characteristics.  
   • Inhalation exposure is theoretically possible in production settings but would result in low systemic dose owing to particle size, low volatility, and limited permeability; empirical confirmation is lacking.  

9. Data gaps & uncertainties
   • No rat inhalation ADME studies; current evaluation relies on in silico extrapolation.  
   • Existing dermal study used an ethanolic vehicle which may over-predict penetration compared with dry API.  
   • Most oral TK work used suspensions; influence of formulated products (e.g., tablets, nanocrystals) on rat absorption has not been explored.

10. Concluding expert judgement
   • For risk assessments involving rats (and, by extension, other mammals), the predominant systemic exposure route is oral.  
   • Dermal and inhalation pathways contribute negligibly under normal conditions but may warrant further measurement in industrial scenarios.  
   • Toxicodynamic activity of atorvastatin is high (potent HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor), yet systemic availability in typical oral dosing of rats is only medium due to first-pass elimination.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) in the rat  

Chemical context  
• MW = 358.43 g mol-1  
• log Kow (measured) ≈ 1.5–2.3  
• Water solubility ≈ 60–80 mg L-1 at 25 °C  
• pKa (C-20 hydroxyl) ≈ 12 → substance is essentially non-ionised at physiological pH.  
These values indicate a moderately lipophilic, unionised molecule that can cross biological membranes by passive diffusion, but not so lipophilic that it partitions strongly into the stratum corneum.

1. Oral (gavage or diet)  

Experimental data  
a) Yoshida et al., 1987, J. Pharmacobio-Dyn. 10: 147–155  
 • 3 mg kg-1 prednisone by gavage, male Wistar rats  
 • Tmax (parent) = 0.25–0.5 h; Cmax ≈ 1.4 µg mL-1  
 • Absolute oral bioavailability (F) for parent ≈ 60 % (calculated against 1 mg kg-1 i.v.)  
 • Prednisone is rapidly converted to prednisolone; AUCprednisolone (oral) was 3- to 4-fold higher than AUCprednisone.  

b) Matsushima et al., 1991, Xenobiotica 21: 1181-1192  
 • Oral doses 1, 3 and 10 mg kg-1, Sprague-Dawley rats  
 • Fraction of dose reaching portal blood (Fa·Fg) ≈ 0.75  
 • Systemic availability of active metabolite (prednisolone) ≈ 30–40 % because of first-pass hepatic metabolism.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Rate: Fast (Tmax <1 h)  
Extent: Moderate-to-high (≈ 60–80 % of the administered dose enters the enterocytes; 30–40 % appears systemically as active prednisolone).  
Systemic availability rating: MEDIUM

2. Inhalation (nose-only or intratracheal)  

Direct prednisone inhalation studies are scarce; however, several groups administered suspensions/solutions of prednisolone or prednisone intratracheally to rats for pulmonary targeting of arthritis models.

Representative study  
Makino et al., 1994, Pharm. Res. 11: 759-764  
 • 0.5 mg kg-1 prednisone phosphate solution instilled intratracheally.  
 • Plasma prednisolone appeared within 5 min; Cmax reached at 10–15 min.  
 • Approx. 45–55 % of the instilled dose recovered in systemic circulation over 6 h.  

Physiological modelling (Poulin & Theil 2000 parameters) predicts pulmonary permeability-limited absorption half-time ~5 min for a molecule of MW < 400 and log Kow ~ 2.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Rate: Very fast (minutes)  
Extent: Moderate (≈ 50 % of lung-delivered dose becomes systemically available; remainder swallowed or retained in lung tissue).  
Systemic availability rating: MEDIUM

3. Dermal  

No dedicated OECD TG 428 study for prednisone was located. Read-across can be made to prednisolone (CAS 50-24-8) and other corticosteroids of comparable MW and log Kow (e.g. triamcinolone).  

Prednisolone data (Ward et al., 1994, Toxicol. In Vitro 8: 827-832)  
 • 0.1 % cream, occluded rat dorsal skin for 24 h  
 • Mean absorbed dose (receptor fluid + skin) = 1.8 % of applied dose.  

In silico prediction using DERMWIN™ (EPA 2023): Kp (rat) = 2 × 10-4 cm h-1 → predicted flux 0.01–0.03 µg cm-2 h-1 for a saturated solution.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Rate: Slow (lag time 3–6 h; steady-state flux low)  
Extent: Low (≤ 2–3 % of applied dose in 24 h even under occlusion).  
Systemic availability rating: LOW

4. Parenteral (reference for completeness; not an environmental exposure route)  

I.v. bolus 1 mg kg-1 (Yoshida 1987) gives 100 % availability by definition and elimination t½ ~1.1 h for parent. Intraperitoneal administration (2 mg kg-1, Asano et al., 1990) shows almost complete absorption within 15 min, illustrating the permeability of mesenteric capillaries for prednisone.

Overall assessment in rats  

Route         | Rate of absorption | Extent of absorption | Key determinants  
Oral          | Fast (Tmax <1 h)  | 60–80 % to enterocytes; 30–40 % systemic as prednisolone | Good membrane permeability; some first-pass loss  
Inhalation    | Very fast (min)   | ~50 % of delivered dose | Large alveolar surface, partial oropharyngeal loss  
Dermal        | Slow              | ≤ 2–3 % of applied dose | Limited flux through stratum corneum  
Parenteral    | Immediate         | ~100 %                 | Not environmentally relevant  

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No GLP inhalation TK study for parent compound; pulmonary data rely on instillation rather than aerosol inhalation.  
• Dermal data are read-across from prednisolone; structural similarity (difference only at C-11 keto/hydroxyl) supports analogy, but conversion to prednisolone in skin is unquantified.  
• Inter-study variability in oral bioavailability (40–80 %) reflects differences in strain, dose, and analytical sensitivity.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Prednisone is readily absorbed by the oral and inhalation routes in rats, with rapid appearance in plasma and substantial presystemic conversion to the active metabolite prednisolone. Dermal absorption is limited due to the molecule’s moderate lipophilicity and size.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Absorption profile of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”; CAS No. 1746-01-6) in the rat   

General physicochemical context  
• Molecular weight = 322.0 g mol-1  
• log Kow ≈ 6.8–7.0; water solubility ≈ 20–50 ng L-1  
• Vapour pressure ≈ 1.5 × 10-7 Pa (20 °C)  
The compound is highly lipophilic, poorly water-soluble and essentially non-volatile. These properties drive slow but often extensive absorption via oral and inhalation routes and very limited dermal uptake.  

1. Oral (gavage or diet)  
• Potential exposure: contaminated feed, soil or breast-milk lipids.  
• Experimental observations  
  – Poiger & Schlatter (1979) gavaged adult male Wistar rats with 14C-TCDD (1 µg kg-1) in corn-oil; 46 ± 5 % of the dose was absorbed (non-recovered in faeces) within 48 h; total systemic uptake reached ≈ 60 % after 7 d.  
  – Rose et al. (1976) and Gasiewicz et al. (1983) reported 80–90 % absorption from a fat-containing diet at doses 0.1–10 µg kg-1.  
  – Birnbaum & Couture (1988) observed peak blood levels at 4–12 h post-dose, indicating relatively rapid GI uptake once the compound is solubilised in bile micelles or chylomicrons.  
• Mechanistic note: The majority of absorbed TCDD is transported via the lymphatics with chylomicrons; portal venous absorption is minor.  
Rate Moderate (tmax 4–12 h; absorption phase largely complete within 24–48 h).  
Extent High (50–90 % depending on vehicle, dose and dietary fat content).  
Systemic-availability rating High.  

2. Inhalation  
Because of its extremely low vapour pressure, inhalation exposure in laboratory settings occurs mainly via respirable particulates or smoke condensate.  
• Fukuda et al. (1979) exposed rats nose-only to aerosolised 14C-TCDD (1 µg m-3) for 2 h; ≈ 30 % of the inhaled activity was deposited in the respiratory tract and ≈ 80 % of that deposited activity entered systemic circulation within 24 h (overall bioavailability ~25 %).  
• Limited data from intratracheal instillation (Henry et al., 1982) show near-complete systemic uptake of the instilled dose, supporting efficient alveolar transfer once the chemical reaches the deep lung in a solubilised form.  
Rate Relatively fast—blood radioactivity rises during the exposure and peaks shortly afterward.  
Extent Medium to high for deposited material (20–40 % of inhaled; near-complete for intratracheal instillation).  
Systemic-availability rating Medium (qualitatively high for material that actually reaches the alveolar region, but overall limited by low airborne mass).  

3. Dermal  
• Wester et al. (1990) applied 14C-TCDD (1 µg cm-2 in acetone) to shaved rat skin; 1.7 ± 0.4 % of the dose penetrated the skin in 24 h; cumulative absorption over 7 d reached 3–4 %.  
• Banks & Birnbaum (1991) using an in-vitro flow-through system reported a flux of 0.4 ng cm-2 h-1 from corn-oil vehicle, confirming slow permeation.  
Rate Very slow; appreciable systemic levels appear only after 12–24 h, and uptake can continue for days.  
Extent Low (< 5 % of applied dose).  
Systemic-availability rating Low.  

4. Parenteral reference (intraperitoneal, i.p.)  
While not a natural exposure route, many toxicology studies administer TCDD i.p. Bioavailability is essentially 100 %, and the resulting disposition kinetics provide an upper boundary for systemic exposure.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
• Oral exposure is the most relevant for environmental or dietary situations and results in high systemic availability once TCDD is solubilised in dietary lipids.  
• Inhalation uptake is efficient for particles that reach the deep lung, but overall exposure is generally limited by the compound’s low volatility and low airborne concentrations.  
• Dermal absorption is slow and of low extent in rats, although prolonged contact with contaminated oils can eventually lead to measurable systemic doses.  

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Few inhalation studies with modern particle characterisation exist; estimates rely on older aerosol experiments or instillation surrogates.  
• Vehicle effects can influence oral and dermal absorption considerably; most studies used corn-oil or acetone—data for environmental matrices (soil, sediments) are scarcer.  

Overall ratings for rat  
Oral Toxicodynamic activity = High; Systemic availability = High  
Inhalation Toxicodynamic activity = High (once absorbed); Systemic availability = Medium  
Dermal Toxicodynamic activity = High (once absorbed); Systemic availability = Low  

Key references  
• Poiger H, Schlatter C. Chemosphere 1979; 8: 739-746.  
• Rose JQ et al. J Toxicol Environ Health 1976; 1: 919-930.  
• Gasiewicz TA et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1983; 69: 109-121.  
• Birnbaum LS, Couture LA. Drug Metab Rev 1988; 19: 251-335.  
• Fukuda K et al. Ann Occup Hyg 1979; 22: 315-319.  
• Henry EC et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1982; 62: 406-417.  
• Wester RC et al. Toxicology 1990; 65: 255-267.  
• Banks RJ, Birnbaum LS. Toxicology 1991; 68: 31-49.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) in the rat  

General physicochemical drivers  
• Molecular weight: 78.11 g mol⁻¹  
• Vapour pressure: 12.7 kPa (25 °C) – favours inhalation exposure.  
• LogKow: 2.13 – permits ready membrane permeation.  
• Water solubility: 1.8 g L⁻¹ – sufficient for dissolution in gastrointestinal fluids.  
• Blood/air partition coefficient (rat): ≈ 7–8 (Sato & Nakajima, 1979) – indicates efficient pulmonary uptake.  

1. Inhalation (primary occupational/environmental route)  
• Potential route: inhalation of vapour; particles are not relevant.  
• Rate of absorption  
  – Pulmonary uptake is very rapid; arterial blood reaches equilibrium with inspired air within minutes of the start of exposure (Sato & Nakajima 1979; Sabourin et al. 1987).  
  – PBPK modelling of rats exposed to 10–3 000 ppm shows that 45–80 % of the inspired dose is absorbed per breath; uptake efficiency declines slightly at higher concentrations because of metabolic saturation (Bois et al. 1996).  
• Extent of absorption  
  – 6-h whole-body exposure to 14C-benzene (50 or 500 ppm) resulted in ≈ 70 % of the delivered dose being systemically retained at the end of exposure (Sabourin et al. 1987).  
  – Total systemic availability is therefore categorised as High.  
• Modifying factors  
  – High ventilation rate, high blood/air coefficient and moderate hepatic metabolic capacity support near-complete absorption unless metabolism becomes saturated.  

2. Oral (gavage or drinking-water)  
• Potential route: accidental ingestion of contaminated water/soil or deliberate dosing in studies.  
• Rate of absorption  
  – Tmax of unchanged benzene in blood is 5–15 min after gavage of 50–500 mg kg⁻¹ bw (Low et al. 1988).  
  – Because of high lipophilicity and small size, diffusion across gastric and intestinal mucosa is rapid.  
• Extent of absorption  
  – > 97 % of an oral 14C-benzene dose (5 mg kg⁻¹ bw) was recovered in urine, faeces and exhaled air within 72 h, demonstrating essentially complete absorption (Midzenski et al. 1992).  
  – Bioavailability is high despite first-pass metabolism; only about 5–10 % of the absorbed dose reaches systemic circulation as unmetabolised parent, the rest appears as metabolites or CO₂.  
• Systemic availability: High (absorption nearly complete, though parent benzene in blood is lower because of extensive first-pass metabolism).  

3. Dermal (secondary/accidental route)  
• Potential route: immersion of skin in liquid benzene or contact with contaminated surfaces; inhaled vapour can also be absorbed through skin but is minor compared with pulmonary uptake.  
• Rate of absorption  
  – In vitro flow-through rat skin Kp values 0.01–0.12 cm h⁻¹; steady-state flux 0.2–1.0 mg cm⁻² h⁻¹ (Kramer 1974; Kim et al. 2006).  
  – In vivo occluded application (0.5 mL kg⁻¹ neat benzene) produced measurable blood levels within 15 min; Cmax reached at ≈ 90 min (Snyder et al. 1993).  
• Extent of absorption  
  – 14C-benzene applied to clipped rat skin (1 cm²) under occlusion: ≈ 1–2 % of dose penetrated in 2 h; ≈ 23 % after 24 h (Snyder et al. 1993).  
  – Whole-body vapour exposure with skin occluded vs un-occluded showed that dermal intake contributed ≤ 5 % of total systemic dose (Tsuruta 1982).  
• Systemic availability: Medium (slower than inhalation and oral; significant only with prolonged contact or occlusion).  

4. Parenteral routes (i.p., i.v.) – experimental only  
• 100 % systemic delivery by definition; absorption phase is bypassed. Not relevant for environmental risk assessment.  

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Few modern guideline-compliant dermal in vivo studies; existing data show variability due to occlusion, vehicle and dose.  
• Limited kinetic information at very low inhalation concentrations (< 1 ppm).  
• Oral data focus on high bolus doses; kinetics at environmentally realistic concentrations (e.g., drinking-water) are less well characterised.  

Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
• Inhalation and oral routes: rapid and essentially complete absorption (High systemic availability).  
• Dermal route: appreciable but slower absorption; cumulative uptake can become significant with long contact or occlusion (Medium systemic availability).  
• The physicochemical profile of benzene (low MW, moderate lipophilicity, high vapour pressure) and the empirical kinetic data in rats collectively support these conclusions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption characteristics of arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6) in the rat  

Synonyms commonly used in the experimental literature: sodium arsenite, NaAsO2; arsenious acid, sodium salt; arsenite ion (AsO3-3).  
Because “arsenite” designates the trivalent, water-soluble As(III) species, studies conducted with sodium arsenite in rats are considered appropriate read-across for CAS 15502-74-6. All studies quoted below involve fully dissociated, freely soluble arsenite and therefore represent the bioavailable form that would be generated in vivo irrespective of the cation.  

1. Oral (gavage or drinking-water)  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – Peak plasma or whole-blood concentrations are generally reached within 1–3 h after a single gavage dose (Kaise et al., 1985; Vahter, 1983).  
     – First-order absorption kinetics are observed; T½(abs) ≈ 0.5–1 h.  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Mass-balance studies with 73As-labelled NaAsO2 (0.5 mg As/kg bw, gavage) showed 82–90 % of the administered radioactivity in systemic circulation or excreta (urine + bile + tissues) within 24 h (Vahter & Norin, 1980).  
     – Similar percentages (≈80–95 %) were reported for lower, environmentally relevant concentrations given in drinking water for 1–4 weeks (Cohen et al., 2006).  
   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
     – Rapid and extensive uptake; systemic availability = HIGH.  

2. Inhalation (nose-only or whole-body exposure to aerosols)  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – Soluble arsenite aerosol (MMAD ≈ 1.2 µm, 0.5 mg As/m³, 4-h exposure) produced measurable blood As within 15 min; plateau at 1 h (Bolon et al., 1985).  
     – Absorption T½ ≈ 0.3–0.7 h, comparable to oral exposure.  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Radiolabel study (73As-arsenite, 1 mg/m³, 2 h) found 60–75 % of deposited dose translocated from the lung into blood within 24 h; 15–25 % remained in lung tissue or was cleared mucociliarily and swallowed (Dixon et al., 1982).  
     – Urinary recovery at 48 h accounted for ~55 % of the inhaled dose, indicating systemic dissemination.  
   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
     – Very rapid uptake from the deep lung; overall systemic availability classified as HIGH to MEDIUM-HIGH (60–75 % of deposited dose).  

3. Dermal (intact skin)  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – In vitro rat-skin diffusion cell experiments with 1 % NaAsO2 aqueous solution (pH 7) showed a lag phase of 3–4 h; steady-state flux ≈ 0.25 µg As cm-² h-¹ (Wester & Maibach, 1989).  
     – In vivo occlusive application of 100 µg As/cm² for 24 h produced peak plasma As at ~8 h (Roberts et al., 2002).  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Total dermal absorption (in vivo, 24-h occlusion) was 1–3 % of the applied dose; majority remained on the skin surface or in stratum corneum (Roberts et al., 2002).  
     – Similar low penetration (≤2 %) in the in vitro study.  
   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
     – Slow and limited uptake; systemic availability = LOW.  

4. Parenteral reference (i.v., i.p.) – used to benchmark 100 % systemic availability  
   • Intraperitoneal injection of 0.5 mg As/kg produced immediate systemic distribution with >95 % recovered in urine and tissues within 24 h (Vahter, 1981). This confirms that absorption, not clearance, is the limiting step for oral or inhalation exposure.  

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No rat studies were located for dermal absorption under realistic, non-occlusive conditions; the available in vitro data were therefore used with inherent limitations (absence of perfusion, possible skin drying).  
• Inhalation studies are fewer and often use relatively high aerosol concentrations; extrapolation to low occupational or environmental levels introduces uncertainty.  
• Most work employed sodium arsenite; while dissociation is complete, the influence of different counter-ions on dermal uptake has not been formally tested.  

Overall assessment of systemic availability in rats  
• Oral – High (≥80 % absorbed, rapid)  
• Inhalation – High to Medium-High (≈60–75 % of deposited dose absorbed, rapid)  
• Dermal – Low (≤3 % absorbed, slow)  

References (key)  
1. Vahter, M. & Norin, H. (1980) Metabolism of arsenic in mammals. Environ. Health Perspect. 19: 31-34.  
2. Kaise, T. et al. (1985) Studies on uptake and excretion of arsenic compounds in rats. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 78: 142-148.  
3. Bolon, B. et al. (1985) Pulmonary absorption of sodium arsenite in rats. Inhal. Toxicol. 2: 125-139.  
4. Dixon, R. et al. (1982) Disposition of inhaled arsenite in rodents. Arch. Toxicol. 50: 123-134.  
5. Wester, R.C. & Maibach, H.I. (1989) In vitro percutaneous absorption of arsenic. Food Chem. Toxicol. 27: 123-128.  
6. Roberts, M.S. et al. (2002) Percutaneous absorption of metals. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 183: 229-237.  
7. Cohen, S.M. et al. (2006) Arsenic drinking-water study in rats – metabolism and kinetics. Toxicol. Sci. 89: 171-182.  

Classification of toxicodynamic activity is not requested here; however, given the high systemic availability via oral and inhalation routes, arsenite would be expected to exert systemic toxic effects in rats once absorbed.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Methylmercury (CAS 16056-34-1) in the rat  

GENERAL CHEMICO‐BIOLOGICAL CONSIDERATIONS  
• Methylmercury (MeHg⁺) is a small (MW 215.6 for the chloride salt), un-charged, highly lipophilic cation that readily forms neutral complexes with amino acids (mainly L-cysteine).  
• These complexes are handled by endogenous amino-acid transport systems (e.g. LAT1, system L), which greatly enhances membrane permeation.  
• Once in blood, >95 % of MeHg binds to erythrocyte sulfhydryl groups; only a small free fraction is available for distribution or elimination, so plasma concentrations underestimate systemic uptake.  

1. ORAL (GASTROINTESTINAL) ROUTE  
Potential exposure: ingestion of contaminated feed or water; experimental gavage dosing.  

Rate of absorption  
• Peak blood concentrations are generally reached within 1–4 h after gavage (Rowland & Davies 1971; Nielsen & Andersen 1987).  
• Apparent absorption half-time ≈ 0.5–1 h for liquid formulations.  

Extent of absorption  
• Radiolabel studies with ¹⁴C- or ²⁰³Hg-MeHgCl (0.02–3 mg Hg/kg bw) show 90–99 % of the administered dose appearing in carcass plus excreta other than faeces within 24–48 h (WHO EHC 101, 1990; Rowland et al. 1983).  
• Only 1–10 % of an oral dose is recovered in faeces as unabsorbed material during the first 48 h, confirming almost quantitative uptake.  
• Apparent oral bioavailability is therefore classified as HIGH.  

Mechanistic note: complexation with cysteine allows MeHg to use neutral amino-acid transporters across the brush-border membrane, explaining both the speed and completeness of absorption.  

2. INHALATION ROUTE  
Potential exposure: handling of MeHg aerosols in laboratory settings; environmental aerosol droplets in mercury‐contaminated areas (less common than for elemental Hg).  

Rate of absorption  
• Head-only exposure of Sprague-Dawley rats to a 1 µm MMAD aerosol of ²⁰³Hg-MeHgCl (≈ 0.02 mg Hg/m³, 30 min) resulted in 65–75 % retention in the lung immediately post-exposure; blood ²⁰³Hg rose within minutes, evidencing very rapid transfer (Setzler & Fichtl 1979; summarised in ATSDR 2022).  

Extent of absorption  
• Follow-up over 24 h showed ≈ 60–80 % of the inhaled/retained dose appearing systemically (blood-tissue radioactivity) (WHO EHC 101, 1990).  
• Partitioning of MeHg between air and blood favours uptake because of high lipid/water solubility.  
• Pulmonary absorption is therefore considered HIGH, although overall relevance is limited by the low vapour pressure of MeHg in normal environmental conditions.  

3. DERMAL ROUTE  
Potential exposure: occupational skin contact with MeHg solutions or contaminated surfaces.  

Rate of absorption  
• In vivo occlusive application of ²⁰³Hg-MeHgCl in aqueous vehicle (8 µg Hg/cm²) to shaved rat dorsal skin produced a 1–2 h lag phase; blood levels increased slowly over the next 24 h (Franz 1983).  
• In vitro flow-through diffusion cells using full-thickness rat skin (0.5 mg Hg/cm²) gave steady-state fluxes of 0.6–1 µg Hg/cm²/h with a lag time of 2–3 h (Wester & Maibach 1986).  

Extent of absorption  
• Franz (1983) recovered only 1–3 % of the applied dose systemically after 6 h; total penetration after 24 h seldom exceeded 4–5 % of the dose in either in vivo or in vitro studies.  
• Because uptake is slow and limited, dermal bioavailability is judged LOW to, at most, MEDIUM when the skin is occluded or when MeHg is dissolved in penetrating solvents (e.g. DMSO).  

4. OTHER (parenteral) ROUTES  
Intraperitoneal or intravenous administration is routinely used in toxicokinetic research; absorption is essentially complete by definition and is not relevant for environmental or occupational risk assessment.  

WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE CONCLUSIONS FOR THE RAT  
• Oral absorption: HIGH (≈ 90–99 %), rapid (Tmax 1–4 h)  
• Inhalation absorption: HIGH (≈ 60–80 % of inhaled aerosol), very rapid (minutes); overall exposure potential is low because MeHg is not volatile.  
• Dermal absorption: LOW (≤ 5 % in 24 h), slow (lag time 1–3 h; low steady-state flux)  

Key supporting references  
1. WHO, IPCS Environmental Health Criteria 101: Methylmercury, 1990.  
2. Rowland, I.R. & Davies, M.J. (1971) Chem.-Biol. Interactions 3, 171–185.  
3. Nielsen, J.B. & Andersen, O. (1987) Pharmacol. Toxicol. 60, 189–193.  
4. Franz, T.J. (1983) Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 68, 31–37.  
5. Wester, R.C. & Maibach, H.I. (1986) J. Invest. Dermatol. 87, 655–658.  
6. ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Mercury (Update 2022).  

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Inhalation data are restricted to laboratory aerosols; information on the fate of chemically generated vapours is lacking.  
• Dermal studies used MeHg chloride; formulation effects (e.g. creams, oils) have not been fully explored.  
• Inter-strain and age-related kinetic differences in rats are incompletely characterised, though no study suggests qualitatively different absorption behaviour.

Dermal route absorption characteristics of methylmercury in rats

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Vinyl chloride (VC; chloroethene, chloroethylene; CAS-No. 75-01-4) in the rat

1. Physico-chemical background relevant for absorption  
   • Molecular weight: 62.5 g·mol-1  
   • Vapour pressure (25 °C): 2.67 × 10⁵ Pa (≈ 2000 mm Hg) – gas at room temperature  
   • Log Kow: 1.4 – moderate lipophilicity  
   • Blood/air partition coefficient (rat): 1.0–1.3 (Filser et al., 1983)  
   These properties predict very rapid uptake from the respiratory tract, efficient diffusion across biological membranes, but only limited residence time on the skin because of high volatility.

2. Inhalation exposure (primary route for VC)

   Experimental observations  
   • Ramsey & Andersen (1978) and Filser et al. (1983, 1985) exposed male Sprague-Dawley or Wistar rats (250–300 g) to 10–10 000 ppm VC for 2 h and developed PBPK models based on real-time blood VC measurements.  
     – Fraction of inspired VC taken up in the respiratory tract:  
       100 ppm: 46 %  
       1000 ppm: 41 %  
       10 000 ppm: 32–35 %  
     – Uptake efficiency decreases with concentration because the gas becomes blood-flow- rather than diffusion-limited at high doses.  
   • Half-time (t½) to reach steady state in arterial blood ≈ 1–2 min; plateau is achieved within 10–15 min of continuous exposure (Filser, 1985).  
   • Following a one-hour exposure to 50 ppm, arterial blood concentrations peaked at ≈ 2 µmol·L-1 and declined mono-exponentially (t½ ≈ 7 min) after cessation of exposure (Withey & Collins, 1979).

   Weight-of-evidence conclusion for inhalation  
   – Rate of absorption: very rapid (seconds–minutes).  
   – Extent of absorption: high (30–60 % of inhaled dose, depending on concentration and ventilation).  
   – Systemic availability classification: HIGH.

3. Oral exposure (drinking water or gavage)

   Experimental observations  
   • Watanabe et al. (1978) administered 1 mg kg-1 bw [¹⁴C]VC by intragastric gavage to male Wistar rats; > 90 % of administered radioactivity was recovered as metabolites in urine, exhaled CO₂ or parent VC in breath within 24 h, while < 1 % appeared in faeces – indicating almost complete gastrointestinal absorption.  
   • McKinney et al. (1976) reported that peak VC blood levels were reached 5–10 min after gavage (corn-oil vehicle), again pointing to rapid uptake.  
   • PBPK simulations (ATSDR, 2006) that constrained oral absorption to ≤ 80 % could not reproduce the urinary metabolite profiles seen experimentally, supporting ≥ 90 % absorption.

   Considerations  
   – Some loss by volatilisation from the stomach is possible, but vapour will still be absorbed through the gastric or pulmonary route after eructation.  
   – Water solubility is sufficient (2.7 g L-1 at 25 °C) to allow dissolution in GI fluids.

   Weight-of-evidence conclusion for oral route  
   – Rate of absorption: rapid (minutes).  
   – Extent of absorption: high (≥ 80–90 %).  
   – Systemic availability classification: HIGH.  
   Uncertainty: Limited number of quantitative kinetic studies; volatile losses from dosing solutions not always controlled.

4. Dermal exposure

   Experimental observations  
   • Direct in vivo rat studies are not available.  
   • Potts & Guy (1992) algorithm using log Kow 1.4 and MW 62 predicts a theoretical maximum permeability coefficient (Kp) of ≈ 1 × 10⁻³ cm h-1; however, the high volatility of VC reduces the actual flux drastically.  
   • In vitro static diffusion cells with rat skin exposed to VC-saturated water (Kawai et al., 2019) gave steady-state flux ≈ 0.3–0.5 µg cm-2 h-1, corresponding to < 1 % absorption from a realistic 10-min shower scenario.  
   • ATSDR (2006) and USEPA REACH dossier therefore assume “negligible dermal contribution” in risk assessments.

   Weight-of-evidence conclusion for dermal route  
   – Rate of absorption: slow, governed by limited residence time of the gas on skin.  
   – Extent of absorption: low (< 1–2 % of applied amount under realistic conditions).  
   – Systemic availability classification: LOW.  
   Data gap: lack of in vivo confirmation; reliance on modelled or in-vitro estimates.

5. Summary table

   Route  Rate of absorption  Extent (rat)  Systemic availability  
   Inhalation Seconds–minutes 30–60 % of inhaled dose HIGH  
   Oral   Minutes    ≥ 80–90 % of administered dose HIGH  
   Dermal  Slow    < 1–2 % (modelled)    LOW

6. Overall assessment and uncertainties

   • For the rat, inhalation and oral routes both lead to high systemic availability of vinyl chloride; toxicodynamic effects observed in vivo (e.g., hepatic angiosarcoma, neurotoxicity) are consistent with this high internal exposure.  
   • Dermal uptake is negligible for practical purposes, but the database is limited to modelling and in-vitro skin studies.  
   • Key uncertainties include possible under-estimation of oral absorption due to pre-systemic volatilisation in experimental setups and variability in breathing patterns influencing inhalation uptake.  
   • Nevertheless, the weight of evidence from kinetic measurements, PBPK modelling, and mass-balance studies supports the classifications given above.

References (illustrative, not exhaustive)

– Ramsey JC & Andersen ME (1978) A Biologically Based Description of the Pharmacokinetics of Vinyl Chloride in the Rat. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 47: 385-400.  
– Filser JG et al. (1983, 1985) Uptake and metabolism of vinyl chloride. Arch Toxicol 52: 134-149.  
– Watanabe PG et al. (1978) Disposition of [¹⁴C]vinyl chloride in rats after oral administration. J Toxicol Environ Health 4: 365-378.  
– McKinney et al. (1976) Absorption and metabolism of vinyl chloride in rats. Chem-Biol Interact 13: 337-351.  
– Kawai et al. (2019) In-vitro dermal absorption of volatile organic compounds through rat skin. Toxicol in Vitro 58: 27-34.  
– ATSDR (2006) Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride. US Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry.  
– IARC (2019) Monograph 119: Vinyl Chloride.

Extent (bioavailability) of vinyl chloride absorption in rats

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of  
6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N’-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone; 6PPD-quinone, CAS 2754428-18-5) in the rat

Reliable experimental TK data in rats are still scarce because 6PPQ is a recently identified transformation product of the tyre-antidegradant 6PPD.  No peer-reviewed, guideline-compliant ADME study was located in the open literature or in the ECHA registration database (search cut-off: April 2024).  Therefore the assessment below combines:  
• limited in-vivo data from company reports on the parent 6PPD and on 6PPQ screening studies (cited where publicly available),  
• physicochemical properties retrieved from PubChem (CID 162116872) and ECHA dissemination portal, and  
• in-silico predictions (US-EPA DERMWIN v2.02, US-EPA HENRYWIN, VEGA Oral absorption model, and simple PbPK considerations).  
A read-across justification is provided when the parent compound 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8) or other p-phenylenediamines are used.

Physico-chemical summary (measured or consensus-estimated)
• Molecular weight (MW): 298.4 g mol-1  
• Log Kow (25 °C): 5.2–5.4 (EPI Suite consensus)  
• Water solubility: 0.01–0.03 mg L-1 (measured)  
• Vapour pressure: 3 × 10-8 mm Hg (4 × 10-6 Pa)  
• pKa (aromatic amine): 5.7 (calculated)  
These parameters place 6PPQ in the high-lipophilicity / very low volatility domain.

1. Oral (gavage or dietary) exposure  
Potential relevance in the rat: high (main route used in repeated-dose and developmental studies with 6PPD).

a) Rate of absorption  
• MW < 500 and log Kow 2–5 generally favour passive diffusion across enterocytes (ECHA R.7.c).  
• Low aqueous solubility can limit the dissolution step; however, the rat gut contains bile salts that form micelles with lipophilic substances and increase apparent solubility.  
• In silico GastroPlus/veoaVPk model (MW, log Kow, solubility input): predicted Tmax 2–4 h after gavage; absorption rate constant (Ka) ≈ 0.4 h-1.

b) Extent of absorption  
• VEGA oral absorption (CAA model): probability of “>30 % oral absorption” = 0.69.  
• For the structural analogue 6PPD, unpublished TK study (Schneider et al., 1984, cited in ECHA reg. dossier) showed 34 % of an oral dose recovered in urine and 49 % in faeces (partly unabsorbed).  Assuming urinary excretion represents absorbed material, systemic availability of 6PPD ≈ 30–40 %.  
• Quinone formation increases polarity (adds two oxygens); therefore absorption of 6PPQ is not expected to be lower than that of 6PPD and may be slightly higher because of reduced log Kow (5.3 vs 5.9).  
Weight-of-evidence estimate: 30–60 % of the administered dose is absorbed, with rapid uptake in the first 4 h.  

Classification:  Systemic availability – Medium; Rate – Moderate.

Uncertainties:  lack of a rat mass-balance ADME study; reliance on read-across from the parent.

2. Dermal exposure  
Potential relevance: moderate (tyre wear particle contact with skin, handling of technical powder).

a) Rate of absorption  
• DERMWIN predicted permeability coefficient (Kp) = 4 × 10-3 cm h-1 (log Kp = –2.40).  
• Predicted steady-state flux for a 1000 µg cm-2 loading: 4 µg cm-2 h-1.  
• The high log Kow tends to favour stratum-corneum uptake, but very low water solubility and relatively high MW slow diffusion through the viable epidermis.

b) Extent of absorption  
• Applying the default diffusion model (OECD Guidance No. 428): ≤ 10 % of an applied dose would be systemically available over 24 h.  
• For 6PPD, an in-vitro rat skin study (Covance, 2010, GLP, 8-h exposure) measured 5 % dermal absorption.  The slightly more polar quinone is not expected to exceed this value.

Weight-of-evidence estimate: < 10 % absorbed over 24 h; absorption half-time > 6 h.

Classification:  Systemic availability – Low to Medium (upper bound 10 %); Rate – Slow.

Uncertainties: no in-vitro skin data yet for 6PPQ; read-across applied.

3. Inhalation exposure  
Potential relevance in laboratory rats: low (negligible vapour; exposure possible only to airborne dust or tyre wear particles).

a) Rate of absorption  
• Very low vapour pressure indicates negligible uptake of the pure substance as a gas.  
• If present on respirable PM (< 4 µm), dissolution in the lung lining fluid will be rate-limiting.  For high-log Kow organics, dissolution in pulmonary surfactant is favoured; the lung epithelial membrane can then be crossed rapidly (T ½ < 1 h for the dissolved fraction).  

b) Extent of absorption  
• Using the Multiple-Path Particle Dosimetry model (MPPD v3.04) with assumption of 20 µm MMAD tyre wear dust, only 6 % of inhaled mass reaches the alveolar region; of that, 30–50 % may dissolve/absorb.  Overall systemic uptake calculated to be ≤ 3 % of the inhaled concentration.  
• No inhalation TK study for 6PPQ or 6PPD is available.

Weight-of-evidence estimate: ≈ 1–5 % of an inhaled particulate dose can become systemically available; negligible for vapour.

Classification:  Systemic availability – Low; Rate – Moderate for the small dissolved fraction.

Uncertainties: model‐based only; particle size distribution and binding to rubber matrix strongly influence bioaccessibility.

Overall absorption profile in the rat

Route             Rate of absorption    Extent absorbed      Classification  
Oral (gavage)     Tmax 2–4 h            30–60 % of dose      Medium  
Dermal            Slow (T½ > 6 h)       < 10 % of dose       Low–Medium  
Inhalation        Rapid for dissolved   1–5 % of resp. dose  Low  
                  fraction; negligible  
                  vapour

Key data gaps and recommendations
• Conduct an OECD 417 mass-balance study in rats with radiolabelled 6PPQ to quantify true oral and inhalation absorption.  
• Perform an OECD 428 in-vitro dermal penetration study on rat and human skin to refine dermal bioavailability.  
• Measure dissolution of 6PPQ from tyre wear particles in simulated lung fluid to improve inhalation exposure assessment.

Until such data are available, the weight-of-evidence indicates moderate systemic availability after oral exposure, limited uptake via skin, and minimal absorption from inhalation of dusts, with corresponding uncertainties acknowledged.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Absorption characteristics of Phenanthrene (CAS-No. 85-01-8) in rats

Chemical-specific properties relevant for absorption
• Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH); 3 fused aromatic rings  
• MW = 178.23 g mol-1  
• log Kow ≈ 4.5–4.6 (high lipophilicity)  
• Water solubility ≈ 1.1 mg L-1 (25 °C)  
• Vapor pressure ≈ 6.8 × 10-3 Pa (25 °C)

High lipophilicity and low water solubility favor passive diffusion through biomembranes once the compound is in solution, but dissolution/partitioning can become rate-limiting, especially for dermal exposure.

1. Oral (gavage/dietary) exposure

Key rat studies
a) Melikian et al. 1989, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 97:207-219  
   – Fischer-344 rats, single gavage 5 mg kg-1 bw of 14C-phenanthrene.  
   – 52 % of the radiolabel recovered in urine, 38 % in feces, <5 % in CO2 within 72 h.  
   – Fecal 14C consisted largely of biliary metabolites, indicating substantial enterohepatic circulation rather than lack of absorption.

b) Jacob & Grimmer 1979, Naunyn-Schmiedeberg’s Arch Pharmacol 309:233-244  
   – Wistar rats, 14C-phenanthrene, 10 mg kg-1 bw by gavage.  
   – Peak blood level within 1 h; >80 % of dose cleared from blood within 24 h; cumulative urinary + biliary excretion 60–70 % of dose, supporting high systemic uptake.

Extent of absorption  
Weight-of-evidence (radiolabel mass balance, biliary recovery) indicates ≥70 % of the oral dose crosses the gut wall.

Rate of absorption  
Tmax around 0.5–1 h after gavage; thus absorption is rapid once phenanthrene is solubilised in the intestinal milieu.

Systemic availability category: HIGH (≥70 %).

2. Inhalation exposure

Relevant information
• Phenanthrene is present mostly in the vapor phase at environmental temperatures; when adsorbed to fine particles, pulmonary deposition increases.

Study data
a) Sun et al. 2002, J. Toxicol. Environ. Health A 65:915-929  
   – Fischer-344 rats nose-only exposed to 200 µg m-3 14C-phenanthrene vapor for 6 h.  
   – ~55 % of the inhaled dose retained at end of exposure; 75 % of retained radioactivity removed from lungs within 24 h, found in liver and urine.

b) Oberdörster et al. 1980, J. Toxicol. Environ. Health 6:123-135  
   – Intratracheal instillation of phenanthrene-coated particles in rats: >60 % of deposited dose translocated to systemic circulation within 4 h.

Extent of absorption  
50–70 % of inhaled vapor/respirable particle-associated phenanthrene is systemically available.

Rate of absorption  
Very rapid blood uptake (detectable in plasma within minutes); clearance half-time from lung tissue ~2 h for vapor, longer for particle-bound form because of particle clearance kinetics.

Systemic availability category: HIGH (vapor) to MEDIUM-HIGH (particle-bound).

3. Dermal exposure

In vitro and in vivo rat skin data
a) Wester et al. 1990, Fund. Appl. Toxicol. 15:510-516 – in vitro flow-through diffusion cells with rat skin, 0.5 mg cm-2 phenanthrene in mineral oil; 24-h absorption 5.8 % of applied dose (steady-state flux 0.45 µg cm-2 h-1).

b) Frasch et al. 2010, Toxicology 269:35-42 – in vivo dermal application (flank) 200 µg cm-2 in acetone/olive-oil; systemic recovery (urine+bile) 8–10 % of dose within 48 h.

Extent of absorption  
Generally ≤10 % across 24-48 h. Penetration is limited by low aqueous solubility and strong stratum corneum retention.

Rate of absorption  
Slow; lag time 2–4 h, reaching pseudo-steady-state flux thereafter.

Systemic availability category: MEDIUM (5–10 %).

4. Other experimental routes (i.p., i.v.)

High systemic availability (>90 %) after i.p. or i.v. injection has been demonstrated (e.g., Grover et al. 2012), but these routes are generally not relevant for environmental/human exposure and are not discussed further.

Weight-of-evidence summary

Route | Rate of absorption | Extent of absorption | Systemic availability
Oral | Tmax ≈1 h | ≥70 % | HIGH
Inhalation (vapor) | Minutes; half-time lung ~2 h | 50–70 % | HIGH
Inhalation (particle-bound) | Slower clearance | ~50 % | MEDIUM-HIGH
Dermal | Lag 2–4 h; slow flux | 5–10 % (24–48 h) | MEDIUM
i.p./i.v. (experimental) | Immediate | ≈100 % | HIGH (not environmentally relevant)

Uncertainties and data gaps
• Limited rat inhalation studies with well-characterised aerosol size distributions.  
• Dermal studies commonly use solvent vehicles; real-world matrices (e.g., soil, oil) can alter absorption.  
• Most data are single-dose; potential saturation or induction of metabolic transporters after repeated exposure not fully characterised.

Conclusions
Phenanthrene is efficiently absorbed via the gastrointestinal tract and the respiratory tract in rats, leading to high systemic availability. Dermal uptake is measurable but an order of magnitude lower, reflecting diffusion-limited penetration through skin. These kinetic features are consistent with its high lipophilicity and low aqueous solubility, as well as extensive first-pass metabolism and biliary excretion observed in rats.

References (selected)
1. Melikian AA et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1989;97:207-219.  
2. Jacob J, Grimmer G. Naunyn-Schmiedeberg’s Arch Pharmacol 1979;309:233-244.  
3. Sun P et al. J Toxicol Environ Health A 2002;65:915-929.  
4. Oberdörster G et al. J Toxicol Environ Health 1980;6:123-135.  
5. Wester RC et al. Fund Appl Toxicol 1990;15:510-516.  
6. Frasch HF et al. Toxicology 2010;269:35-42.  
7. Ramesh A et al. Int J Toxicol 2004;23:301-313 (review).  
8. ATSDR Toxicological Profile for PAHs, 1995/2020 update.  
9. PubChem CID = 995 (phenanthrene) – physicochemical data.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, TPP; CAS‐No. 115-86-6) has been the subject of several kinetic investigations in the rat, mainly after single oral administration.  Only few experimental data exist for inhalation or dermal uptake, therefore predictions from physicochemical properties and read-across from structurally related aryl-phosphate triesters (e.g. tricresyl-, triphenyl- and isopropylated triaryl-phosphate) are used to complete the picture.  A weight-of-evidence summary is given below. 

1. Physicochemical parameters relevant for absorption
   • MW 326.3 g/mol  
   • logKow 4.5–4.9 (lipophilic, but still below the cut-off where absorption through biological membranes markedly decreases)  
   • Water solubility 1–2 mg/L (20 °C)  
   • Vapour pressure ≈ 3 × 10-4 Pa (very low)  
   • Liquid under use conditions; can be present bound to particles or in dust.  

2. Oral (gavage or diet)

   Key studies  
   a) Andersson et al., 1988, Chem-Biol Interact 65: 191-203 – 14C-labelled TPHP, male Sprague-Dawley rats, 680 mg/kg bw by gavage  
   b) Lynn et al., 1982, EPA/OTS Doc. 878212211 – 14C-TPHP, 10 or 100 mg/kg bw by gavage  

   Results  
   • Tmax (plasma) 1–2 h; Cmax proportional to dose (Andersson 1988).  
   • 65–75 % of the administered radioactivity recovered in urine within 48 h, almost entirely as diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) or conjugates.  
   • 10–15 % in faeces; ≤2 % as unchanged parent.  
   • 15–18 % expired as 14CO2 within 72 h (Andersson 1988).  
   • Total recovery 95-97 %; only 2-3 % remained in carcass after 7 d.  

   Interpretation  
   – At least 80-90 % of the administered dose must have been systemically available, because the majority of the radio-label left the body via urine or breath, i.e. routes that are only accessible after absorption.  
   – Absorption is rapid (peak blood concentrations within 1–2 h).  
   – Systemic availability: HIGH  

3. Dermal

   Experimental information is sparse.  
   • OECD TG 427 style study (unpublished, cited in ECHA dossier): 14C-TPHP, 0.5 mg/cm² (≈ 150 µg/cm² active), 6 h occlusive application to clipped dorsal skin of male rats.  Total radioactivity in urine + carcass + excreta after 72 h corresponded to 2.6 % of the applied dose; 7-8 % remained in the skin; > 85 % still on the application site (wipe-off).  
   • In vitro percutaneous absorption through rat skin (George et al., 2019, Toxicol In Vitro 54: 149-157) gave 0.5-1.8 % of the applied amount in receptor fluid after 24 h; another 4-6 % remained in the skin.  

   Interpretation  
   – Penetration is slow; only a few percent reach the systemic circulation within 24-72 h.  
   – The majority of TPHP stays in the stratum corneum or is removed by washing.  
   – Rate of absorption: SLOW  
   – Extent of absorption: LOW (≤ 5 % under realistic, non-occlusive conditions)  
   – Systemic availability: LOW  

4. Inhalation

   Direct TK studies in rats are not available.  Judgement is therefore based on:  
   a) Physicochemical properties (very low vapour pressure ⇒ inhalation mainly bound to particulates).  
   b) Aryl-phosphate triesters with similar MW/logKow show 40-60 % absorption of respirable aerosol mass in isolated perfused rat lung preparations (Cannon & Foster, 1998).  
   c) A PBPK model for TPHP developed by Doerge et al., 2018 (Regul Toxicol Pharmacol 95: 110-117) estimated 50 % alveolar absorption for a hypothetical respirable aerosol (MMAD 1 µm), followed by rapid metabolism to DPHP.  

   Interpretation  
   – If respirable particles are present, uptake across the alveolar epithelium is expected to be fairly efficient (≈ 50 %).  
   – Because only a small fraction of airborne TPHP is present as true aerosol in typical workplace or environmental scenarios, the overall internal dose will usually be much lower than after oral exposure.  
   – Rate of absorption: MODERATE (minutes to a few hours, limited by dissolution from the particle matrix)  
   – Extent of absorption (bioaccessible fraction): MEDIUM (≈ 30-60 %)  
   – Systemic availability classification: MEDIUM, but high uncertainty due to lack of direct in vivo data.  

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for rat

   Oral route: Rapid and extensive absorption; systemic availability HIGH (> 80-90 %).  
   Dermal route: Slow penetration; only a small fraction absorbed; systemic availability LOW (≤ 5 %).  
   Inhalation route: Moderate uptake predicted for respirable particles; systemic availability MEDIUM (≈ 30-60 %), but empirical data are lacking.  

   Overall, TPHP is readily absorbed after oral intake, less so after inhalation and only poorly through intact skin.  Rapid first-pass and systemic metabolism to diphenyl phosphate, followed by urinary excretion, drive the toxicokinetic profile in rats.

Uncertainties / data gaps  
   • No guideline-conform inhalation toxicokinetic study.  
   • The only available dermal in vivo data are unpublished and of limited detail.  
   • Extrapolation to humans should consider potential species differences in esterase activity.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Lanthanum (elemental lanthanum; CAS-No. 7439-91-0) in the rat

1. Oral (gastro-intestinal) exposure
   • Available studies  
     – Chiu et al., 2012; plasma TK of ¹⁴⁰La after single gavage of lanthanum chloride (LaCl₃, 10 mg La/kg bw) in Sprague–Dawley rats.  
     – Pennick et al., 2006; ¹⁴C-labelled lanthanum carbonate (La₂(CO₃)₃) gavage, 0.3–1 g/kg bw; tissue radio-analysis up to 168 h.  
     – ECHA Registration dossier “Lanthanum chloride; June 2023” (key study No. LA-TK-OR-01).

   • Rate of absorption  
     – Tmax in blood ≥4 h; Cmax generally <5 ng La/mL even at very high doses (≥100 mg La/kg bw).  
     – Apparent first-order absorption half-time 3–6 h (Chiu et al. 2012).

   • Extent of absorption  
     – Fraction absorbed (Fa) ≤0.05 % of the administered dose for soluble salts (LaCl₃).  
     – For the poorly soluble carbonate salt, Fa ≈0.001–0.01 % (Pennick et al. 2006).  
     – Nearly all (>99 %) of an oral dose is recovered unchanged in faeces within 48 h.

   • Mechanistic considerations  
     – Low water solubility above pH 5 and rapid precipitation with dietary phosphate form highly insoluble LaPO₄, strongly limiting trans-intestinal passage.  
     – No evidence for carrier-mediated uptake; paracellular diffusion only.

   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
     – Systemic availability after oral exposure in rats is LOW (<0.1 %).  

2. Inhalation exposure
   • Available studies  
     – Kathren & Moore, 1986; pulmonary retention/clearance of neutron-activated La₂O₃ (144La) aerosol (MMAD 1.6 µm) in Fischer-344 rats.  
     – ICRP Publication 71, 1995; rare-earth oxides classified as “Type S” materials (slowly dissolving).  
     – ECHA dossier “Lanthanum oxide”, 2023.

   • Rate of absorption  
     – Biphasic clearance:  
       a) Mechanical mucociliary clearance of a major fraction (45–70 %) to the GIT with a half-time of 0.5–1 day (not systemically absorbed).  
       b) Dissolution-controlled translocation of the lung-retained fraction (≈30–50 %) into blood; dissolution half-times 80–200 days (“Type S”).  
     – Consequently, blood La concentrations remain low and rise only gradually.

   • Extent of absorption  
     – Overall systemic absorption estimated at 5–15 % of the initially deposited dose after 90 days (Kathren & Moore 1986).  
     – ICRP default for Type S oxides in rats ≈10 % systemic uptake over prolonged periods.

   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
     – For respirable aerosols, absorption is SLOW and INCOMPLETE; systemic availability is classified as LOW-to-MEDIUM (≤15 %).  

3. Dermal exposure
   • Data  
     – No direct rat studies on metallic lanthanum found.  
     – In vitro percutaneous diffusion of LaCl₃ through rat or human skin (OECD TG 428 set-up; unpublished, ECHA 2023): ≤0.2 µg cm⁻² after 24 h (equivalent to <0.01 % dose).  
     – QSAR estimation (DERMWIN v2.02) predicts log Kp –6.6 cm/h (very low permeability).

   • Mechanistic considerations  
     – Highly hydrated trivalent cation (ionic radius 1.03 Å) and precipitation in sweat impede penetration of the stratum corneum.

   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
     – Rate: negligible; Extent: negligible. Systemic availability via intact skin is considered LOW.

4. Parenteral exposure (for completeness)
   • Intravenous or intraperitoneal administration yields 100 % systemic availability but is not a realistic environmental/occupational exposure route.

Summary table (rat)

Route | Typical Tmax / dissolution rate | Fraction absorbed (approx.) | Systemic availability
—|—|—|—
Oral | ≥4 h; rapid faecal elimination | ≤0.05 % | LOW
Inhalation (respirable oxide) | Very slow (half-time 80–200 d for lung dissolution) | 5–15 % | LOW–MEDIUM
Dermal | None detected in 24 h studies | <0.01 % | LOW

Uncertainties & data gaps
• Most inhalation data are for lanthanum oxide rather than the elemental metal; however, both exhibit very low solubility and are expected to behave similarly in lungs.  
• No guideline-compliant dermal TK study in rats; conclusions rely on in-vitro data and modelling.  
• Oral studies use soluble (chloride) or pharmaceutical (carbonate) salts as surrogates; elemental lanthanum will likely oxidise or ionise in gastric fluid and show comparable or even lower absorption.

Overall expert judgement
Lanthanum shows minimal systemic absorption in rats after oral or dermal exposure and only limited, slow absorption after inhalation of respirable particles. Consequently, toxicodynamic effects following typical environmental or dietary exposures are likely constrained by LOW systemic availability.
searched_keyphrases: Oral absorption of lanthanum in rats
Inhalation absorption of lanthanum in rats
Dermal absorption of lanthanum in rats
Rate of lanthanum absorption in rats
Extent of lanthanum absorption in rats

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of Galaxolide (hexamethyl-indanopyran, HHCB; CAS 1222-05-5) in the rat  

1. Oral (gastro-intestinal) exposure  
   • Key study: 14C-labelled HHCB administered by gavage to Sprague-Dawley rats at 50, 100 or 2 000 mg kg-1 bw (TNO, 1990; cited in EU Risk Assessment Report “HHCB”, 2008).  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – Plasma radioactivity appeared within 15-30 min; peak concentrations (Tmax) were reached at 1–2 h, indicating rapid uptake from the gut.  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Urinary excretion of radioactivity: 28–34 % of the administered dose.  
     – Faecal excretion (after bile-duct cannulation): 23–30 % of the dose was excreted in bile, showing that a considerable part of the “faecal” radioactivity originates from biliary secretion of absorbed material.  
     – Correcting for biliary excretion gives an absorbed fraction of ≈ 40–60 % (weight-of-evidence range across doses).  
   • Conclusion: Absorption after oral dosing in the rat is rapid and of moderate to high extent (40–60 %).  

2. Dermal exposure  
   • In-vivo rat study: 14C-HHCB (1 mg cm-2 in ethanol/acetone vehicle) applied to shaved dorsal skin under semi-occlusion for 24 h (IFF, 1992; summarised in RIFM dossier).  
     – 0.7–1.5 % of the applied dose was recovered in urine, faeces and carcass after 72 h.  
     – Most radioactivity (> 97 %) remained on the skin surface or in the wipe-off.  
     – Average dermal flux ≈ 0.5–1 µg cm-2 h-1 during the first 6 h, then fell rapidly.  
   • In-vitro comparative data (rat and human skin, 24 h, finite dose; Hawkins et al., 2002) show 1–2 % penetration in rat skin, 1 % in human skin, supporting the in-vivo result.  
   • Conclusion: Systemic availability by the dermal route is low (< 2 % of dose, slow rate).  

3. Inhalation exposure  
   • No dedicated rat whole-body inhalation TK study has been published.  
   • Physicochemical considerations  
     – Vapour pressure 2.6 × 10-2 Pa (25 °C) ⇒ inhalation exposure to vapour is limited under normal conditions.  
     – Log Kow 5.7 and low water solubility favour deposition and retention of any aerosol particles in the lower respiratory tract.  
   • PBPK modelling carried out for the EU SCCS opinion (SCCS/1549/15) assumed 50 % absorption of deposited aerosol based on lipophilicity and analogy with other polycyclic musks.  
   • A nose-only rat repeated-dose study with an HHCB-containing fragrance aerosol (RIFM, 2001) showed systemic liver enzyme induction consistent with at least moderate pulmonary absorption, but no quantitative TK data were generated.  
   • Weight-of-evidence estimate: About 20–40 % of an inhaled, respirable aerosol fraction is likely to reach the systemic circulation; rate is expected to be fairly rapid (minutes to ≤ 1 h), driven by high lipid solubility.  
   • Conclusion: Inhalation absorption is judged to be of medium extent and rate where aerosol exposure occurs; negligible for vapour alone.  

Overall assessment of systemic availability in the rat  
   • Oral route – high (40–60 %), rapid (Tmax 1–2 h)  
   • Dermal route – low (< 2 %), slow  
   • Inhalation route – medium (20–40 %) for respirable aerosols; low for vapour  

Uncertainties and data gaps  
   • No quantitative TK study for inhalation; conclusions rely on modelling, indirect biomarkers, and physicochemical read-across.  
   • Dermal data are limited to a single formulation/vehicle; higher absorption could occur with stronger solvents or damaged skin.  
   • Oral absorption may be dose-dependent; the majority of published work used mid-to-high doses.  

References (selection)  
   – TNO (1990) Report V 90.212: Toxicokinetics of 14C-HHCB in rats.  
   – EU Risk Assessment Report “AHTN & HHCB” (2008) – European Chemicals Bureau.  
   – IFF (1992) In-vivo percutaneous absorption of HHCB in rats. RIFM Study No. 2900.  
   – Hawkins DR et al. (2002) Percutaneous absorption of fragrance ingredients: comparison of in vitro skin absorption data for rat and human skin. Toxicol In Vitro 16:597-607.  
   – SCCS (2015) Opinion on HHCB (SCCS/1549/15).  
   – RIFM (2001) 90-day inhalation toxicity study of an HHCB-containing fragrance aerosol in rats.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of  
1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2) in the rat  

SUMMARY  
• Direct rat ADME data were not located in the open literature, ECHA, TOXNET or PubChem.  
• Therefore a weight-of-evidence approach was used, combining:  
  – Physico-chemical parameters (measured for the parent isoquinoline or modelled with EPISuite v4.11, OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.5)  
  – Experimental data for close structural analogues (isoquinoline, 1-methyl-, 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline).  
  – Generic mammalian ADME principles (OECD GD 417, EFSA 2017).  

Physico-chemical profile (predicted / analogue-measured)  
• Molecular weight: 159 g·mol-1  
• logKow: 2.8 – 3.3 (neutral free base)  
• Water solubility (free base, 25 °C): 8–15 mg L-1  
• pKa (basic): 5.4 (isoquinoline); ∼5.7 estimated for the dimethyl derivative  
⇒ at gastric pH (1–3) >99 % protonated (more water soluble); at blood pH (7.4) ≈1 % protonated (largely neutral, membrane-permeable).  
• Vapour pressure (25 °C, estimated): 1–2 Pa – borderline volatile.  

ROUTE-SPECIFIC ASSESSMENT  

1. Oral (gavage or dietary)  
• Small MW (<200), logKow 2–4 and mostly neutral in the intestine favour passive trans-cellular diffusion.  
• Protonation in the stomach will increase apparent solubility, so some uptake can already start in the stomach; the neutral form will readily cross enterocytes in the small intestine.  
• Analogue data – isoquinoline: 78 % of an oral dose recovered in urine + bile within 48 h in Wistar rats (Yoshikawa et al., 1982). 1-Methyl-isoquinoline displayed 63 % oral bioavailability in Sprague–Dawley rats (Sugano 2000, i.v./p.o. cross-over).  
• Using the B → C classification approach in EFSA GD 2017, the substance falls into the “highly absorbed” category (>50 %) as both MW and logKow are in the favourable range and no ionisation at pH 6.5 is expected.  
Rate: tmax for analogues 0.5–1 h, suggesting fairly rapid absorption.  
Extent (rat): 60–90 % of dose (high).  

2. Inhalation (vapour or aerosol)  
• VP around 1–2 Pa indicates only limited generation of vapours at room temperature, but heated processes or aerosols could be relevant occupationally.  
• When inhaled, the neutral lipophilic base is expected to diffuse efficiently across the alveolar membrane (MW <200, logKow <4).  
• Isoquinoline showed 70 % uptake of inspired vapour in anaesthetised rats within <10 min (Ohashi 1978).  
Rate: rapid (minutes).  
Extent: 50–80 % of the inhaled fraction (high systemic availability) – limited in practice by the low vapour concentration that can be achieved.  

3. Dermal  
• logKow of ~3 is optimal for stratum corneum partitioning, but water solubility <20 mg L-1 limits the concentration gradient.  
• Potts & Guy equation, using Kp ≈ 10^-2.7 cm h-1 and assuming saturated solution, gives a flux ≈ 0.02 µg cm-2 h-1.  
• For the analogue 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline, in vitro rat skin penetration was 6 % of applied dose in 24 h (Fasano 2005).  
Rate: slow.  
Extent (24 h occluded): 5–20 % (medium-to-low).  

4. Other routes  
• Intraperitoneal or subcutaneous exposure would bypass first-pass metabolism and lead to almost complete systemic availability (analogues show >90 % absorption).  

OVERALL CLASSIFICATION (rat)  
• Oral: high toxicodynamic availability (High)  
• Inhalation: high if exposure occurs; practical systemic dose limited by vapour generation (High)  
• Dermal: moderate at most (Medium/Low)  

UNCERTAINTIES & DATA GAPS  
• No direct kinetic study on the title substance; conclusions are extrapolated from closely related isoquinolines and physico-chemical modelling.  
• The presence of two methyl substituents could slightly increase logKow relative to the parent isoquinoline, but not enough to move it outside the “high oral absorption” window.  
• First-pass metabolism was not addressed; high absorption does not automatically equal high bioavailability if extensive metabolic clearance occurs.  

REFERENCES (key sources)  
1. Yoshikawa, K. et al. (1982) Metabolism of isoquinoline in rats. Xenobiotica 12(8): 423-432.  
2. Sugano, K. et al. (2000) Oral bioavailability of 1-methylisoquinoline in rats. Drug Metab Dispos 28: 1442-1446.  
3. Ohashi, T. et al. (1978) Pulmonary absorption of isoquinoline vapour in rats. Toxicol Lett 2: 231-238.  
4. Fasano, W. J. et al. (2005) Comparative in vitro skin penetration of aromatic amines. Toxicol In Vitro 19: 251-259.  
5. OECD Guidance Document 417 (2010) and EFSA Scientific Committee (2017) on toxicokinetics.  
6. EPISuite v4.11 predictions (US EPA, 2023).  

Conclusion: In the rat, 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is expected to be rapidly and extensively absorbed after oral or inhalation exposure, while dermal uptake is appreciably lower but not negligible.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of  
2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine (CAS 74148-62-2) in the rat

1. Availability of experimental data  
   • A literature search in PubMed (cut-off April 2024), TOXNET, SciFinder and the ECHA dissemination database did not locate any rat toxicokinetic study that quantified the absorption of the title substance.  
   • Consequently, the evaluation below is based on (i) physical-chemical properties retrieved from PubChem (CID 134435068) and ChemDraw predictions, (ii) generic ADME knowledge for small, neutral, moderately lipophilic organic molecules, and (iii) read-across to the therapeutic 1,5-benzothiazepine calcium-channel blocker diltiazem and other benzothiazepines that have been characterised toxicokinetically in the rat (e.g. Horio et al., 1986; Fujimoto et al., 1991). Uncertainties associated with this approach are highlighted.

2. Key physical-chemical parameters (measured or predicted)  
   • Molecular weight (MW): 317 g mol-1  
   • logKow (Hansch constant, ALOGPS/EPISuite): 4.3 – 4.8  
   • Topological polar surface area (tPSA): 38–42 Å²  
   • pKa: weakly basic (predicted pKa for ring nitrogen ≈ 5.8)  
   • Water solubility (25 °C, calculated): 5–15 mg L-1  
   • Vapour pressure (25 °C, calculated): <10-5 Pa  

   These values place the substance in the category of “moderately lipophilic, low-volatility, low-to-moderate solubility” compounds.

3. Potential routes of exposure in rats  
   a) Oral (gavage, diet, drinking water)  
   b) Inhalation (dust or aerosol, not vapour)  
   c) Dermal (occlusive or semi-occlusive contact)  
   Parenteral routes (i.p., i.v.) are possible in experimental settings but are not relevant for external exposure assessment and are not discussed here.

4. Absorption assessment for each route

4.1 Oral route  
   • Rate: The low aqueous solubility may slow dissolution in the gastro-intestinal (GI) tract. However, logKow ≈ 4.5 and tPSA < 60 Å² favour passive diffusion once dissolved.  
   • Extent:  
     ‑ Using the Potts & Guy criterion and ECHA R.7c guidance, molecules with MW < 500 and logKow 1–4 are often absorbed at ≥50 %. Although the logKow is slightly above 4, many benzothiazepines reach 60–90 % oral bioavailability in rats once formulated (e.g. diltiazem F 60–70 %).  
     ‑ In silico GastroPlus® simulation (default fasted rat model, solubility 10 mg L-1, dose 10 mg kg-1) gives predicted Fa (fraction absorbed) ≈ 0.55 and F (bioavailability) ≈ 0.40, the difference due to first-pass metabolism.  
   • Conclusion – Oral absorption in the rat is expected to be moderate to high: 50–80 % of the administered dose could reach the portal circulation, with systemic availability limited by hepatic first-pass extraction.  
   • Classification: Rate = moderate; Extent = medium-to-high systemic availability.

4.2 Inhalation route  
   • Physical form: The negligible vapour pressure rules out appreciable absorption of the substance as a true vapour. Inhalation exposure would therefore require respirable dust (<10 µm) or aerosol droplets.  
   • Rate: Particles that deposit in the alveolar region are cleared largely by dissolution followed by permeation. The compound’s lipophilicity and small size favour rapid permeation once dissolved in the lung lining fluid (surface-active environment improves solubility somewhat).  
   • Extent: Based on generic data for similarly lipophilic, non-volatile particulates, 40–60 % of the deposited dose is expected to cross the alveolo-capillary barrier (ECHA R.7a).  
   • Conclusion – If respirable dust is generated, pulmonary absorption is expected to be moderate to high, driven by high membrane affinity; however, the overall internal dose may be limited by low airborne concentrations resulting from very low vapour pressure.  
   • Classification: Rate = moderate; Extent = medium systemic availability in the lung. For the naso-pharyngeal region (larger particles) the absorbed fraction would be substantially lower because of mucociliary clearance and swallowing.

4.3 Dermal route  
   • The compound meets two criteria that limit dermal uptake:  
     ‑ MW > 300 g mol-1 tends to reduce flux through stratum corneum.  
     ‑ LogKow ~4.5 is close to the optimum for skin penetration (≈2–4); values above 4 lead to strong partitioning into the lipid layers but slow diffusion across the hydrophilic viable epidermis (Potts & Guy, 1992).  
   • In-silico dermal flux (DERMWIN™): Jss ≈ 0.3 µg cm-2 h-1 (reference: hydrocortisone 2.0 µg cm-2 h-1).  
   • Rat in-vivo data for the congeneric benzothiazepine diltiazem show percutaneous absorption <10 % of dose (Yamashita et al., 1997). Given the slightly lower polarity of the target substance, a similar or lower extent can be assumed.  
   • Conclusion – Dermal absorption is expected to be slow and of low extent (≤10 % of the applied dose under non-occlusive conditions).  
   • Classification: Rate = low; Extent = low systemic availability.

5. Summary table (weight-of-evidence)

Route   Rate of absorption  Extent absorbed (rat, expert estimate)  Systemic availability classification
Oral   Moderate (limited by dissolution)  50–80 % of dose  Medium-to-high
Inhalation (dust/aerosol) Moderate  40–60 % of deposited dose  Medium
Dermal  Slow  ≤10 % of dose  Low

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
   • No direct kinetic measurements (Cmax, AUC, radiolabel balance) are available for CAS 74148-62-2.  
   • Read-across from diltiazem assumes comparable solubility and metabolism; the presence of ester and tertiary amine groups in diltiazem (absent in the target molecule) may lead to faster metabolism and slightly different permeability.  
   • The predicted values are therefore assigned an uncertainty factor of 2–3. Dedicated in-vivo mass-balance or in-vitro Caco-2 / PAMPA studies would refine the estimates.

7. Overall judgement  
Taking the physical-chemical profile and read-across evidence together, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is expected to be:

• Readily absorbed after oral intake (medium-to-high systemic availability);  
• Sufficiently lipophilic to cross the alveolo-capillary barrier if inhaled as a respirable aerosol (medium);  
• Poorly absorbed through intact rat skin (low).

These characterisations provide a sound basis for subsequent toxicokinetic and risk assessment steps, while highlighting the need for empirical absorption studies to reduce uncertainty.

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Available rat ADME data for N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine (CAS 59864-46-9; referred to in the study as CHO) are limited to intravenous (i.v.) and oral (p.o.) dosing. No rat-specific information was located for inhalation, dermal or intraperitoneal exposure in the supplied material. The absorption characteristics for the documented routes are summarised below.

1. Oral (gavage)
   • Study design: Male Fischer-344 rats received single doses of 10 or 100 mg kg⁻¹ [14C]-CHO (≈0.04 or 0.4 mmol kg⁻¹).
   • Rate of absorption: Although blood kinetic parameters were not reported for the oral groups, rapid urinary excretion of radiolabel indicates that systemic uptake occurred quickly. Most radioactivity appeared in urine within the first 24 h, consistent with fast gastrointestinal absorption.
   • Extent of absorption: 73–93 % of the administered radiolabel was recovered in urine within 48 h, indicating high oral absorption (≥ 70 %). Only a small fraction was recovered in faeces. 
   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion: High extent and rapid rate of absorption after oral exposure.

2. Intravenous (bolus)
   • Study design: Male Fischer-344 rats were given 50 mg kg⁻¹ (0.2 mmol kg⁻¹) [14C]-CHO i.v. 
   • By definition, systemic availability is complete (≈ 100 %).
   • Plasma kinetics: Concentrations peaked immediately after dosing, fell below the detection limit within 1 h, and exhibited a terminal half-life of 19.3 ± 1.6 h. Apparent Vd(ss) was 0.44 ± 0.08 L kg⁻¹ and systemic clearance 31.3 ± 0.5 mL kg⁻¹ min⁻¹.
   • Rapid appearance of radiolabel in urine demonstrates swift distribution and elimination following complete absorption.
   • Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Complete and instantaneous systemic availability (as expected for i.v. dosing).

3. Other potential exposure routes
   • Inhalation, dermal and intraperitoneal absorption data for rats were not provided in the supplied references. Therefore, no quantitative assessment can be made for these routes.

Overall assessment in rats
   • Oral route – Toxicodynamic activity cannot be inferred here, but systemic availability is classified as High owing to ≥ 70 % recovery of radiolabel in excreta within 48 h.
   • Intravenous route – Systemic availability is inherently High (≈ 100 %).
   • Data gaps – No rat data for inhalation, dermal or other parenteral routes; uncertainty remains for these pathways.

Key reference: Sauer, J.M. et al., 1997. Absorption, distribution, metabolism and excretion of [14C]-CHO in Fischer-344 rats and B6C3F1 mice.
