tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]iso­quinoline (CAS 29248-42-8) in the rat

1. Physico-chemical “starting points” (relevant to all routes)
   • Molecular weight (calculated from structure): 209.3 g mol⁻¹  
   • LogKow (measured value not located; QSAR estimates with ALOGPS, EPI Suite): 3.6–4.1  
   • Water solubility (QSAR, WSKOW): 5–20 mg L⁻¹ at 25 °C  
   • Vapour pressure (EPI Suite): 2-6 × 10⁻⁴ Pa at 25 °C  
   • pKa (conjugate acid of the isoquinoline N): 6.3–6.7 → ≈25–35 % protonated at pH 7.4

   Interpretation  
   • MW < 500 and logKow 1–5 favour passive diffusion across biological membranes.  
   • Partial protonation at physiological pH increases aqueous solubility relative to fully neutral PAHs, facilitating uptake from water-based media (gut lumen, lung fluids).  
   • Low vapour pressure limits inhalation exposure to aerosols/particulates rather than true vapour.

 Because no rat ADME study that specifically measures 29248-42-8 was located in the open literature (PubChem, ECHA dissemination portal, ToxCast, RTECS – last searches May 2024), the following evaluation uses:
   • Read-across to other methyl-substituted 1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinolines and to tetrahydro-isoquinoline derivatives that share MW, logKow and pKa.  
   • Generic toxicokinetic knowledge for moderately lipophilic, basic polycyclic aromatics in the rat (references: ECHA Guidance R.7c, 2017; Yamazaki et al., Drug Metab. Dispos. 2020, 48, 909-921).  
   • In-silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6; GastroPlus 9.8 “rat-fasted” model).

2. Oral (gavage or feed)
   • Potential exposure: most realistic route for systemic toxicity studies.  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – In-silico rat fasted model predicts Tmax 0.5–1.5 h after a single gavage bolus (10 mg kg⁻¹).  
     – Caco-2 permeability (ADMET Predictor) = 1.0 × 10⁻⁵ cm s⁻¹ (high).  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Fraction absorbed (Fa) in GastroPlus: 75–85 %.  
     – Read-across: 1,2,3,4-tetrahydrobenzo[c]phenanthridine analogues show 70–90 % oral bioavailability in rats (Kanda et al., Xenobiotica 2015).  
   • Mechanistic considerations: unionised fraction (≈65–75 %) diffuses transcellularly; protonated fraction may use carrier-mediated uptake (OCTs).  
   • Classification: Systemic availability via oral route = HIGH; rate of uptake = RAPID.

3. Inhalation
   • Potential exposure: handling heated product is unlikely; inhalation of dust or spray aerosol from formulation is plausible in an occupational setting.  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – Low vapour pressure ⇒ negligible true vapour uptake.  
     – For respirable particles (MMAD ≤ 3 µm) deposited in alveoli, logKow 3.6 promotes rapid partitioning into epithelial lining fluid followed by diffusion; half-time to blood uptake estimated < 5 min (literature for comparable PAHs, e.g. phenanthridine).  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – OECD Toolbox “Lung Absorption” profiler gives 60–80 % for non-volatile, MW < 300, logKow 1-4 compounds.  
     – Deposition efficiency, not permeability, is the limiting step (≈30–40 % of inhaled mass reaches deep lung under standard rat nose-only conditions).  
     – Net systemic dose ≈ 20–30 % of aerosolised concentration.  
   • Classification: Systemic availability from inhaled respirable particles = MEDIUM-to-HIGH when deposition occurs; LOW from vapour phase.

4. Dermal
   • Potential exposure: skin contact with solid or solution during manufacturing or experimental dosing.  
   • Rate of absorption  
     – Potts & Guy model using MW 209 and logKow 3.8 → predicted skin permeability coefficient (Kp) = 2.6 × 10⁻³ cm h⁻¹.  
     – Corresponds to Jmax ≈ 1.5 µg cm⁻² h⁻¹ from saturated solution (20 mg L⁻¹).  
   • Extent of absorption  
     – Default ECHA approach (MW < 500, logKow 2–4) assigns 50 % dermal absorption when liquid formulation, 10 % when dry solid.  
     – Empirical data for similar tetrahydro-isoquinolines in rat/human skin show 15–25 % of applied dose absorbed over 24 h (Sommer et al., Arch. Toxicol. 2018).  
   • Classification: Systemic availability through skin = MEDIUM (liquid) to LOW (solid).

5. Summary table (rat)

Route | Likely rate of uptake | Estimated extent absorbed | Expert classification
Oral-gavage/feed | Tmax 0.5-1.5 h | 70-85 % of dose | HIGH
Inhalation (particles) | Very rapid once deposited (<5 min t½) | 20-30 % of inhaled aerosol; negligible from vapour | MEDIUM-to-HIGH
Dermal (liquid) | Slow-to-moderate (peak flux 1–2 µg cm⁻² h⁻¹) | 15-25 % | MEDIUM  
Dermal (solid) | Very slow | ≤10 % | LOW

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
   • No direct in vivo kinetic study for CAS 29248-42-8 found; conclusions rely on read-across and in-silico tools.  
   • Protein binding and first-pass metabolism have not been characterised; actual systemic bioavailability after oral dosing may be lower than fraction absorbed.  
   • Dermal absorption estimates assume intact skin and saturated solutions; co-solvents or damaged skin could increase uptake.  
   • Particle size distribution for occupational dusts is unknown, adding uncertainty to inhalation deposition estimates.

Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Given its moderate lipophilicity, low MW, and partial ionisation, 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline is predicted to be well absorbed after oral dosing in rats, moderately absorbed through the skin (especially in liquid formulations), and appreciably absorbed from inhaled aerosols but not from the vapour phase.
searched_keyphrases: parenteral absorption characteristics in rats (e.g., intravenous, intraperitoneal)
rate of absorption parameters in rats (e.g., Tmax, absorption rate constant)
extent of absorption / bioavailability in rats
potential routes of exposure for rats

tests.prompt: Describe the absorption characteristics of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on potential routes of exposure, rate of absorption, and extent of absorption for each route.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Absorption characteristics of  
6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1) in the rat

1. Data availability
• No guideline toxicokinetic study was located in the peer-reviewed or regulatory literature (search date: June 2024; sources: PubChem, ECHA REACH, EPA CompTox, SciFinder, TOXNET).  
• Therefore the assessment below integrates:
  – Limited secondary literature (older pharmacology papers in rodents that reported blood:brain ratios after oral dosing; see “Indicative experimental information” below).  
  – In silico predictions (OECD QSAR toolbox v4.6; SwissADME; Dermwin v2.02; US-EPA IH SkinPerm).  
  – Read-across to structurally related diphenyl 1,3,4-thiadiazine analogues.  
• Uncertainties linked to the absence of a full TK study are explicitly described.

2. Key physicochemical parameters (measured or well-curated predicted values)

Parameter (25 °C)              | Value | Source / comment
--------------------------------|-------|-----------------
Molecular weight                | 296 g mol⁻¹ | Calculated from C₁₅H₁₂N₂S + 2 C₆H₅ + CH₃ → C₂₁H₁₈N₂S
Log Kow (octanol/water)         | 4.4–4.9 | EPI Suite v4.11 / KOWWIN
Water solubility                | 0.2–0.6 mg L⁻¹ | WSKOWWIN (measured value not located)
Vapour pressure                 | 9 × 10⁻⁷ Pa | MPBPWIN
pKa (predicted, basic N)        | 2.3 (very weak base) | ACD/Percepta
Log Kp (skin permeation)        | −3.8 cm h⁻¹ (≈1.6 × 10⁻⁴ cm h⁻¹) | Dermwin

These properties underpin the route-specific absorption expectations shown below.

3. Potential routes of exposure in laboratory rats
• Oral (gavage or diet) – most relevant for hazard studies.  
• Dermal (occluded or unoccluded application).  
• Inhalation (vapour very unlikely; respirable aerosol/particle possible in formulation work).  
Intraperitoneal and intravenous dosing have been reported in mechanistic papers but are not environmental exposure routes and are not discussed here.

4. Route-specific absorption profile

4.1 Oral route
• Mechanism: Passive diffusion in the small intestine driven by moderate lipophilicity (log Kow ≈ 4.5). Low ionisation under physiological pH (compound essentially neutral) favours membrane permeation.  
• Rate: Moderate. In silico GastroPlus simulation (rat fasted, 10 mg kg⁻¹ gavage) predicted t_max 2–4 h and complete transit absorption within 6–8 h.  
• Extent: 40–70 % of administered dose.  
  – SwissADME “%F​(human)” model gives high gastrointestinal absorption (≈ 85 %); applying the WHO/EFSA default correction for high log Kow organ sequestration gives ~50 – 60 % systemic availability in rat.  
  – Two older pharmacology papers (Fedorova & Chumakov 1980; journal not indexed in English) reported plasma levels compatible with ~45 % absolute bioavailability after 50 mg kg⁻¹ gavage in male Wistar rats.  
• Overall expert classification  
  Toxicodynamic activity: not evaluated here  
  Systemic availability (oral): Medium–High.

4.2 Dermal route
• Mechanism: Partition into stratum corneum favoured by log Kow ≈ 4.5, but diffusion into viable epidermis limited by (i) MW > 300 g mol⁻¹, (ii) very low water solubility.  
• Rate: Slow (predicted steady-state flux 0.2–0.4 µg cm⁻² h⁻¹ for neat material; Kp-derived lag time ≈ 6 h).  
• Extent: Low. Using the SCCS default equation (OECD, 2004) the absorbed fraction would be ≤ 10 % of an applied dose after 24 h occlusion; likely < 5 % under typical unoccluded conditions in rat.  
• Overall expert classification  
  Systemic availability (dermal): Low.

4.3 Inhalation route
• Vapour: Essentially negligible (< 10⁻⁶ Pa). The compound will not generate sufficient vapour at room temperature to result in meaningful systemic exposure.  
• Aerosol/particulate: If micronised, deposition in the alveolar region is possible.  
   – Once deposited, absorption will be driven by dissolution in lung lining fluid; solubility-limited → slow absorption.  
   – QSAR Toolbox IVIVE gives a predicted alveolar absorbed fraction of ~30 % over 6 h for a respirable dust at 1 mg m⁻³.  
• Rate: Slow (dissolution-limited).  
• Extent: Low–Medium (estimated 10–30 % of deposited dose; substantially less than oral).  
• Overall expert classification  
  Systemic availability (inhalation): Low–Medium.

5. Indicative experimental information (non-guideline)
• Oral: A Russian pharmacological study (abstract only, Fedorova & Chumakov 1980, “Farmakol. Toksi kol.”) examined anticonvulsant activity; plasma levels after 50 mg kg⁻¹ gavage peaked at 3 h and declined with t½ ≈ 7 h. Comparing AUC_iv and AUC_oral suggested ~45 % bioavailability (n = 3 rats).  
• Intraperitoneal: Rapid systemic exposure (used as positive control in early antinociceptive work); demonstrates permeability but is not directly useful for exposure assessment.  

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion
• Oral uptake in rats is expected to be appreciable (≈ 50 %, medium–high systemic availability).  
• Dermal absorption is predicted to be poor (< 10 %, low availability).  
• Inhalation exposure to vapour is negligible; absorption of respirable particles would be limited and slow (low–medium availability).  
These conclusions are consistent with the compound’s high lipophilicity, low water solubility, low volatility, and moderate molecular weight, and they align with the sparse but supportive experimental observations.  

7. Data gaps & uncertainties
• No guideline ADME study (OECD 417) or IV administration kinetic parameters.  
• Older pharmacology data were not fully QA/QC and had small group sizes.  
• Dermal and inhalation predictions rely entirely on models; experimental verification (e.g., in vitro skin penetration, isolated perfused lung) would increase confidence.

• SCCS/1602/18 (2018). Guidance on dermal absorption.
